docs.title: —Conformance
docs.text:
The conformance phase of the project integrates the individual calibrated models into a full watershed model and assures acceptable results can be achieved. The watershed model is first validated for performance against historic events, and then a single long-term realization of storm events is computed. Conformance metrics, which compare simulated frequency curves at key locations to observed frequency curves, are evaluated. Acceptable conformance metrics must be achieved prior to completing the phase and proceeding to production level computations.

Then during the conformance phase, the individual models will be promoted to the conformance directory, adjusted to represent synthetic events, fully linked, tested for successful execution in the cloud, and checked against desktop results for fidelity. After fidelity is verified, a long-term realization of events will be executed in the cloud and the suite of models will be adjusted as necessary to meet conformance metrics as defined in the SOP. After conformance metrics are met, reviewed, and approved, the full model suite is considered finalized, and the conformance phase is closed out. After the conformance phase is completed, the production phase begins, in which the model suite is utilized for execution of the full production level simulation, including multiple long-term realizations.\

Figure 12.1. Context of the Conformance Phase in the Future of Flood Risk Data Workflow

docs.title: Staffing for Conformance Phase
docs.text: The conformance phase is led by the model integration lead and executed by a relatively smaller team that utilizes the final calibrated models delivered during the calibration phase. The team integrates the models and makes watershed-wide adjustments as necessary to meet the QC and conformance metrics described in the sections below. Significant support from the cloud environment lead is also required build the compute manifests and to execute models in the cloud through several iterations. Any significant changes to the configuration of the model suite must be coordinated with the appropriate team members involved in the calibration and development of the models (e.g. breach location changes, dam operations changes, etc.).

docs.title: Selection of Time Window Duration for Stochastic Events
docs.text: The time window for stochastic simulations will be selected early in the conformance phase. The time window selected will be of adequate duration, such that the storm event occurs, and peak water surfaces are fully routed through the entire basin prior to the end of simulation.

docs.title: Conformance Workflow Overview
docs.text:
The conformance phase of the project includes several logical major steps to achieve success as briefly described below.

  1. Cloud Compute Setup: Setup of cloud compute to accept the fully integrated system of models for computation of stochastic events. Includes pre-processing random seeds, synthetic years, and fishnets for storm placements.Model Integration: Full model linking following the previously scoped model linking plan and naming conventions in desktop environment.

  2. Historic Event Replication: QC step to ensure that the fully linked system of models still reproduces historic event results, which can be completed in desktop environment.

  3. Stress Testing: Desktop execution of model runs that push the limits of model stability and accuracy. Events selected include extreme combinations of storm events and breaches of dams and levees.

  4. Model Delivery for Cloud Compute: The full model suite is tailored for cloud compute and delivered to the appropriate location in the cloud directory to allow cloud computations.

  5. Pre-conformance Review: Review that thoroughly checks the models delivered to ensure all standards are met, documented with review checklist.

  6. Cloud-compute Manifests: Development of compute manifests that contain instructions for each cloud-compute plugin to perform the computations.

  7. Initial Cloud Computation: Cloud computation of 3 to 5 synthetic events.

  8. Fidelity Check Cloud versus Desktop: Direct comparison of desktop results to cloud results for the same storm events to ensure fidelity.

  9. Single Realization Computation: Cloud computation of a single realization of stochastic storm events.

  10. Event Filtering for Hydraulic Model: Selection of a subset of events for computation through the hydraulic model that are relevant for characterizing flood risk. This recognizes that the cost of computing every stochastic event through the hydraulic model is not justified.

  11. Hazard Maps and Consequences Computation: Cloud computation of event-based depth grids, summarized hazard maps, and consequences.

  12. Conformance Metric Evaluation: The results from the single realization will be evaluated against the established conformance metrics.

Figure 12.2. Conformance Phase Workflow

\

docs.title: Conformance Task Descriptions
docs.text: The following subsections provide additional descriptions of the tasks shown in the workflow overview above.

docs.title: Cloud Compute Setup
docs.text: There are several important cloud setup activities that can being immediately at the start of the conformance phase and can occur concurrently while models are being integrated, tested, and prepared for Model Delivery to the cloud.

docs.title: Generate Event Seeds
docs.text: The generated seeds provide the basis for stochastic sampling for the project.

docs.title: Storm Sampling and Placements via Fishnets
docs.text: The storm events including placements are sampled for the conformance realization.

docs.title: Breach Trigger Sampling
docs.text: The breach triggers are sampled for each event within the conformance realization. See Section 9 for criteria for the breach sampling scheme.

docs.title: Model Integration
docs.text:
Model integration is the responsibility of the model integration lead and is performed in a desktop environment. This integration includes the hydrologic model, reservoir operations model and all hydraulic models for the project. The model linking register and detailed naming convention tables are created during the scoping and data preparation phase and will guide all model integration efforts. Communication of the linking register table can be aided by graphical representations of how output data is used as input for subsequent models as shown in [Figure 12.3 below as an example.](#_Ref207117602)

[During model integration, all models undergo several changes so they can accept synthetic events rather than observed events. This includes various boundary conditions and naming adjustments that are detailed in Job Aid 13, Model Delivery for Cloud Compute.](#_Ref207117602)

[Desktop model integration allows for the team members to thoroughly review and verify the configuration and performance of the full watershed model for individual events with easily identifiable and traceable boundary conditions in a traditional computing environment.\ ](#_Ref207117602)

[Figure 12.3. Conceptual Model Linking Diagram](#_Ref207117602)

[](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Historic Event Replication](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [During the previous calibration phase the individual models were calibrated to match observations from historic storm events. Within the desktop environment, the fully integrated models must be checked against those same historic events to ensure acceptable results are achieved.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Stress Testing](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [The system of models will be subjected to a wide variety of boundary conditions ranging from a dry basin with no rainfall to extreme floods on wet antecedent conditions during the stochastic model runs. Dams and levees will also be subjected to breaches. To minimize model instabilities during the production runs, the models are stress tested by the project’s model integration lead in the desktop environment. This effort involves running extreme or unusual storm conditions through the model to check for any model instability or other issues. For instance, the largest storm in the meteorologic region (from the storm catalog described in Section 5.4) could be centered over a critical location in the basin. All levees and dams could be allowed to breach during the same event. The initial model states may also be adjusted to extreme conditions to pair with the precipitation. Extreme low flows and dry conditions are considered as well as extreme high flows.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Model Delivery for Cloud Compute](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[After the models have been integrated, verified, and stress tested, they are nearly ready for delivery for cloud compute. However, there are several steps that must be taken, which are fully detailed in Job Aid 13, Model Delivery for Cloud Compute. After models have been posted to the correct locations on the conformance folder along with the final version of the Model Linking Register, the model integration lead is responsible for ensuring the data is accurate and ready for cloud compute via the pre-conformance review (next step).](#_Ref207117602)

[After the integrated models are initially delivered to the cloud, changes will be needed to address errors or to iterate to achieve the desired model results. The archive workflow, as described in Job Aid 13, Model Library Use for FFRD, is designed to minimize mistakes in model versioning. Before any model is updated, the previous version of the model is archived in Model Library through the rename functionality. The model should be renamed with /archive/ prepended to the model’s name to support the model archive. After the previous model is archived, the new model is updated where the old model previously was stored. This workflow ensures the most current models exist in a static location that does not change, while also maintaining a record of the superseded models.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Pre-Conformance Review](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[After the models have been integrated in the desktop environment, verified against historic events, and stress tested, they are nearly ready to be delivered to the cloud storage for cloud computations of synthetic events.](#_Ref207117602)

[Prior to hand off to the cloud compute team member, a full review of the models to be delivered will be completed and the models will undergo a QC check documented by in the Pre-Conformance Review Checklist. Cloud compute steps do not begin until the pre-conformance review has been fully closed and approved by the model integration lead.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Cloud-compute Manifests](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [After all models have been reviewed and delivered to the cloud, the cloud-compute manifests are created as described in Job Aid 12, Cloud Compute Manifests. These files provide the instructions for cloud-compute plug-ins to perform compute jobs. The Model Linking Register and the data in the actual delivered models are utilized as inputs to create the compute-manifests. Strict adherence to naming conventions and scripting to automate certain steps in the production of the compute manifests has the potential to improve efficiency and ability to QC linking data.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Initial Cloud Computation](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [Three to five events will be computed as a starting point before running any large-scale, cloud-compute simulations for the project. The cloud-compute lead builds the required compute-manifest to execute the hydrologic, reservoir operations, and hydraulic models. The cloud-compute lead will verify that inputs and results from the events are being properly varied and then pass the model to the model integration lead for additional review checks.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Fidelity Check Cloud versus Desktop](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [The model integration lead will work with the cloud compute team member to perform checks to ensure that cloud compute is producing identical results to a desktop computation with identical boundary conditions and inputs for each event. Any identified issues with the model linkages, desktop models, or cloud compute will be addressed. The cloud results will be evaluated for model linking, antecedent conditions, storm placements, and breach triggers among other items. Iterations will occur until discrepancies are fully resolved. The conformance review checklist includes items to verify that this step was completed.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Single Realization Computation](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[A single realization will be executed through cloud compute, which allows model performance over a long-term simulation to be evaluated against observed data. Frequency curves must be compared across the range of observed data with one realization of 2,000 years having a stable mean to the 100-year event.](#_Ref207117602)

[To inform the event filtering described in the next section, each event in the realization is first computed through the HEC-ResSim model. The filtered events to run through the hydraulic model are then selected based on the results from the earlier models.](#_Ref207117602)

[Each selected event will be executed through the hydraulic model step (i.e., depth grids, hazards grids and consequences are not evaluated initially) and summary results are output to allow for analysis of model performance. Performance is then evaluated as described in the next step, and models are updated and recomputed until conformance metrics are met.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Event Filtering for Hydraulic Model](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[It is recognized that not every synthetic event contributes to the overall characterization of the flood risk for the project since some of the sampled events produce only minor runoff compared to all other events in the synthetic year. The fully 2D hydraulic models for the project are relatively computationally expensive and costs associated with running every event in cloud compute are not justified.](#_Ref207117602)

[Leveraging the results from computation of all events through hydrology, reservoir operations, and breach sampling, a list of specific events for computation through the hydraulic models is developed. Tabulated summaries of the event-by-event maximums of many variables in many locations for each event are generated.](#_Ref207117602)

[The cloud compute team member and model integration lead will work together to create a filtered list of sampled events that will be executed through cloud compute based on their importance to flood risk characterization for the project following the process described in Job Aid 19, Selection of Events for Hydraulic Modeling.](#_Ref207117602)

[Results from a full realization through the hydrologic and reservoir operations models is needed to inform the filtering, therefore there is a feedback loop with the Single Realization Computation step described in the previous section.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Hazard Maps and Consequence Computation](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [Cloud computation of event-based depth grids, summarized hazard maps, and consequences are computed based on the results from the hydraulic model. It is recognized that there is a relatively high computation cost associated with generation depth grids for each event and summarized hazards grids. Therefore, the conformance metrics described in the section below that relate model results through the hydraulic model will be evaluated to ensure conformance prior to the computation of hazard maps and consequences.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Consequences](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[Structure inventories are updated based on the results of the previously completed survey and quality checks. Event-based consequence estimates are computed using FEMA damage function curves approved for each occupancy type and foundation type combination. Expected annual damages and frequency of when structures are first inundated are Produced. Refer to the Job Aid 17, Consequence Processes, for details on the workflow.](#_Ref207117602)

[Consequence team members should review outlier results for reasonableness, mapping results for coverage, and ensure that consequences appear to be working as intended.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Conformance Metrics Evaluation](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[The model results of the single realization allow for a robust evaluation of model performance over a long-term simulation against observed data. Fundamentally, the FFRD modeled results must reasonably align with observed long-term data and a thorough evaluation of the single realization results during the conformance phase provides credibility that the modeling system can provide flood risk estimates for the watershed.](#_Ref207117602)

[[Table 12.1](#_Ref207276912) lists the relationships that must be evaluated for an FFRD study during the conformance phase. Evaluations must be performed via a graphical comparison to observed data and via computation of objective statistical metrics. Additional guidance on metrics to be evaluated and processes for evaluation are provided in Job Aid 12, Model Calibration.](#_Ref207117602)

[Additional specifics regarding objective pass/fail criteria for acceptable frequency-based model results will be considered for a future update to this SOP.](#_Ref207117602)

[Table 12.1. List of Relationships of Long-term Modeled Data versus Observed Data for Conformance Evaluation](#_Ref207117602)

[Relationship](#_Ref207117602) [Storm Types](#_Ref207117602) [Storm Dates/Seasonality](#_Ref207117602) [Storm Locations](#_Ref207117602) [Precipitation Frequency](#_Ref207117602) [Liquid Water at Soil Surface Frequency](#_Ref207117602) [Flow Frequency](#_Ref207117602) [Elevation Discharge Relationship](#_Ref207117602) [Elevation Frequency](#_Ref207117602) [Dam and Levee Breaching Frequency](#_Ref207117602) [Flood Extents versus Historical Event Information](#_Ref207117602) [Consequence Frequency versus Claims Data](#_Ref207117602)

[In addition to ensuring the model results match observed data, model stability is also a key criterion for model conformance. A broad review of overall results from the system of models must be completed. In particular, hydraulic model volume errors must be less than 5 percent but greater than 3 percent for all events.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Iterations to Achieve Conformance](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[The models are expected to undergo multiple iterations to achieve conformance to long-term observed data. Job Aid 12, Model Calibration, includes guidance and best practices for updates to a system of models to achieve the targets.](#_Ref207117602)

[Deliberate and careful model data management is required for successful completion of the project and improves the ability to communicate model updates with the project team. The current version of each model must be maintained as a static directory, which requires an archival of the previous version prior to upload of the update. This archival process and the standard directory names are described in Job Aid 07, Model Library Use.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Computational Resources](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [During the earliest cloud-compute efforts, the compute team monitors and records the demand for central processing unit (CPU), random access memory (RAM), disk space, and runtime for computing an event through the full system of models. These resource demand estimates inform the cloud computing resources needed for the full watershed multi-realization compute.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Optimization of Computes](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [There are several variables that can be adjusted to optimize cloud computations. Shorter compute times for individual events can be achieved by using more powerful cloud resources, but experiences to date have shown that compute costs are optimized when less powerful cloud resources are utilized. Additionally, certain model settings related to the use of compute resources also impact cost and runtime, which must be optimized prior to initiation of large-scale cloud computes for FFRD. The primary objective of optimization is to achieve the lowest compute cost. Additional detail and considerations for optimization are included in cloud-compute documentation.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Compute Costs](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [After optimization of the computations, an estimate of resources and cost to compute the full set of events for the study is made.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Conformance Phase Reporting](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[A set of standard reports is written for each phase in the FFRD project. Appendix B contains report templates for the hydraulic modeling reports.](#_Ref207117602)

[The conformance phase report describes model integration, calibration verification, and conformance outcomes. The report also includes a full description of updates required in the hydrologic, reservoir operations, system response, or hydraulic models that were made to achieve conformance targets. Reports are filed in the documentation folder within the watershed repository in the Model Library.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Conformance Phase Review Process and Deliverables](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [Review of the conformance phase deliveries is critical for project success. The review steps during the conformance phase are defined below. Review checklists are used to track comments regarding pre-defined aspects checked throughout the review process. Appendix A contains checklist templates.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Pre-Conformance Review](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [The pre-conformance review occurs after the system of models has been integrated in the desktop environment, tested and delivered for cloud compute. The pre-conformance review ensures that the system of models is fully ready for the cloud compute process. A review checklist template with standard prompts guides the review and documents completion of the review. This pre-conformance review checklist must be closed out prior to hand-off of the model to the cloud compute team member.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Conformance Review](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[The conformance review covers all aspects of review of cloud-compute results and the conformance phase report will be documented via the conformance review checklist template. This checklist includes items related to review of the 3 to 5 initial cloud compute events as well as the review of results from the full realization.](#_Ref207117602)

[Throughout the conformance phase, iterations to the models may be necessary to achieve acceptable results.](#_Ref207117602)

[After the initial cloud computations are complete the team will verify the fidelity of results and iterate as necessary to ensure the desktop models and cloud compute are producing intended results.](#_Ref207117602)

[After the computation of a single realization in cloud compute, the results will be thoroughly reviewed and assessed for conformance and again iterated as necessary to ensure conformance targets are met.](#_Ref207117602)

[The final delivery for the conformance phase close out includes:](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Conformance phase report](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Final models posted to /conformance/[model]/ directories](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Conformance phase event-level model results posted to conformance/simulations directory](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Conformance phase summary model results posted to conformance/simulations.](#_Ref207117602)

[—Production of Stochastic\ Simulations](#_Ref207117602) ===========================================================================

[The production phase of the project begins after demonstration that models achieve the standards set in the conformance phase of the project and the conformance phase review has been closed out. The number of runs transitions from a single realization to a full multi-realization simulation. The dominant characteristic of the production phase is the computation of a very large number of events, which requires significant cloud computing resources to achieve.](#_Ref207117602)

[Figure 13.1. Context of the Production Phase of the Future of Flood Risk Data Workflow](#_Ref207117602)

[](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Promotion of Models to Production Directory](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[All activities for the project leading up to the production phase ensure that the system of models meet quality standards and can confidently be executed for a very large number of events. The models will not experience significant changes or updates during the production phase.](#_Ref207117602)

[At the start of the production phase, the final set of models from the conformance phase are copied to the production directory and will be used directly in the production computations.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Cloud Compute Setup](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [Event seeds, storm selection, storm placement, and breach trigger elevations are all produced for each event for the full stochastic simulation early in the production phase. This is done in a similar manner as described in Section [12.4.1](#_Ref215572395).](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Strategies to Ensure Efficient Use of Cloud Compute Resources](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[Early in the production phase, the models have already been fully prepared for execution of all events in the stochastic simulation using cloud-compute tools. Before submitting the full set of compute jobs for all events for all models, small batches of runs should be made and evaluated to ensure accurate cloud configurations are in place. The following batches of runs are recommended for execution and evaluation in a step-wise fashion to confirm configurations:](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Execute full model suite for 3 to 5 events and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Execute hydrologic model for entire simulation and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Execute reservoir operation model for entire simulation and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Execute hydraulic models for all selected events and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Execute hazard grid generation for all selected events and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Compute consequences and evaluate results](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Event Filtering for Hydraulic Model](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[The simulation structure for FFRD includes modeling of multiple storm events per year. The goal is to produce annual probabilities of hazard and risk and not every event contributes to the annual maximum of hazards. Given the computational burden of executing a large number of events through the hydraulic models, it is worthwhile to select the subset of events that have the potential to contribute to annual maximum hazards.](#_Ref207117602)

[No single event necessarily drives the annual maximum hazards everywhere for a given variable of interest. Within each synthetic year, the maximum hazards for unique locations could result from a locally intense precipitation events, upstream runoff, breaches, or other complex interactions.](#_Ref207117602)

[Events will be filtered to execute only a portion of the events through the hydraulic model. Additional description and process for filtering the events is included in Section [12.4.11](#_Ref215572516) and Job Aid 19, Selection of Events for Hydraulic Modeling.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Hot Fixes](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[Running large scale quantities of numerical models within the stochastic simulation can yield conditions that cause the model to fail. Very low failure rates are expected due to the rigorous QC and QA steps that take place prior to the start of the production phase. However, since production simulations can produce extreme events or unique combinations of conditions, model failures may occur.](#_Ref207117602)

[When model failure occurs, the team will investigate the cause of failure. Typically, the failure can be addressed with a hot fix of the models. A hot fix is an alternate version of the input model(s) that addresses a specific accuracy or stability issue that arises in one or more events during the production phase. Hot fix model versions are applied only to specific events where they were deemed necessary.](#_Ref207117602)

[The standard directory structure for the conformance phase provides for storage of the hot fix model versions along with metadata files that document the specific events there were utilized for.](#_Ref207117602)

[The general workflow for implementing hot fixes is shown below.](#_Ref207117602)

  1. [Model integration lead investigates cause of model failure (see Job Aid 15, Verification of Cloud Compute Event Results, for process to acquire date for investigation).Model integration lead should take appropriate actions to correct the model.](#_Ref207117602)

  2. [Model integration lead should upload the new model files into Model Library in a hot fix location consistent with the required standard directory structure (see Job Aid 07, Model Library Use) and notify the cloud compute team. Also refer to Job Aid 13, Model Delivery for Cloud Compute, to ensure models are uploaded in the appropriate format for cloud compute.](#_Ref207117602)

  3. [Cloud compute team member reruns the failed events and works with model integration lead to verify the hot fix resolved the issue.](#_Ref207117602)

  4. [Cloud compute team member identifies which events the hot fix resolved and uploads a list of those event numbers to the hot fix model folder.](#_Ref207117602)

[When hotfixes are applied to the project, the overall results from the full simulation are sourced from slightly different input models; but represent a complete and cohesive quantification of hazards and risk for the basin.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Investigation of Outliers](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [In addition to certain events that may lead to a model failure due to model stability, the team must evaluate the results for possible outlier results that may indicate stability or accuracy issues. Outliers must be reviewed to determine if the results are valid or if a hot fix may be required.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Significant Model Updates](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [If the team determines the required fixes for certain failed events or outlier events would significantly impact the results for the entire basin, it may suggest the need for a more comprehensive update to the inputs models for all events. The review process leading up to the start of the production phase ensures major model changes during the production phase will be extremely rare. Any decisions related to major model changes during production must be elevated to program leadership.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Production Results Comparison to Observed Data](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [Results of the full stochastic simulation will be evaluated compared to observed data. This evaluation is similar to the evaluation done during the conformance phase (Section [12.4.13](#_Ref215572530)), although multiple realizations will be available to provide insight into the quantification of knowledge uncertainty and there is no expectation to adjust the models to improve the results.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Production Phase Reporting](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text: [A set of standard reports is written for each phase in the FFRD project. Appendix B contains the report template for the production phase report. This report will contain an overview of the tasks executed during the production phase, a summary of the results, and documentation of any model hot fixes or patches that occurred during the production phase.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [Stochastic Simulations Review Process and Deliverables](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[A review the full production level results including precipitation, hydrology, reservoir operations, system response, hydraulics, mapping, and consequences is conducted. Knowledge uncertainty in results as quantified by differences among the multiple realizations is evaluated during this review. Review of the production phase deliveries is documented through a review checklist. Appendix A contains the checklist template.](#_Ref207117602)

[The deliverables from the production phase are:](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Completed Production Phase Review Checklist](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Completed Production Phase Report](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Final models applied to simulation (including any hot fix alternate versions) posted to /production/[model]/ directories](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Conformance phase event-level model results posted to production/simulations directory](#_Ref207117602)

  • [Conformance phase summary model results posted to production/simulations](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: [—Final Report Phase](#_Ref207117602)
docs.text:
[After the production phase has been completed, including close-out of the quality control review checklist and approval from the technical oversight review, the final reporting phase transfers and communicates the results of the study. The full project directory dataset is archived for long term storage. The final report is prepared to document the overall effort and to formally close the project.](#_Ref207117602)

[A set of standard reports is written for each phase in the FFRD project, and templated for these reports are included in Appendix B. The final report will be a relatively short document that refers to the documentation that is created at the end of each phase of the project for detailed model development, calibration and results.](#_Ref207117602)

[Figure 14.1. Context of the Final Report Phase of the Future of Flood Risk Data Workflow](#_Ref207117602)

[](#_Ref207117602)

[\ ](#_Ref207117602)

[This page is intentionally blank.](#_Ref207117602)

docs.title: Job Aid 1—Scoping Processes
docs.text: This job aid describes considerations for the major project scoping decisions that are used to define the overall project scope.

docs.title: Watershed Study Extent
docs.text:
The Future of Flood Risk Data (FFRD) project will typically be scoped using Hydrologic Unit Code (HUC)-4 watersheds as previously established in the national watershed boundary dataset, but special considerations may apply. The scoping documentation will clearly describe all rational related to any adjustments to the watershed study extent and these adjustments should be vetted with FFRD program leadership.

An FFRD specific geospatial polygon will be created for the project, be based on the full resolution project digital elevation model (DEM), and follow FFRD spatial projection standards.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > study-area-polygon

docs.title: Hydraulic Modeling Unit Delineation
docs.text:
FFRD studies at the HUC-4 level are generally too large to efficiently develop and calibrate two-dimensional (2D) hydraulic models with sufficient detail to meet the study objectives. Therefore, the study basin is broken out into multiple hydraulic model units that define the extents for separate 2D hydraulic models. Considerations for hydraulic model unit delineation for FFRD studies are provided below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > hydraulic-model-units

docs.title: Basin Size
docs.text:
The FFRD program uses HUC-4 boundaries to define the watershed study area. Developing full basin 2D hydraulic models at the HUC-4 scale following the procedures in this document is generally deemed impractical using the computing capability available with current technology. Therefore, smaller hydraulic modeling units will be defined as part of FFRD scoping process.

Subbasins or model units with drainage areas of 500 to 1,000 square miles are practical for FFRD hydraulic modeling. The HUC-8 level boundaries provide a rough guide for subbasin delineation. The technical lead reviews the HUC-8 boundaries and adjusts to find more natural breaks from a hydraulic modeling perspective. There are various reasons the HUC-8 boundaries may be adjusted, specifically consideration is given to location of significant dams, stream gages, or population centers.

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Boundaries
docs.text:
The boundaries will also align with hydrologic model boundaries, therefore subbasins of the FFRD hydrologic models must be considered. It is best practice for hydraulic model unit boundaries to align with hydrologic model boundaries, such that hydrologic model output can be more seamlessly used as an input boundary condition for the hydraulic model. Although hydrologic sub-basin boundaries may be edited during the FFRD model development process, this is an important planning consideration.

Hydrologic model boundaries and catchments are commonly delineated in a coarser resolution DEM than is generally used for hydraulic modeling. This can result in differences in watershed delineation, which should be resolved for FFRD projects so that the boundaries match.

docs.title: Digital Elevation Model Alignment
docs.text: Hydraulic model unit boundaries should align with the full resolution DEM for the project. Effort should be made to identify structures, such as culverts and other hydro-connecting features, which can significantly alter the watershed delineation. Local knowledge and judgment may be required for a satisfactory watershed delineation, for example when there are interbasin canals or in watersheds which are very flat or contain significant underground drainage systems.

docs.title: Stream Gages
docs.text:
Stream gage data is critical to successfully developing the runoff, conveyance, and backwater parameters. These gages also serve as the primary means of evaluating model accuracy by providing observed data, which modeled results can be compared with. Therefore, model unit boundaries should generally be located away from important stream gage locations to avoid model results at gage locations from being unduly sensitive to boundary condition assumptions. Locating model unit boundaries away from important stream gages allows for model results and calibration metrics to be independent of boundary conditions.

If model unit boundaries must be located near stream gages for reasons that cannot be avoided, these gage locations can still be useful for informing boundary condition assumptions and ensuring accurate transition between upstream and downstream model units. In this case, it may be appropriate to use the gage’s published stage-discharge rating curve (which may need to be extended to include all flows of interest, even infrequent and extreme flows) as a downstream boundary condition in the hydraulic model.

docs.title: Levees
docs.text: Model unit boundaries should generally not intersect levee alignments as this splitting of the levee limits the ability to evaluate the levee’s behavior as a system. There may be occasional cases for extremely long levees where model unit boundaries must split the levee. In this case, careful consideration is required to avoid likely breach and overtopping locations. If a levee breach or overtopping occurs at the upstream end of the levee, separating the leveed area into multiple model units makes it difficult for leveed area inundation to flow through the artificial model unit boundary. The same effect can occur even if the breach/overtopping occurs near the downstream end of the levee. Modelers and team leads should give careful consideration to representation of inundation in areas where levees are adjacent to or split a levee system.

docs.title: Backwater Effects and River Confluences
docs.text:
Model unit boundary selection should consider whether a given area is subject to significant backwater effects, which can significantly complicate boundary condition assumptions. River confluences are a common location where backwater impacts can be significant and for this reason, model unit boundaries should avoid major river confluence areas.

Backwater impacts can be due to many factors other than river confluences. River control structures (such as locks and dams) can produce significant backwater effects, as can valley-wide constructions which limit the conveyance of a river valley. It may not be practicable to avoid all backwater impacts when selecting model unit boundaries. In these cases, boundary condition assumptions must account for these backwater influences. One way to accomplish this is to use a stage-discharge rating curve developed to account for backwater influence as the boundary condition. These rating curves can be developed from downstream model unit results and therefore require additional coordination between model units.

The scoped model unit polygons do not overlap, and the edges will match at all boundaries. Note that the extent of the hydraulic models developed for each model unit will commonly extend beyond the downstream boundary in order to avoid errors associated with boundary condition assumptions (more guidance included in SOP Volume II, Section 8).

docs.title: Urban Areas
docs.text: Urban areas should be contained within a single hydraulic model unit whenever possible.

docs.title: Dams
docs.text:
When possible, hydraulic model unit boundaries should not be located close to significant dams for the following reasons:

  • To minimize complex linking with other models. The linkages within the FFRD system of models can be simplified when each dam is internal to a single hydraulic model unit.

  • To better utilize available observed data for calibration. Dams often have associated flow and stage observed data that is best utilized for model calibration if boundary condition assumptions are remote from the observed data.

  • To limit impacts to dam breach scenarios. HEC-RAS hydraulic breach scenarios are impacted by the reservoir pool level and the downstream tailwater can rapidly rise during a breach event. The local hydrodynamics associated with a breach are more easily modeled when contained within a single model unit with minimal interactions with separate models.

docs.title: Delineation Process
docs.text: Considering factors included in the section, a geospatial polygon will be developed and coordinated with the larger scoping team to ensure workability and to identify any potential modeling issues. When a final delineation is made, it will be uploaded to the directory location listed in Section 1.2 as a key scoping deliverable.

docs.title: Uncalibrated Hydrologic Model
docs.text:
A complete uncalibrated hydrologic model will be developed as part of the scoping phase, in advance of development of other models during the calibration phase. This model will fully define the subbasins. Reaches and reservoir elements are delineated during the scoping phase, which is important baseline information for development of the name table and model linking register.

The guidance for development of the HEC-HMS model and the configuration for elements for FFRD studies is included in Volume II of the SOP.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > uncalibrated-hydrologic-model

docs.title: Mesh Development Criteria for Hydraulic Model
docs.text:
The scope for the development of hydraulic 2D meshes for the FFRD project is guided by specific layers developed during the scoping phase. The criteria for developing each of the layers is shown in Volume II, Section 4.3 of the SOP. All areas not included in these 3 layers will be part of the background mesh in FFRD models.

  • Urban Areas: Polygons should be sourced directly from the U.S. Census and provided in a geospatial format to match FFRD projection standards.

  • Key Streams: Key streamlines will be selected manually by following the criterial in the SOP. This geospatial layer that defines a portion of the scope of the modeling effort may be based on the existing National Hydrography Dataset, which may not align perfectly with stream centerlines based on the project DEM. This scoping layer will be used together with the Elevation Derived Hydrography (based on project DEM) stream layer during the modeling development process (Calibration Phase).

  • Other Streams: Developed manually based on criteria in the SOP. Similar use caveats as the key stream layer described above.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > mesh-development-criteria

docs.title: Name Table
docs.text:
The comprehensive name table will include all planned element names for all models. This is aided by the HEC-HMS subbasins, reaches, and reservoir elements that are built into the uncalibrated base model during the scoping and data preparation phase. Appendix C of SOP Volume II includes a template document with all necessary attributes. The names will all follow the conventions in Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > name-table

docs.title: Model Linking Register
docs.text:
The comprehensive model linking register table will include all planned connections for all models. There will be a separate table for historic event scenarios and for synthetic event scenarios (where no historic observation data is leveraged for inputs). A template document in included in Appendix C and includes all necessary attributes. The names will all follow the conventions in Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions.

Optionally, a graphical version of the linking tables may be developed to support team communication and understanding of the data linkages.

Special planning for reservoir components linkages is highly recommended during the scoping phase. It may be necessary to link separate components of the dam to separate outflow timeseries independently (for example, one timeseries for conduit releases, another for spillway flows, and a third for dam overtopping flows).

Note that the purpose of the model linking register is to develop a clear plan during scoping and improve team communication with regards to model linking throughout the project. This file is not directly used in cloud compute for applying the linkages. The process for ensuring accurate linkages are applied within cloud-compute events is described separately.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > model-linking-register

docs.title: Dam Data
docs.text: Dam data refinement and dam data collection are mentioned in sections below but are not core scoping activities.

docs.title: Dam Data Refinement
docs.text: The National Inventory of Dams (NID) will be leveraged as the official dam inventory source for FFRD studies. Prior to or during the scoping phase, the NID will undergo a data refinement process to ensure sufficient accuracy and completeness of the data for the entire study basin with a focus on ensuring data is of adequate quality to make dam scoping decisions for the FFRD study. The guidelines and procedures for this dam data refinement process are under development as of December 2025.

docs.title: Dam Data Collection
docs.text: Efforts to obtain additional dam information for the purposes of FFRD modeling, such as the physical structure data, reservoir storage information, risk assessment information, or operation manuals will be targeted to specific dams and based on the Dam Model Scoping Table developed according to the guidelines below. Processes for obtaining these datasets are provided in Job Aid 02, Data Preparation Processes.

docs.title: Dam Modeling Scope Selection
docs.text:
During the scoping phase, the model treatment for each dam in the basin will be clearly specified in the Dam Scoping Table, which provides an authoritative summary of the planned modeling approach for each dam. Appendix C of SOP Volume II contains a template dam scoping table.

The Dam Scoping Table will include a listing for every dam listed in the NID. The modeling approach in terms of operations modeling, hydrologic modeling, hydraulic modeling, breaching, and treatment of initial pool levels for synthetic events will be specifically defined in the table.

There are many combinations by which to model a dam across FFRD models, and dam scoping decisions must be made with consideration for the function of the full system of models. Guidelines for scoping the treatment of each dam within each component model are provided in the sections below, which balance modeling efficiency, accuracy, and the magnitude of impact to overall flood characteristics among other factors.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > dam-scoping-table

docs.title: Reservoir Operations Model Scope Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
Major reservoirs or reservoir systems that are actively operated for a downstream constraint should be considered for possible inclusion in an FFRD reservoir operations model. If a dam operates as part of a system only in rare cases, as a small system of dams, or other areas of uncertain or complicated operations scoping decisions will require engineering judgement. Reservoirs without a controlled outlet, or those that do not operate for a downstream constraint or in coordination with a system of other reservoirs should not be included. Reservoirs without controlled outlets should only be modeled in the reservoir operations model if their state (i.e., pool elevation, storage, outflows, etc.) is required to determine the operations at another facility being modeled. Reservoir operations that can be reasonably represented with simplified approaches in either the hydrologic or hydraulic model should not be scoped for inclusion in the reservoir operations model for the FFRD project.

Each dam selected for inclusion in the reservoir operations model will be documented in the Dam Scoping Table, and high-level modeling approach will be specified according to the attributes listed in the tables below.

Table 1. Reservoir Operations Model Scoping Options

Include in Reservoir Operations Model (Y/N)? No Yes, Include in HEC-ResSim

Table 2. Scoping Options for Reservoir Operations

Number of Unique Outflow Features? ("ResSim Dams") N/A 1 2 3 >3

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Scope Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
HEC-HMS reservoir elements should be included for dams that are expected to significantly impact flood characteristics at the basin scale, with special consideration for dams that impact the ability to match downstream observed gage data for flood events. The availability of physical dam data and operational data to support modeling inputs may be considered when scoping the hydrologic modeling approach for individual dams. Watershed size, reservoir storage, operation characteristics, and river slope are among the factors in the evaluation of impact of individual dams. As a general guideline, dams with over 10,000 acre-feet in storage and a flood control mission are candidates for inclusion as HEC-HMS reservoir elements.

For dams selected for inclusion as reservoir elements in the hydrologic model, the specific modeling method for outflows will also be included in the Dam Scoping Table. Various methods are available depending on the reservoir characteristics. As reservoirs and their operations increase in size and complexity, additional modeling techniques are required. These techniques are described below in Table 3.

During the Calibration Phase of the project, it is acceptable to use the Specified Release routing method with observed outflows from the reservoir. This ensures that downstream subbasins can be calibrated without the added uncertainty of reservoir routing. When observed specified releases are leveraged for the Calibration Phase, the simplest reservoir routing method necessary should be chosen for use during the Conformance Phase and Production Phase models.

Simple reservoir operations include one to one storage and discharge relationships, uncontrolled outlets, and ungated spillways. Typically, if the outlets and spillways have simple rating curves not dependent on inflow or seasonality, the dam can be modeled with simple procedures in HEC-HMS. A single outflow curve is the simplest hydraulic routing method available in HEC-HMS. If outlets are gated or controlled, simple operations can still be used by analyzing observed data and estimating an average outflow for a given elevation or storage condition. For more complex reservoir operations, modelers typically rely on HEC-ResSim to model operating schedules, rules, and downstream constraints, however rule-based operations in HEC-HMS can serve as a close approximation.

Table 3. Hydrologic Model Dam Treatment for Historic Events

Hydrologic Modeling Treatment – Historic Events Reservoir Element—Rule-Based Operations Reservoir Element—Outflow Structures Reservoir Element—Outflow Curve Reservoir Element—Specified Release No Reservoir Routing Element

docs.title: Hydraulic Model Scope Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
In the hydraulic model, various treatments and levels of detail are available depending on the reservoir characteristics. As reservoirs and their operations increase in size and complexity, more detailed modeling techniques are needed to account for the impact of dam operations, initial pool levels, and the possibility of breach events to accurately characterize flood risk. The selection of the specific hydraulic modeling treatment for each dam must occur in coordination with selected techniques for the hydrologic and reservoir operations models with the goal of establishing a cohesive plan for the full system of models. Tables 4 and 5 show the options for hydraulic modeling techniques and methods. Section 8 of the SOP includes specific modeling techniques for each type of model treatment.

Table 4. Hydrologic Model Dam Treatment for Synthetic Events

Synthetic Event Hydrologic Modeling Treatment Reservoir Element—Rule-Based Operations Reservoir Element—Outflow Structures Reservoir Element—Outflow Curve Reservoir Element—Specified Release No Reservoir Routing Element

Table 5. Hydraulic Model Dam Modeling Treatment

Hydraulic Modeling Treatment Type A—Operation Modeled outside of Hydraulic Model* Type B—Operation Modeled by Hydraulic Model* Type C—Simplified Outflows Modeled by Hydraulic Model* Type D—Basic Representation in Hydraulic Model Type E—Not Modeled

*Hydraulic Model Treatment can support breach events

The key consideration for hydraulic model treatment selection is to ensure the effects of all dams that have a significant impact on flood risk characteristics by way of providing flood storage or inducing excess flooding if breaches are representing by the model. All dams that meet these criteria shall be scoped to receive Type A, B, or C treatment, which account for reservoir storage and are capable of assessing the impacts of breach events.

A threshold of 2,500 acre-feet of storage can be considered a guideline for inclusion of dams for apply Type A, B, or C treatments. The volume of storage relative to drainage area for the dam, hazard classification, and immediate proximity to population centers may also be considered in the selection of hydraulic model treatment.

Type A treatment will be chosen for dams that have been selected for inclusion in either the reservoir operations model or for advanced outflow modeling method in the hydrologic model.

Type B treatment will be chosen for dams when the hydraulic model will provide superior outflow simulation compared to the chosen methods for hydrologic model, and physical dam information and operational information is available to support a more detailed simulation within the hydraulic model.

Type C treatment will be chosen for dams when the hydraulic model will provide superior outflow simulation compared to the chosen methods for hydrologic model, but physical and operational data is limited, and outflow features must be approximated.

Type D treatment will be chosen for dams that do not meet the threshold for Type A, B, or C treatments.

Type E treatment is reserved for dams that are deemed to be insignificant. This determination may be related to inability to confirm a dam exists, very small dams, partially breached dams, or other factors determined by professional judgement.

Table 6. Initial Pool Elevation

Include Hydraulic Initial Pool Elevation (Initial Condition [IC] Point)? Yes No

IC points must be used in the hydraulic model to simulate events with reservoirs that are not dry at the start of the storm event. All dams selected for Type A, B, or C modeling treatments must include IC points, and all other dams are not required include initial conditions points.

docs.title: Dam Breach Model Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
The Dam Scoping Table will include a clear determination of which dams will be allowed to breach in the FFRD stochastic simulations. The guideline for selection of these dams follows the threshold described for selection of hydraulic model treatments, and therefore all dams that are modeled with Type A, B, or C treatments will also be allowed to breach.

Table 7. Breach Modeling of Dams

Sample Breach Yes No

docs.title: Reservoir Initial Pool Elevations for Stochastic Events
docs.text:
The variation in initial reservoir pool elevations at the start of simulated events can be an important factor for flood characterization within the context of the quasi-continuous simulations approach for FFRD. A clear determination of which dams will be simulated with a varied initial pool elevation, will be included in the Dam Scoping Table. The source for the sampled elevations will also be identified.

The available data to support sampling of varied pool elevations is the key consideration regarding this scoping decision. Calibrated period of record modeled simulations from either the reservoir operations or hydrologic models in certain locations may provide a defensible simulated observed record for locations where observed data is not available or reliable.

When no observed or simulated data is available to support sampling, a static water surface for all stochastic events is acceptable but care should be taken to ensure that this assumption does not drive representation of flood characteristics for the basin, and it should be avoided for larger significant dams that may have variable water levels.

Table 8. Options for Initial Pool Elevation for Stochastic Events

Vary Initial Pool Elevation during Stochastic Events Yes—Sourced from Reservoir Operations Model Period of Record Simulation Results Yes—Sourced from Hydrologic Model Period of Record Simulation Results Yes—Sourced from Observed Period of Record No—Static Initial Pool for all Events Not Applicable (No IC Point)

docs.title: Levee Data
docs.text:
The National Levee Database (NLD) will be leveraged as the official source of inventory, alignments, and crest profiles of levees for FFRD studies. Prior to the study, the NLD will undergo a data refinement process to ensure sufficient accuracy and completeness of the data for the entire study basin to adequately support the FFRD study. The guidelines and procedures for this levee data refinement process are documented outside of this SOP.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > levee-data > nld

Files: levee alignment and profile geospatial data

docs.title: Levee Modeling Scope Selection
docs.text:
All levees will be represented in the FFRD hydraulic models (according to guidelines in Job Aid 09, Levee Representation) and all levees will be scoped for at least one breach location (as described in Job Aid 11, Levee Breach Workflows).

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > levee-modeling-scope

Files: levee breach locations (geospatial with attributes)

docs.title: Calibration Scope
docs.text:
A clear plan for the approach to event-based calibration of FFRD models is established during scoping. This plan consists of both establishing stream gage performance metric target prioritization and selecting specific historic events to be used during model calibration efforts.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > calibration-scope

Files: Gages (geospatial with attributes), Calibration Event Table

docs.title: Calibration Gage Category Assignments
docs.text: The following sections outline how assignment of gages in primary, secondary, and tertiary categories are completed.

docs.title: Calibration Metric Hierarchy
docs.text:
All active stream gages with a significant period of record of observed data are used for calibration of FFRD models. Each of the gages are categorized as either primary, secondary, or tertiary gages to describe the hierarchy for use in model calibration. For example, more rigorous calibration metrics are used for primary gages than for secondary gages. The gages are categorized during the scoping phase ensuring consistency in calibration targets across all FFRD modeling efforts for the basin. The selected gages—with primary, secondary, and tertiary category attributes—are delivered as part of the scoping and data preparation..

Primary gages are typically located on major streams, have plentiful and reliable observed data, and/or are used in reservoir operational decisions. Secondary gages may also be cited similarly as primary gages, however, they may be located on smaller streams and not used when operating flood control reservoirs. In general, one primary and one secondary gage should be located within each hydraulic modeling domain with the remaining gages designated as tertiary. However, this categorization is subjective.

docs.title: Prioritization of Gages
docs.text:
Some modeling units may include numerous stream gages, and while information from all these gages would be incorporated into FFRD models in an ideal scenario, it must be acknowledged that there are constraints of time and funding which limit what refinement can be performed. Prioritization of gages should consider factors such as data availability, potential for flood risk near the gage, and many other factors, such as:

  • Location of gages within model unit—At least one primary gage within each model unit, if one exists.

  • Gage period of record—Consider:

  • Does observed data cover majority of calibration events?

  • Does the data contain significant gaps?

  • Do these gaps occur at times of peak flow/stage?

  • Do large reservoirs or other control features exist?

  • Is this gage used in reservoir operational decisions?

  • Population Center near gages.

  • Gage Data Quality—Consider:

  • Is gage data reliable and consistent over the period of record?

  • Does the gage provide data at a sufficient time interval, such as 15-minute or hourly data, as opposed to daily data?

  • If only annual peak flow data or nearby high-water marks are available, this can still be useful as a peak stage/flow comparison.

  • Nearness to other gages—Redundancy of gages representing similar or overlapping areas should be avoided.

  • Spatial Coverage throughout Basin—Ensure coverage throughout the basin, even with observed data of lesser data quality.

  • Give consideration to mainstem rivers, major tributaries, then minor tributaries.

  • Give consideration to representing both regulated and unregulated areas within the basin.

  • Drainage Area Coverage—Assign less priority to areas with small drainages.

  • Similarity of Physiographic Features between Nearby Gages (e.g., slope, land-use, land-coverage, soil type, etc.)—Give priority representing the range of conditions existing within the watershed if a modeling unit contains diverse physiographic properties.

docs.title: Exclusion of Gages
docs.text: There may be scenarios where gage data will not be used. Strong justification is required to support the decision to exclude a stream gage from consideration. Examples may include a stage-only gage lacking a reliable elevation datum, a gage representing a very small drainage area (less than one square mile), or a gage where data was not recorded during the calibration events. For some streams and basins where flows are particularly flashy, daily data may not be sufficient for use in calibration. However, there are scenarios where daily data may be a useful data source, engineering judgement should be used in these cases.

docs.title: Gage Selection Rationale
docs.text: Table 9 provides an example of rationale for selection of a gage. In this example, the three gages in the Bardwell model unit can be examined. All three of these gages represent a similar area, however, the gage selected as primary included discharge data as well as stage. The gage selected as secondary had a larger drainage area and was therefore prioritized over the tertiary gage. Similar rational was applied across the watershed.

docs.title: Observed Data Connection to Model Element Names
docs.text: The observed data will be compared to modeled data throughout the project. During the scoping phase, the connection of observed gage locations to the model element names will be included as attributes in the calibration scope geospatial point file. For each gage location HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim, and HEC-RAS model elements (matching the Name Table) will be included as attributes.

docs.title: Calibration Event Selection
docs.text:
Historic storm events to be used for FFRD model calibration will be selected during the Scoping and Data Preparation phase of the FFRD project. Typically, six events are selected. Less events may be selected if data is not available in areas with limited gage record or arid regions with few events.

Calibration events are selected with consideration of the factors listed below in priority order:

  1. Precipitation data availability

  1. Gage data, or other observed data availability

  2. Spatial coverage throughout basin (precipitation coverage in such a way that the entire model unit experiences a range of flooding)

  3. When considering events such that each portion of the basin experiences at least one flood event, prioritization is given to the largest events of recent record (The largest events with associated high-quality gridded precipitation data)

  4. Type of storm event (representation across all event types applicable to the basin, like snowmelt, rain on snow, tropical storm, etc.)

  5. Consideration is given to similar events within weather generation—a single event instead of a wet season or long series of smaller flood events, when possible

  6. Consideration is given to changes within a watershed that would majorly impact calibration. If possible, events should be selected that best represent the current condition of the basin. Examples of large system changes are long term cyclical weather patterns, construction of a large dam or levee system, significant mining or urbanization, significant changes to a system response due to wildfire or sedimentation.

  7. Seasonality of impactful events

Each model type (i.e., HEC-RAS, HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim) should use consistent simulation times (start time and end time) for each calibration event. For example, for each calibration event, all hydraulic models should specify identical simulation windows. These simulation windows may vary between model types, but models with dependencies should fully cover the simulation window of the models which depend on them. For example, HEC-HMS simulation window should equal or exceed the HEC-RAS simulation window because HEC-RAS depends on output from HEC-HMS.

docs.title: CALIBRATION EVENT Length—Rainfall
docs.text: The time window for each selected event will be clearly stated. A rainfall driven calibration event ranges from just prior to start of precipitation and extends through the hydrograph peak at the downstream model unit boundary. Where possible these events should extend 1 to 2 weeks, but up to 30 days is acceptable. HEC-HMS model times may start just before the HEC-RAS models (generally 1 day but could extend further) to address ICs. If a downstream boundary includes a tropical surge, HEC-RAS modeling should start prior to both the surge and rainfall.

docs.title: CALIBRATION EVENT Length—Snowmelt
docs.text: A snowmelt driven event should start prior to the rising of the flow and extend through to peak of hydrograph at the downstream model unit boundary. If rainfall is a driver of snowmelt, the simulation should start prior to the start of precipitation. Initiating snowmelt may take more than just a single day of warm weather, so the timing of when to start event simulation should take the flow hydrograph into consideration in addition to the start of forcing factors. HEC-HMS model times may start just before the HEC-RAS models (generally 1 day but could extend further) to address these ICs. Snowmelt is typically a slow process; however, a snowmelt flood event is typically associated with a factor that triggers more rapid snowmelt. These events last on the order of one month but may need to extend up to 2 to 3 months to capture the peak of the snowmelt hydrograph and the receding limb. Snowmelt is an annual event, so it can be helpful to model consecutive water years and examine the observed and modeled snow water equivalent (SWE) in a continuous model to understand the snowmelt seasonal patterns and typical snowpack duration.

docs.title: CALIBRATION EVENT Length—Combination Event
docs.text: A rain-on snow event should range from just prior to start of precipitation and extend through to peak of hydrograph at the downstream model unit boundary. Where possible these events should extend 1 to 2 weeks, but up to 30 days is acceptable. During a rain-on-snow event that occurs during the snowmelt season, prior to precipitation falling there already be an underlying baseflow due to snowmelt occurring in the basin. HEC-HMS model times should start at a point to capture any of these ICs contributing to the overall peak flow. Therefore, it is acceptable if HEC-HMS models start just before the HEC-RAS models (generally 1 day but could extend further) to address these initial conditions. If a downstream boundary includes a tropical surge, HEC-RAS modeling should start prior both the surge and rainfall.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
The scoping and data preparation phase of an FFRD project includes the preparation of several datasets that will serve as the foundation for model development and production during the subsequent phases of the project. This document details the guidance and workflows for collecting many of the required datasets as listed in Section 4 of SOP Volume II.

Note that other datasets that are not included in this appendix are also prepared during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase, but they require additional coordination with Hydraulics and Hydrology modeling team members and are described in more detail documents.

docs.title: Data Delivery Location and Naming Conventions
docs.text: All shared data prepared and delivered for FFRD project use is stored in the cloud-based project directory accessible via Model Library according to the standard directory structure. Specific paths for individual datasets are called out throughout this document. Naming conventions (provided in a Job Aid 06) must also be followed.

docs.title: Geospatial Data Projection
docs.text: SOP Volume II discusses the specific parameters associated with the standard projection, which must be adhered to for all geospatial datasets.

docs.title: Geospatial Data Formats
docs.text:
The preferred file format for vector data (points, lines, and polygons) is the GeoPackage.

The preferred file format for basin-wide raster data (digital elevation models [DEMs], landcover, etc.) is the GeoTIFF.

docs.title: Data Manifest
docs.text:
The data manifest is a required deliverable from the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase. It serves as a comprehensive record of all datasets delivered during this phase, including metadata for geospatial files, models, reports, and other data types provided. The data manifest must document all pertinent information about the datasets. The manifest ensures traceability, consistency, and usability of the data for subsequent phases of the project.

Multiple team members contribute to the preparation of data supplied during the Scoping and Data Preparation phase. Therefore, multiple team members must contribute to population of the data manifest. The prepared data can be broadly categorized into three categories: Geospatial data, reference data, and scoping data, which are described below.

  1. Geospatial Data. The collection of all official input data to be used as a consistent source for the entire FFRD modeling effort. Many of the datasets are data pulls from centralized nationwide databases that are maintained by others, and represent a static snapshot of the data, clipped to the project study extent to be applied to the FFRD project consistently. This includes the DEM, National Land Cover Database (NLCD), National Hydrography Dataset (NHD), and several other datasets.

  2. Reference Data. The collection of all relevant data to be consistently referenced by all efforts for an FFRD project. This includes observed existing models and existing reports among other items.

  3. Scoping Data. Datasets that define the scope of the FFRD project. Many of the scoping datasets involve analysis by the technical team to define the scope. This includes the study extent polygon, model unit polygons, mesh development criteria, and calibration event selection among other items.

A template file for the data manifest in available in Appendix C. The following attributes are included in the data manifest.

Table 1. Attributes for Datasets Included in Data Manifest Template

Attribute Description Dataset Name Name of dataset Path Location of dataset in FFRD repository Description of Content Content description Category Category of dataset: Geospatial, Reference Data, Scoping Data, Observed Data, Models, other. Purpose Purpose of the dataset for FFRD Project Use. Refer to Section 4 of FFRD SOP. Generation Method Is data direct from source or derived based on the source? Study Type Applicable for Flood Insurance Rate Map Database (FIRM) Specific datasets: Effective, Base Level Engineering (BLE), BLE Effective Source Published organization plus any specific data systems within the organization. (e.g., Federal Emergency Management Agency [FEMA] Mapping Information Platform [MIP], U.S. Geological Survey [USGS] Three-Dimensional Hydrography Program [3DHP], National Structure Inventory [NSI], etc.) Source Type National, State, Local, Multiple, Other Source Detail Descriptive text regarding source, such as URL used for access. File size size of the dataset Currency Last update date of the dataset provided for FFRD project Geospatial Data Type Polygon/Point/Polyline/Raster/Image/Not Applicable

At the end of the scoping and data preparation phase, the data manifest must be delivered to the project location below, and it will be reviewed for accuracy and completeness prior to close-out of the phase.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > data-manifest.xlsx

docs.title: FEMA Datasets
docs.text:
Applicable FEMA data for the FFRD modeling efforts include regulatory effective data and BLE data. Various spatial vector data, raster data, and reports are available to download from both publicly available and access-restricted sources. The information in the sections below provide comprehensive listings of available FEMA data and access information. The items listed in Section 4 of SOP Volume II are the minimum required deliverables for the FFRD Scoping and Data Preparation Phase.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > fema

*all FEMA data including FIS reports and effective NFIP flood zone polygons.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > existing-models

*all existing H&H models associated with FEMA efforts

docs.title: Effective Data
docs.text: Relevant effective data include the FIRM database, Flood Insurance Study (FIS) reports, and Flood Risk Database.

docs.title: Flood Insurance Rate Map Database Geospatial Layers
docs.text:
The FIRM is the official regulatory map of a community on which FEMA has delineated the Special Flood Hazard Areas (SFHAs) and Base Flood Elevations (BFEs). The effective FIRM data relevant to FFRD modeling efforts include the spatial vector layers and tables noted below.

Metadata, project narratives, and the source citation table may also be available to reference for the sources used in the study and other background information. Effective data issued more than 10 years ago may not have as much detailed data available due to changes in the data, software, tools, and modeling methods used over the years. Most hydraulic data for studies newer than 1995 are based on Hydrologic Engineering Center’s River Analysis System (HEC-RAS) software, but most pre-1995 studies are based on HEC-2 data. Most hydrologic data for studies newer than 1992 are based on HEC’s Hydrologic Modeling System (HEC-HMS) software, but most pre-1992 studies are based on HEC-1 data.

  • Base Flood Elevations: The S_BFE layer shows the location and attributes for BFE lines and can be used to reference elevations where no effective cross sections are available.

  • Flood Hazard Areas: The S_Fld_Haz_Ar layer shows the location and attributes for flood risk zones.

  • Gages: The S_Gage layer is based on hydrologic data and provides the location and attributes for gages in a study area.

  • General Structures: The S_Gen_Struct layer shows the location and attributes for flood control structures such as dams, culverts, bridges, etc., in the study area.

  • Profile Baselines: The S_Profil_Basln layer shows the location and attributes for profile baseline and stream centerline features for the Flood Risk Project area.

  • Cross Sections: The S_XS layer shows the location and attributes for cross section lines for flooding sources. This layer contains all cross sections in a model, not just the lettered cross sections. This layer can be used as a reference for locating relevant cross section data, such as elevation and bathymetric data not captured by LIDAR.

  • Subbasins: The S_Subbasin layer is based on hydrologic data and provides the extents and attributes for subbasins in the study area.

  • Summary of Discharges: The L_Summary_Discharges table is based on hydrologic data and provides information about the hydrologic discharge information associated with nodes referenced in the FIS Report Summary of Discharges table. The information on stationing, discharges, and elevations in this table can be used to inform model development and cross-check results.

  • Summary of Elevations: The L_Summary_Elevations table provides information about SFHAs with static elevations that are referenced in the FIS Report Summary of Elevations table. This table can be used as a reference for cross-checking water surface elevation results.

  • Water Areas: The S_Wtr_Ar layer shows the location and attributes for hydrography features shown on the FIRM as areas. This layer can be used as a reference for flooding sources not included in the S_Fld_Haz_Ar.

  • Water Lines: The S_Wtr_Ln layer shows the location and attributes for hydrography features shown on FIRM as lines. This layer can be used as a reference for flooding sources where profile baselines are not available.

docs.title: Flood Insurance Study Reports
docs.text: The FIS report is a compilation and presentation of flood risk data for specific watercourses, lakes, and coastal flood hazard areas within a community. When a flood study is completed for the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the information and maps are assembled into a FIS.
