The FIS report contains detailed flood elevation data in flood profiles and data tables, and can be used as a reference for elevation data and FIS background information that may be referenced in developing FFRD hydrologic and hydraulic models.

docs.title: Flood Risk Database
docs.text: The Flood Risk Database is resource that supplements the flood hazard information on effective FIRMs, FIS, and FIRM database products. This database includes water surface elevation grids, depth grids, and Areas of Mitigation Interest. This database may not be available for all communities.

docs.title: Base Level Engineering Data
docs.text:
There are some instances where effective data is not available for a community, but BLE data is available. The BLE process uses the best available data and combines automated techniques with traditional model development to create flood hazard boundaries for the 1-percent-annual-chance event. Flood hazard boundaries are also typically estimated for other recurrence intervals. The results from BLE can be used to produce both regulatory and non-regulatory products. BLE data relevant to the FFRD modeling efforts include HEC-RAS models, vector spatial data, raster data, and a BLE report. More information on the relevant data associated with BLE studies is listed below:

  • Base Flood Elevations: S_BFE data is available for BLE studies and follows the same requirements as the S_BFE layer listed in the FIRM Database subsection above.

  • Flood Hazard Areas: S_Flood_Haz_Ar data is available for BLE studies and follows the same requirements as the S_Flood_Haz_Ar layer listed in the FIRM Database subsection above.

  • Water Lines: The S_Wtr_Ln data is available for BLE studies and follows the same requirements as the S_Wtr_Ln layer listed in the FIRM Database subsection above.

  • HEC-RAS Models: Hydraulics modeling associated with BLE studies are based on HEC-RAS software and typically include multiple events, including the 1- and 0.2-percent-annual-chance events. Types include both steady-state, one-dimensional (1D) models as well as unsteady, two-dimensional (2D) models.

  • Flood Depth Grids: These grids are produced as raster data and typically include the 1- and 0.2-percent-annual-chance events. The grids are typically stored in the Flood Risk Database referenced in the Effective Data section above.

  • Water Surface Elevation Grids: These grids are produced as raster data and typically include the 1- and 0.2-percent-annual-chance events. The grids are typically stored in the Flood Risk Database referenced in the Effective Data section above.

  • BLE Report: This report provides detailed information on the methodology used for the study.

docs.title: Data Gathering Processes
docs.text: A structured data gathering process is essential to ensure the accuracy, comprehensiveness, and documentation of information available for FFRD modeling efforts. This section outlines the key sources and steps for obtaining relevant effective and BLE data.

docs.title: Sources for FEMA Data
docs.text:
Sources for effective and BLE data from FEMA are listed below:

  • FEMA Flood Map Service Center (MSC): This is a publicly available source that can be used to obtain effective flood hazard data, including Flood Risk Databases, FIRMs, and FIS reports. Digital effective spatial data is available to download by county or state through the National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL).

  • Estimated Base Flood Elevation Viewer (eBFE): This is a publicly available source for Region 6 (Arkansas, Louisiana, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Texas) and Region 9 (Arizona, California, and Nevada) that can be used to review which Hydrologic Unit Codes (HUCs) are located within the area of interest and to download available BLE data. Data is not available for other regions.

  • Mapping Information Platform: This source requires access to the Risk Analysis and Management (RAM) Access Portal (RAP). The Flood Risk Study Engineering Library (FRiSEL) and MIP File Explorer can be used to obtain hydrologic and hydraulics data.

  • FileTrail: This source requires access to the RAP. It can be used to request data for studies too large to download from the MIP.

docs.title: Process to Obtain Data
docs.text:
By leveraging the available tools and platforms above, users can efficiently collect and validate the necessary datasets. The following subsections provide a comprehensive guide to accessing and utilizing these resources effectively.

While the MSC and eBFE Viewers are valuable resources for effective and BLE data, ensuring an individual with MIP access is involved in the data gathering process can significantly enhance the data available to use. MIP users benefit from advanced search tools and broader data access, which can help overcome the limitations faced by non-MIP users. MIP users have access to a broader range of hydrologic and hydraulic data, including detailed datasets that may not be available through public sources, such as the MSC or eBFE Viewer.

docs.title: Mapping Information Platform and Flood Risk Study Engineering Library
docs.text:
1. Use key words, such as the subbasin, watershed, or county name to search for hydraulics data using FRiSEL on the MIP to get an initial idea of what may be available for the area in question.

  2. Use Geographic Information System (GIS) software to determine which counties intersect the watershed or subbasin for your area of interest.

  3. Search FRiSEL using the Advanced Search to search by state, county, or community to determine availability of hydrologic and hydraulics data.

  4. For data too large to download from FRiSEL, use File Explorer in the MIP to download hydrologic or hydraulics data in smaller portions where possible.

  5. For remaining data too large to download from FRiSEL or File Explorer in the MIP, request data from FileTrail.

docs.title: Map Service Center and Estimated Base Flood Elevation Viewer
docs.text:
1. To obtain data from the MSC, use the MSC Search All Products feature to search by county or community to locate any effective data available for the area in question. If you search for effective NFHL data by state, you may search for any community in the state.

  2. To obtain data from the eBFE Viewer, select whether your area of interest is in FEMA Region 6 or Region 9 on the homepage. On the next window that appears, select the I Want to Download option. Then select the watershed(s) that note data is available to download available datasets and models.

docs.title: Digital Elevation Model
docs.text:
The requirements for the project DEM are provided in the main body of the SOP. The delivery location for the DEM is shown below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > terrain-base

*note full resolution and 100-foot versions are posted to same directory

docs.title: Source Data Search
docs.text:
The primary source for DEMs in the United Statis is the USGS 3DEP program, where the best available data can be accessed from the National Map.

A search for other local data sources that may provide higher quality DEM data should also be investigated. For example, many states maintain DEM data, as demonstrated in the following examples:

  • Connecticut: https://cteco.uconn.edu/data/download/flight2016/index.htm

  • Illinois: https://clearinghouse.isgs.illinois.edu/data/elevation/illinois-height-modernization-ilhmp

  • Kentucky: https://kyfromabove.ky.gov/maps/kygeonet::kentucky-elevation-data-dem-and-dem-tile-index

  • Massachusetts: https://maps.massgis.digital.mass.gov/MassMapper/MassMapper.html

  • New Hampshire: https://lidar.unh.edu/map/

  • North Carolina: https://sdd.nc.gov/

  • Ohio: https://gis1.oit.ohio.gov/geodatadownload/

  • West Virginia: https://data.wvgis.wvu.edu/elevation/

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: The following considerations and tips are intended to aid in DEM acquisition and preparation for the FFRD project.

docs.title: Snapping/Edge Matching
docs.text:
As described in SOP Volume II, the origin of the base DEM and all derivative grid products must be consistent and any lower resolution products must be based on an integer multiple of the full resolution base DEM. The reference origin is shown in SOP Volume II.

Methods for meeting this standard vary, but the cell size and origin coordinate of any raster can be checked with GIS software using the following GDAL command:

\$ gdalinfo your-dem.tif | grep -E "Origin|Pixel Size"

docs.title: Interpolation Method
docs.text: When reprojecting or resampling to a lower resolution, DEMs should be processed using bilinear interpolation when reprojecting or resampling to a lower resolution.

docs.title: Tiling
docs.text: Tiled GEOTiffs can be preferable to a single, merged, high-resolution DEM for the entire study basin due to file size challenges associated with transferring and consuming the data. Hydraulic modeling software accepts *.vrt files that are capable of compiling tiled GeoTIFFs.

docs.title: Download and processing scripts
docs.text: Processing of data to produce DEMs for FFRD use will typically be aided by scripts that have been thoroughly reviewed, and several open-source tools may be available to support this effort. A draft python script is provided in Appendix F that downloads high-resolution DEM data from the USGS source, converts elevation values to feet, projects to FFRD standard, and tiles the final data to less than 1 GB blocks stacked according to a .vrt file. Instructions and a sample shapefile to test the tool are included. Application of any scripts for FFRD DEM creation must be reviewed to ensure SOP requirements are met.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Metadata associated with the source DEMs should be retained, including metadata that clearly identifies all original sources of the final project DEM and their spatial extent.

docs.title: Elevation Derived Hydrography
docs.text:
Elevation derived hydrography (EDH) refers to geospatial layers that represent water features—including streams, rivers, shorelines, and catchments—directly from high-resolution elevation data. These layers ensure a consistent, topographically accurate hydrography dataset that aligns with terrain representation.

In the context of FFRD, EDH:

  • Replaces or enhances traditional hydrography datasets that may be outdated or misaligned with modern elevation surfaces.

  • Improves spatial accuracy of flow paths and watershed boundaries, which is important for modeling surface water movement and flood behavior.

  • Provides an authoritative hydrographic base layer for hydraulic modeling and floodplain mapping efforts.

If available, USGS 3DHP data is downloaded and used directly. Where 3DHP data is not available, EDH data is produced as part of the data preparation effort for the FFRD project. This centerline data is provided in two versions: short reaches that include attributes such as drainage area, and longer reaches un-attributed segments.

Efforts to align with USGS 3DHP program priorities for data development should be made.

Various GIS tools are available to support development of EDH, such as Esri’s Arc Hydro toolbox. Edits are commonly necessary at breaks in the channel flowline, such as roadway culvert crossings, where the stream may not align correctly between the upstream and downstream channels.

All identified streams with a drainage area of greater than 0.25 square miles should be included in the EDH delivery for FFRD.

The EDH layers provided will be formatted in GeoPackage format and delivered to the location below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > elevation-derived-hydrography

docs.title: National Hydrography Dataset and Watershed Boundary Dataset
docs.text:
The legacy National Hydrography Dataset Plus High Resolution (NHDPlus HR) and Watershed Boundary Datasets (WBD) are available for download by Hydrologic Unit 4-digit or 8-digit identifiers (HU4/HU8), by state, or nationally at the below link:

https://www.usgs.gov/national-hydrography/access-national-hydrography-products

The NHDPlus HR was processed using 10-meter 3D Elevation Program (3DEP) data. The dataset was initially released between 2018 and 2021 as reprocessed for certain regions in 2022 and 2023.

The WBD and WBD layers provided will be formatted in GeoPackage format and delivered to the locations below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > nhd

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > wbd

docs.title: Vertical Datum Conversions
docs.text:
An official listing of vertical datum conversions for point locations at critical sites throughout the basin will be established and documented during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase. Conversion values must be included for all stream gages and infrastructure dam locations that are needed to adjusted water surface elevations records or as-built dam elevation data to the vertical datum used for modeling. The source of the conversion values will be documented in the table.

Conversion of any gridded data such as surveyed bathymetry that is no specific XY point location should also described with a table entry.

When available, conversion values should be directly linked to official USGS stream gage reported values, from adopted values from dam operators or any sources that have been previously adopted by others.

When no previous conversion values are available, NOAA Vertical Datum Transformation tool is recommended.

A template for the listing of conversions is provided in Appendix F, and the completed table will be delivered to the following location.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > vertical-datum-conversions

docs.title: National Land Cover Database
docs.text:
NLCD is used as the minimum standard to aid in the parameterization of roughness coefficients, specifically Manning's “n” values, and percent impervious areas, which are crucial for simulating surface flow.

Data can be downloaded through the Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC) website. Annual data dating to 1985 is available at a 30-meter resolution. This data must be reprojected to the FFRD projection, clipped to the buffered study area, and provided in GeoTIFF format.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > land-cover

docs.title: Supplemental Land Cover Datasets
docs.text:
Optionally, additional land cover datasets may improve the ability to calibrate the FFRD models. The NOAA Coastal Change Analysis Program (C-CAP) Land Cover Atlas is available. Aerial Imagery may be processed with advanced techniques to directly support modeling efforts we well.

If these optional supplemental datasets are scoped for inclusion and coordinated with the leadership team for specific projects, they would be stored in the following project directory location.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > land-cover

docs.title: Soil Coverage
docs.text:
The Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) Soil Survey Geographic Database (SSURGO) provides delineations of soils information in shapefile format to which other attributes can be joined. Soil textures or hydrologic soil groups can be used for hydrologic model parameterization.

This dataset will be processed for the study basin and delivered to the following location.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > soils

docs.title: Bathymetric Data
docs.text:
A full search for available bathymetric data to supplement the above-water terrain information for the study basin is conducted during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase. The availability, type, and format will vary across individual study basins and across the nation. The table below provides common sources of data that may be used to support the FFRD study, but it is not intended to be comprehensive. Judgement and coordination with the larger PDT will be required to determine whether certain available datasets will provide value to the project and if their acquisition is warranted.

Table 2. Non-comprehensive FFRD Data Source List

Source Data and Information Link Description USACE Hydrographic Surveys Program Link includes links to the USACE Hydrographic Surveys interactive map and downloadable data, the National Channel Framework Dataset, and the Joint Airborne Lidar Bathymetric Technical Center of Expertise. NOAA Bathymetry Link includes links to various interactive maps and bathymetry products as well as resources for creating gridded data. Interactive maps include the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) Bathymetric Data Viewer and the International Hydrographic Organization (IHO) Data Centre for Digital Bathymetry Viewer (DCDB). Other products include Coastal Elevation Models, Estuarine Bathymetric Digital Elevation Models, Great Lakes Bathymetry, and other bathymetry projects. Reservoir Sediment Surveys or Tabulated Storage-Elevation Data N/A Owners or operators of large reservoirs may have an estimate of storage for a range of pool elevations may regularly monitor changes in available storage with surveys. USGS Inland Bathymetry Link Inland bathymetry is a component of USGS 3D National Topography Model (3DNTM) and the next generation of the 3D Elevation Program (3DEP), where our goal is to map all the topography of the nation, including submerged topography under lakes, rivers, and reservoirs, using airborne topobathymetric LIDAR (green wavelength lidar). USGS Maps of America’s Submerged Lands Link Compilation of USGS published bathymetric information for select locations Northeast Ocean Data Link Library of information along the coast and great lakes with a focus on the northeastern united states Existing Hydraulic Model Cross Sections N/A Legacy hydraulic models may retain valuable cross-section field survey data not preserved in other formats. Integrating this data into current models may be considered, provided its quality and significance warrant the effort associated with its extraction. Recreational Maps N/A Various sources of recreational maps provide bathymetric information, particularly in lakes.

Generally, the data collected should be provided in its original format, which may include point data, raster data, surveyed river cross sections, contour line data, or other formats. Certain formats should be prepared for FFRD use ensuring the location is represented in a geospatial format that adheres to the FFRD projection and vertical datum standards. Key metadata of each dataset should be maintained with the dataset and included in the data manifest.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > bathymetry

docs.title: National Levee Database (NLD)
docs.text:
A copy of the NLD geospatial data is placed in the FFRD project directory. A data refinement effort for the NLD should be completed prior to the start of the FFRD project and documentation of that effort is also included as a deliverable.

The layers delivered will include system alignment lines, system routes, centerlines, floodwalls lines, closure lines, and point feature data.

Additionally, an XYZM table based on the system route will be produced that captures the XY locations, levee system stationing, and elevation. The XYZM table will apply the FFRD projection, units, and vertical datum standards.

The delivery of NLD datasets is automatable, and the use of scripts to produce the required deliverables in encouraged.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > levees > nld

docs.title: National Inventory of Dams
docs.text:
A copy of the National Inventory of Dams (NID) attributed point data is placed in the FFRD project directory. A data refinement effort for the NID should be completed prior to the start of the FFRD project and documentation of that effort is also included as a deliverable.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > dams > nid

docs.title: National Structure Inventory
docs.text:
The NSI is a comprehensive system of databases managed by USACE. The main purpose of the NSI is to provide a detailed inventory of structures across the United States. This data is crucial for various applications, including flood risk management, emergency response, and infrastructure planning. The NSI databases contain structure inventories of varying quality and spatial coverage. The first version was published in 2011 and has since been updated in 2016, 2019, and 2022.

NSI data is comprehensive and includes information on the location, type, and characteristics of structures. This data is essential for detailed risk assessments and planning. The NSI provides extensive documentation, including technical references and user guides, to help users understand and utilize the data effectively. The NSI data is accessible through various platforms, allowing users to integrate it into their GIS and analysis workflows. An interactive map supports identification and download of available data.

The NSI API provides access to the NSI structures through query by polygon, bounding box, or Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) code. This allows a user to fetch structures in multiple formats, programmatically, to support a variety of workflows.

The naming convention from Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions, must be followed, and additional details regarding interaction with the NSI data is provided in Job Aid 17, Consequence Processes.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > structure-inventory

docs.title: Stream Gage Network
docs.text:
Stream gage data is foundational for calibration of model parameters and validation of results. The USGS stream gage network typically serves as the primary source, although other stream gage networks may be available depending on location.

Separate instructions describe the acquisition of the gage data and the scoping of the use of data at gages for model calibration.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > stream-gages

docs.title: National Bridge Inventory
docs.text:
The National Bridge Inventory includes a shapefile of roadway bridges, including attributes which may be referenced for development of hydraulic model inputs. Background information and a coding guide are available through the U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration Bridges and Structures website. An interactive map supports identification and download of available data.

Bridge attributes relevant to hydraulic modeling to retain include:

  • Road Name

  • Year Built

  • Deck Width

  • Skew

  • Span Type, Number, Length

  • Structure Type (Culvert, Bridge, etc.)

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > transportation

docs.title: National Railroad Bridges
docs.text:
The National Bridge Inventory does not include railroad bridges. However, the Federal Railroad Administration’s Bridge Inventory can be used as a supplement. Its data dictionary and links to an interactive map are available.

Attributes relevant to hydraulic modeling to regain include:

  • Owner

  • Name and ID

  • Number of tracks

  • Storage Location: model-library > ffrd-repository > basin-data > transportation folder

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > transportation

docs.title: U.S. Census Urban Areas
docs.text:
The U.S. Census Urban Area polygons are leveraged directly for certain aspects of scoping the FFRD study. A raw copy of the polygons are placed in the project directory. The data can be acquired at https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > census > urban-areas

docs.title: U.S. Census Incorporated Place Boundaries, Road and Railroad Data
docs.text:
Incorporated place boundaries, roads and railroad data can be a useful reference layers. The data can be acquired at https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > census

docs.title: Risk Assessment Studies
docs.text:
A search for dam and levee risk assessment data will be conducted. Common sources will include USACE, Bureau of Reclamation, and Federal Energy Regulatory Commission risk assessments. For FFRD projects, the USACE Dam and Levee leads will be responsible for assembling the existing risk assessment datasets. Collected reports should be posted to the locations below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > risk-data

docs.title: Physical Structure and Operation Information—Dams and Levees
docs.text:
Physical structure and operation information for dams and levees are key inputs into the scoping and modeling efforts for an FFRD study. These datasets include operations manuals, as-built drawings, and reservoir storage data among other information. Additional details specifying the procedures for searching for and acquiring the data are currently being developed and will be elaborated in a future version of this document.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > dams

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > levees

docs.title: Acquisition of Other Existing Models
docs.text:
A full search for additional existing hydraulics and hydrologic models will be conducted. Acquisition of models associated with the FEMA NFIP is described separately. Other common sources of available models include USACE Corps Water Management System (CWMS) models, local flood risk reduction feasibility study models, and dam breach models among others. Not all models serve as direct inputs into the FFRD project, but they are a useful reference. Judgement may be required to ascertain if model datasets are worth the effort to acquire.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > existing-models

docs.title: Local Data
docs.text:
Various other existing datasets may add value to the FFRD process, such as those that are brought forth during stakeholder engagement activities. Judgement will be required to incorporate certain datasets into the project directory. When data is supplied that does not fit in the categories provided, they may be posted to the directory below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > other

--

docs.title: Job Aid 3—Meteorologic Data Processes
docs.text: This job aid provides supplemental information and details related to the production of meteorologic data for an FFRD study.

docs.title: Watershed Boundary Input
docs.text:
The watershed boundary for an FFRD study is clearly defined during the scoping phase. This boundary is provided as input to the meteorology Project Delivery Team member for determining the appropriate transposition domain and assembling the collection of storms for the study.

The watershed domain as a geopackage with a single part polygon is stored in the root.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > project-scoping > study-data

docs.title: Transposition Region Selection
docs.text:
Storms do not occur consistently throughout the United States. To ensure storms are only moved within areas where the storm is equally probable to exist, the areas for collecting storms to transpose are limited to a transposition domain. A transposition domain represents a spatial area having homogeneous meteorologic conditions. Developing a good transposition domain requires meteorologic training. Transposition domains are generated by a small team and are delivered to the shared basin-data folder of the project directory.

A transposition region is the key assumption and driving factor in the Stochastic Storm Transposition (SST) approach for generating synthetic precipitation events. This domain is a geographic extent that experiences homogeneous storm-generating meteorology and contains the study watershed. The foundational assumption of SST is that a storm that occurred at one location within the transposition domain can freely be transposed to any other location within the domain. The size and shape of the transposition domain extent is informed by meteorological processes.

Rough initial domains can be established using products such as the U.S. Climate Divisions by starting with the division(s) that contain the study watershed and adding adjacent and nearby divisions that are sufficiently similar based on climate. The divisions have several limitations that make them unfit for generating a final transposition domain but can be indicative of transitions between climatic zones within a state. Comparing these boundaries to regions of high gradient in climatic variables (e.g., annual precipitation) or physical characteristics (e.g., elevation) can indicate meteorological driving mechanisms in the region.

An initial investigation of historical flood and storm causal mechanisms for the study watershed is warranted. Storm and flood reports from the largest events on record provide narrative descriptions and data summaries for significant events and an impression of important storms in the region. A transposition domain should contain the area around the study watershed where these kinds of storms are also likely and does not contain physical features that would prevent or drastically alter the behavior of a storm in that area (e.g., mountain ranges).

An initial screening step should be an evaluation of tropical cyclone (TC) activity using a dataset such as International Best Track Archive for Climate Stewardship (IBTrACS). TC activity can be visualized relative to the study watershed and areas with similar numbers of storm tracks are likely to be included in the transposition domain. The tracks may also reflect important physical features, such as blocking mountain ranges, that would need to be excluded. In areas where few TCs affect the watershed, areas of high TC activity should be excluded from the domain. Coastally adjacent areas are likely to have many TC tracks, and the domain should contain similar levels of activity throughout. An important boundary delineator in this case is often the distance from the coastline.

A meteorologist will take the input Hydrologic Unit Code (HUC)-4 watershed domain and utilize key datasets to generate a transposition domain.

Promising techniques that utilize extremal statistics of rainfall maxima determine transposition regions directly based on observed data may also be considered.

The transposition region for the study is developed during the Scoping and Data Preparation phase, stored as a geopackage with a single part polygon and posted to the location below.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > storm-catalog > transposition-region

docs.title: Storm Catalog Development
docs.text:
Storms are identified from a source of gridded precipitation that has a long period of record. Currently the appropriate data source is the Analysis of Record for Calibration (AORC) dataset. It has 45 years of record with hourly precipitation grids, at 4- or 1-kilometer resolution.

Storms are selected based on the target watershed; the objective is to find a storm that optimizes the precipitation over the target watershed for a given duration.

A threshold is leveraged to select storms that exceed a cumulative precipitation total, which helps to define which storms are included or excluded from the catalog. The threshold is set to target a number of targeted storms per year over the duration of the input datasource. For instance, in a 45-year data source, if the objective count of storms per year is 10, a threshold is set to collect 450 storms from the datasource that optimize the storm over the size of the target watershed.

The following inputs are required:

  1. A datasource of gridded precipitation (AORC dataset used for FFRD studies)

  2. A transposition domain (defined according to guidelines and considerations above)

  3. A watershed domain (typically HUC-4 watersheds)

  4. A user defined storm duration (72 hours for FFRD studies)

  5. A target threshold (average of 10 storms per year for FFRD studies)

docs.title: Storm Catalog Development Procedure
docs.text:
The following algorithmic procedures are employed by the cloud-compute, storm-cloud plugin to develop a ranking of storm events within the transposition region, and build the storm catalog for use within the SST framework.

Storm events were identified from the aggregated AORC data by looking at rolling 72-hour precipitation totals and finding events with significant 72-hour precipitation over an area the size and shape of the target watershed. Beginning at t=0, which corresponds to the first day of the period of record (01 January 1979), the procedure works as follows:

  1. Aggregate the daily product to a three-day total by taking the sum of the three daily total precipitation rasters (t, t+1, and t+2).

  1. Optimize the position of the watershed polygon within the transposition domain such that the three-day precipitation is maximized when averaged across the polygon. Limit the movement of the watershed polygon so no portion is ever outside the transposition domain during the search for the optimum position.

  2. Compute summary statistics for the precipitation inside the optimized watershed polygon position and associate those with the date t.

  3. Increment t by 1 and return to Step 1.

This process results in a list of dates with associated precipitation depths creating severe storms in an area the size and shape of the target watershed. The storm dates are separated such that the largest precipitation in each 72-hour window is retained, ensuring the same storm is not double counted. Once there is a complete listing of event dates and the associated maximized watershed precipitation, a threshold is chosen to isolate the most severe events. A threshold that results in an average of k events per year is determined to select storms. The threshold is applied to all storms and the k×number of years largest storms in the storm datasource are selected and added to the catalog.

For each storm event selected to be included in the catalog, additional metadata beyond the rainfall fields are computed and retained.

  • The location of the storm center is stored as a coordinate pair, determined by the center of the pixel where the maximum total accumulation of rainfall occurred.

  • The beginning date of the storm event.

  • The minimum, average, maximum, and total values of the rainfall accumulation pixel values in the maximized position over the watershed.

  • The storm type (assignment of type described in separate section)

  • The ratio of the mean accumulation in the maximized position to the average of the 72-hour 2-year rainfall from NOAA Atlas 14 at the same location.

No assumption is made about whether an individual event is an annual maximum with respect to any statistic, and creating an annual maximum series is not necessary. When the transpositions are performed, the storms themselves are never treated as annual maxima, only one of many possible realizations of a storm event within a year.

docs.title: Visualization and Review of Identified Storms
docs.text:
Figure 1 shows an example of a web-based tool for viewing identified and ranked storm events in the precipitation data. Storm events can be filtered and sorted based on several statistics. Displaying a visualization of the 72-hour precipitation as well as the optimized watershed location within the transposition domain aids in quality control the extracted storm events by providing a visual check on the behavior of the precipitation field, as well as displaying a number of useful statistics about the event that helps determine if the storm event will be added to the catalog.\

Figure 1. Example view of identified storm. Colored raster extents coincide with the transposition domain. Original watershed position in grey. Optimized watershed position in black. This storm event would be included in the catalog because its watershed-averaged precipitation is 5.63 inches, which makes it the largest event in 2011.

docs.title: Delivery of Storm Catalog
docs.text:
The selected storm catalog storms are delivered in named Data Storage System (DSS) files as well as in the form of a full catalog in HEC-HMS .grid format. This allows for easy importing into HEC-HMS for evaluation.

In a sub directory called, seasonality-distributions, a .csv of the cumulative density function of the day of year with the probability of the storm of a given storm type occurring on that day is stored and named by storm type. In a sub directory called, storms, the .dss files containing precipitation and temperature are stored.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > storm-catalog

docs.title: Normalization Grid for Transposition Region
docs.text:
A normalization grid is created to support the Normalized Transposition technique to improve the performance of SST. Section 5.7 of SOP Volume II includes the motivation and recommendations for developing and applying a normalization grid as part of the SST procedure. In areas where NOAA Atlas 14 is available and fully covers the transposition domain, the annual maximum series-derived, 72-hour, 100-year precipitation field should be used to normalize. It may be necessary to mosaic together several rasters spanning multiple volumes of Atlas 14. The rasters should be generated in .GeoTIFF format. When Atlas 15 becomes available, it should replace any usages of Atlas 14. It is anticipated that Atlas 15 will provide a seamless product that is spatially consistent and will not require creating a mosaic.

Where Atlas 14 or 15 is not available, the preferred normalization field is a 72-hour mean annual maximum field developed from the source precipitation dataset (AORC or similar). Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) normal or seasonal precipitation should not be used.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > storm-catalog > normalization-grid

docs.title: Storm Typing
docs.text:
Storm typing is a novel addition to the traditional SST recipe that improves the hydrologic modeling of flood responses and allows for interfacing with the coastal hazards modeling process. The categories used for this approach are intentionally broad since the distinction is important for two uses: storm seasonality and isolating tropical storms. The seasonality of the storm events is used to determine initial watershed conditions during the stochastic simulations so that implausible combinations of meteorology and watershed state cannot be created. Isolation of tropical cyclones is important for handling additional coastal modeling that may be required to characterize compound hazards caused by the combination of rainfall and storm surge. Currently, for FFRD studies, two broad storm categories are used:

  • Tropical Cyclones (TC)

  • Non-Tropical (NT)

Most rainfall events that occur on a sufficiently widespread scale for inclusion in a HUC-4 scale watershed catalog will fall into the NT category.

Manual storm classification of each storm in the catalog is recommended for FFRD studies, as no cloud-compute software action for automated classification has been fully released. Recommended procedures for automated techniques and tools for storm classification are expected to be incorporated into a future version of the FFRD SOP.

docs.title: Manual Storm Classification
docs.text:
TC, which include all phases of the lifecycle of a tropical storm (e.g. tropical depression, hurricane, etc.), are most prevalent near the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Coast, although Pacific storms can occur as well. Watersheds that are significantly inland may occasionally be impacted by remnants of tropical storms. Of all storm types, these are typically the easiest to identify due to the availability of datasets like IBTrACS or Hurricane Database (HURDAT), and their clear impact on surface analysis charts.

A combination of visual information is recommended for the classification process. Daily Weather Maps or other sources of surface analyses can often be used to rapidly identify Mid latitude cyclones and TCs. IBTrACS has a spatial product that makes visualization of TC tracks straightforward. Reanalysis datasets such as the contiguous United States for 40 years at 4-km resolution (CONUS404) produce useful visualizations of other key data indicators in space and can be interacted with using Python or other data visualization tools. Some key variables to evaluate include:

  • Storm season

  • Precipitation

  • Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE)

  • Mean Sea-Level Pressure (MSLP)

  • Storm Relative Helicity (SRH)

  • 500 hPa geopotential height and gradient

Other data and visualizations used at the discretion of the analyst can assist in completing the classifications.

docs.title: Storm Seasonality Output
docs.text: For each type of storm, a seasonality analysis will be produced. The seasonality analysis will describe the daily frequency of occurrence for each storm type based on the start date of the events in the storm catalog. Each day of the year from 01 January through 31 December will have a count of storm occurrence computed as the count of storms of that type beginning on that day of the year. The sum of the counts should equal the total number of storms of that type, and the sum of all tables should total the number of storms in the catalog.

docs.title: Fishnets for Storm Transposition
docs.text:
Fishnets represent valid storm placements for transposition and are created with the help of the HMS-mutator plugin and are saved as a .csv file. Uniform placements will be applied for studies executed with version 1 of the SOP. Additional details on the inputs, outputs, and algorithm for fishnet development are included in the cloud-compute documentation.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > storm-catalog > fishnets

docs.title: Stochastic Meteorlogic Model Results
docs.text:
Basin-average precipitation frequency data from stochastic storm transposition must be extracted from modeled storm placements to evaluate the conformance of results to observed data.

SST is driven by the Uncertainty Analysis, randomly varying the storm selection and storm coordinates, and extracting the precipitation that results from each transposition. An Uncertainty Analysis occurs in HEC-HMS with three parameters: Storm Center X-coordinate, Storm Center Y coordinate, and grid name (representing an event from the storm catalog). Set up a precipitation only discretization basin model for the project with a single subbasin of the entire study domain and follow the steps presented in this Precipitation-only SST Runs Guide, and using this procedure to extract subbasin averaged annual maximum series.

The annual maximum series data of subbasin averaged precipitation must be posted, where it can be programmatically leveraged for analysis, plotting, and evaluation.

Delivery Location:

  • Single Realization Results from Conformance Phase

  • basin-name > conformance > study-data > simulations > frequency-calibration

  • Multi Realization Results from Production Phase

  • basin-name > production > study-data > simulations > frequency-calibration

docs.title: Job Aid 4—Observed Gage Data Preparation
docs.text:
An authoritative set of observed data will be collected during the Data Preparation Phase and shared amongst the modeling team. Subsequent modeling efforts must make use of this authoritative set of observed data in order to avoid using conflicting data. Data that is gathered must also be adjusted prior to use to adhere to vertical datum and time zone requirements (as established in SOP Volume II, Section 3).

The authoritative dataset will be saved to the central basin-data directory for the project.

If other data becomes available during later phases (e.g., Calibration, Conformance, etc.), it must be incorporated into the authoritative set of data and shared with the entire modeling team. Any updates to the authoritative dataset that are must be coordinated with the technical lead.

This document describes the types and sources of data, data processing tools, and typical modifications that are required for use within FFRD applications.

docs.title: Types of data to be collected
docs.text:
Several types of data should be collected during an FFRD application. This includes:

  • Flow

  • Stage

  • Stage-Discharge Relationships

  • Stream Gage Field Measurement Dataset

  • High water marks

  • Flood imagery-satellite or airborne

  • Flood Photography-ground photography

Additionally, data should be collected at numerous temporal resolutions including (but not limited to):

  • Instantaneous (e.g., 5-, 10-,15, 30-, and/or 60-minutes)

  • Sub-daily (e.g., 3-, 6-, and/or 12-hours)

  • Daily

  • Annual Maximums

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
The United States Geological Survey (USGS) collects water resources data at over 10,000 active stream gages. This data is disseminated through websites like the USGS Water Data for the Nation site. Additional information including historical observations, statistics, instantaneous peak flow/gage height, and field measurements are also available. In most FFRD applications, data collected and distributed by the USGS will constitute the vast majority of pertinent data. However, there are numerous additional sources of water resources data that may be pertinent to FFRD applications including (but not limited to):

  • https://tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov/

  • https://www.usbr.gov/pn/hydromet/index.html

  • http://cdec.water.ca.gov/cdecstation2/

  • https://txwaterdatahub.org/dataset/highwater-marks-inventory

  • https://water.usace.army.mil/

  • https://stn.wim.usgs.gov/FEV/ (high water mark data)

  • https://www.usgs.gov/centers/eros (EROS satellite imagery depot)

An excellent summarization of pertinent data sources that are commonly utilized within USACE dam and levee safety studies can be found here: Data Sources for Estimating Hydrologic Hazards for Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessments.

docs.title: Vertical Datums
docs.text:
Commonly, historical data will be reported in a local/project-specific vertical datum or the National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929 (NGVD 29). As established in SOP Volume II, Section 3, all stage and/or elevation data used within FFRD applications must be refenced to the North American Vertical Datum of 1988 (NAVD 88). Utilities such as the NGS Coordinate Conversion and Transformation Tool or NOAA’s VDatum can be utilized to convert stages and/or elevations to NAVD 88.

Conversions from local project datums will also be clearly defined during the scoping and data preparation phase when they are available.

docs.title: Acquisition of Stream Gage Data
docs.text:
For all stream gages within the modeling domain, download and/or ingest the following data using HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue:

  • Instantaneous and/or sub-daily stage and flow data

  • Daily average flow data

  • Annual maximum series of flow

  • Annual maximum series of stage

  • Stream gage field measurement paired dataset (flow and stage)

  • Published stage-discharge relationships

Examples demonstrating these capabilities can be found here: Create a new HEC-SSP Study, Import Data, and Visualize Multiple Data Sets.

Additionally, several commonly available scripting languages (i.e., R and Python programming languages) have libraries that can be utilized to download this data as well. Example of these libraries include:

  • https://doi-usgs.github.io/dataRetrieval/

  • https://github.com/DOI-USGS/dataretrieval-python

docs.title: Extracting N-Day Duration Time Series
docs.text: For all stream gages within the modeling domain, calculate n-day duration flow time series (e.g., 1-, 2-, and 3-day durations) using HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue. Examples demonstrating these capabilities within HEC-SSP can be found here: Data Filtering, Extracting an n-Day Duration Time Series

docs.title: Quality Assurance/Quality Control of Time Series Data
docs.text:
Performing quality control on observed data is a critical step in all FFRD applications as the use of incorrect or poor-quality observed data may result in inaccurate results. An excellent summarization of quality assurance (QA)/quality control (QC) processes which must be undertaken during flood hazard studies is contained within the Hydrologic Hazard Methodology for Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessments: An Inflow Volume-Based Approach to Estimating Stage-Frequency Curves for Dams. The following text was summarized from that document.

In cases where systematic data is obtained for streamflow gages, information on manual measurements of streamflow and gage height, including indirect measurement, can be obtained from the USGS at http://waterdata.usgs.gov/nwis/measurements. These measurements can then be used to develop streamflow rating curves, supplement and/or verify the accuracy of the automatically recorded estimates at gages, as well as to compute streamflow based on gage height. These are valuable for flood frequency studies to aid hydrologists in understanding how the largest flood estimates are made (such as indirect measurements), and in estimating uncertainty. See details on streamflow measurement and computation and discharge ratings from the USGS at https://water.usgs.gov/osw/furnished_records/technical_procedures.html. At most large dams, reservoir inflow is indirectly calculated using measurements of reservoir storage (via stage) and outflow (via rating curves or gage measurements). The water management entity that owns/operates the reservoir in question typically utilizes QA/QC steps on the calculated reservoir inflows, so the data may be used as provided after a cursory review is made to check for any obvious errors.

In addition to potential measurement errors, many datasets have missing data, duplicate values, erroneous data, or other issues that can impact the inflow volume- and stage-frequency assessments. Plotting the flow and stage time series data can assist with identifying any potential errors.

Chapter II-1, Reservoir and River Stage Exceedance Probabilities, from Best Practices (U.S. Bureau of Reclamation and U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, 2015) provides a useful example data set showing daily average reservoir stages for a dam as presented in Figure 1. The plot reveals several potential data quality issues. The time periods associated with the initial reservoir filling, the dam safety emergency, and the pool restriction for interim risk reduction may not be representative of normal operation. Some of the data also appears to be missing and incorrect based on a visual inspection of the plot. Missing data should be filled and flawed data should be corrected or removed, as deemed appropriate.

Figure 1. Example of Daily Average Reservoir Stage Data

docs.title: Produce Probabilistic Summaries
docs.text: Probabilistic summaries of time series data will be used to ascertain the accuracy of the modeling tools and techniques within Calibration, Conformance, and Production Phases.

docs.title: Determine Regulated/Unregulated Time Windows
docs.text:
Prior to extracting any probabilistic results, homogenous time windows for each stream gage must be identified. This must be done in order to avoid violating the independent and identically distributed assumption and align with FFRD modeled outputs. When there is a known source of regulation upstream of a location of interest, but the extent of the impacts is uncertain, further investigation is required. An excellent summarization of steps that can be taken to ascertain the degree of regulation is contained within the technical report titled, Estimating Flood Hazard for Dams and Levees with Upstream Regulation.

For all locations, consider changes in land use, etc. that may impact the homogeneity of the observed data. Also, for locations that are subject to upstream regulation, consider changes in regulation from upstream storage and diversion projects that may impact the homogeneity of the time series (i.e., not all periods of regulation should be considered significant).

docs.title: Annual Maximum Series
docs.text: Commonly, annual maximum series (AMS) of flow and stage/elevation will be available at most (if not all) USGS gages within the modeling domain. However, these AMS are typically indicative of an instantaneous peak duration (i.e., 1-hour). While the instantaneous peak duration is adequate for use when comparing observed and computed stage/elevation data, additional durations for flow data are needed. Thus, for all stream gages within the modeling domain, flow AMS must be extracted from the observed data for 1-day, 2-days, and 3-days.

docs.title: Plotting Positions and Confidence Limits
docs.text:
For all stream gages within the modeling domain, 5- and 95-percent confidence limits (i.e., 90 percent confidence interval) must be computed for both flow and stage/elevation for homogenous time periods.

Confidence intervals for all homogenous flow AMS should be computed using one of the following approaches:

  • For unregulated locations and time periods, use either multiple Bulletin 17 (i.e., one analysis per duration) and/or a single volume frequency analysis to compute confidence intervals using Bulletin 17C procedures. Examples demonstrating these capabilities can be found here: Bulletin 17C | Expected Moments Algorithm Examples.

  • For regulated locations and time periods, use a general frequency analysis to graphically fit an empirical distribution and estimate confidence intervals using order statistics. Examples demonstrating these capabilities can be found here:

  • Create and Parameterize a General Frequency Analysis

  • General Frequency – Graphical Analysis

Confidence intervals for all homogenous stage/elevation AMS should be estimated by first graphically fitting an empirical distribution and then computing using order statistics. Examples demonstrating these capabilities can be found here:

  • Create and Parameterize a General Frequency Analysis

  • Example 7. Analyzing Stage Data

docs.title: Format for Delivery
docs.text:
Observed data should be provided in HEC DSS format for efficient usage by subsequent modeling applications. Probabilistic results must be provided in both .DSS and Comma-Separated Values (.csv) format for use within the Calibration, Conformance, and Production Phases. The prescribed naming convention laid out within the FFRD SOP must be utilized.

Authoritative observed data will be posted to the basin-data/observed-data central directory for the project at the end of the Scoping and Data Preparation phase.

docs.title: Documentation of Data Collection Efforts
docs.text:
All data collection efforts must be summarized and provided to the modeling team and included in the report for the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase.

Entries for observed data will be included in the Data Manifest.

docs.title: Job Aid 5—Observed Gridded Data Preparation
docs.text: This document describes the processes to develop the authoritative set of observed gridded data for the study basin for use in an FFRD project. This data is collected, processed, quality control (QC) checked, and delivered to the project directory during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase of the FFRD project.

docs.title: Tools available
docs.text:
Data tools for processing the gridded datasets include the following:

  • HEC Vortex

  • HEC-DSSVue

  • HEC-SSP

  • QGIS or ArcGIS Pro

docs.title: Data to be Delivered
docs.text:
The observed gridded datasets in the following subsections are to be prepared for the FFRD project. These datasets include available observed gridded data that occurred over the study basin (which is separate from gridded storm catalog data for the larger region, developed separately). The spatial extent of these datasets should be buffered beyond the study basin extent to ensure no data is missing along the edge of the boundary and that the data can accommodate slight adjustments to the study basin boundary if they occur later in the project.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > observed-data

docs.title: Period of Record for Study Basin Extent
docs.text:
Gridded datasets for the full continuous period of record (POR) of precipitation, temperature, and snow are acquired, adjusted, and delivered to the project directory in DSS format.

Processes for acquisition and considerations for adjustments and QC of the data sets are outlined in sections below.

docs.title: Historic Calibration Events
docs.text:
Gridded precipitation, temperature, and snow datasets clipped to the historic event time windows are clipped from the POR data for use in model event-based calibration efforts and delivered to the project directory in DSS format.

The time windows for the events will be defined according to the guidance for calibration event selection provided in Job Aid 01, Scoping Processes. Observed gridded datasets should be buffered both before and after the selected events by at least 1 week. For example, if an historic event is expected to span 15 January 1996 through 15 February 1996, create DSS files containing gridded precipitation, temperature, and snow data that spans 01 January 1996 through 28 February 1996 (i.e., buffered in time by a little bit to avoid reprocessing if the event window is changed).

docs.title: Observed Precipitation Annual Maximum Series
docs.text:
During the scoping and data preparation phase of the project, annual maximum series (AMS) of observed sub-basin averaged precipitation will be created and delivered. The precipitation AMS will be created based on the final gridded dataset after all QC and normalization adjustments.

Using the uncalibrated HEC-HMS model and the POR precipitation data, a precipitation-only period simulation can provide the data to extract the AMS. All hydrologic processes are turned off in the model, except for discretization. AMS for subbasin average, observed precipitation for 1-hour, 24-hour, and 72-hour increments will be calculated and extracted in HEC-DSSVue for the POR simulation. Results will be plotted and assessed for possible input data problems. Reference the Generating Subbasin Averaged Precipitation AMS With HEC-HMS Guide for guidance on this process.

docs.title: Observed Snow Water Equivalent Annual Maximum Series
docs.text:
During the scoping and data preparation phase of the project, the AMS of observed sub-basin averaged snow water equivalent (SWE) will be created and delivered. The SWE AMS will be created based on the final gridded dataset after all QC and normalization adjustments.

Extract subbasin-averaged time series using the Grid-to-Point tool within the HEC-Vortex utility in HEC-HMS. Reference the Grid to Point Converter Tutorial for guidance on this process. This should be done separately for both Analysis of Record for Calibration (AORC) and Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) gridded precipitation datasets as the comparison of the two can be a helpful resource during the QC process. The Mean statistic should be selected to get a single observed value averaged over the spatial domain of each subbasin.

With the hourly and/or daily subbasin-averaged time series, compute the n-day duration precipitation accumulations time series (e.g., 1-, 2-, and 3-day durations) using either HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue. Reference the Data Filtering, Extracting an n-Day Duration Time Series Guide for guidance on this process. Finally, compute an AMS of precipitation and SWE using either HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue. The deliverables from this process are separate DSS files for each dataset containing the subbasin-averaged precipitation and SWE AMS to provide to the HEC-HMS modeler(s).

docs.title: Observed Liquid Water at Soil Surface Annual Maximum Series
docs.text:
During the calibration phase of the FFRD project after the POR HEC-HMS model has been calibrated and complete, the observed AMS of observed sub-basin averaged liquid water at the soil surface (LWASS) will be created and delivered.

The Grid-to-Point tool within the HEC-Vortex utility in HEC-HMS is used to extract the subbasin average time series of LWASS from the POR simulation results. Reference the Grid to Point Converter Tutorial for guidance on this process. The Mean statistic should be selected to get a single observed value averaged over the spatial domain of each subbasin.

With the hourly subbasin-averaged time-series, compute the n-day duration LWASS accumulations time series (e.g., 1-, 2-, and 3-day durations) using either HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue. Reference the Data Filtering, Extracting an n-Day Duration Time Series Guide for guidance on this process. Finally, compute an AMS of LWASS using either HEC-SSP or HEC-DSSVue. Reference the Data Filtering, Extracting an Annual Maximum Series Guide for guidance on this process.

docs.title: Spatial Projection and Naming Conventions
docs.text: The spatial projection and naming conventions for these datasets must conform to the standards described in other sections of the FFRD SOP.

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
The primary source for short-interval gridded precipitation and air temperature is the National Oceanic Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) AORC dataset. The AORC dataset contains one-hour accumulated surface precipitation and the temperature data is comprised of instantaneous 2-meter above ground air temperature. Both precipitation and air temperature are reported at the top of the hour.

Another source for gridded precipitation data is the Oregan State University PRISM dataset. This includes daily, monthly, and long-term averages for precipitation and is typically used if data normalization is deemed necessary.

SWE data is available from the University of Arizona and is based on assimilated Snow Telemetry (SNOTEL) and PRISM data.

docs.title: Raw Gridded Data Acquisition
docs.text:
Gridded datasets are imported and converted to a DSS file using HEC-Vortex utility. Raw datasets should be clipped to the buffered modeling domain during the import process. This creates a DSS file containing gridded precipitation, temperature, and snow data for 1979 through the present. This set of files will be used within the HEC-HMS POR simulations as well as Stochastic Storm Transposition (SST) processing. An overview of the this process is provided in the Gridded Data Import Guide.

All available datasets should be acquired as inputs the assessment of the need for normalization described in sections below. Only the recommended final (adjusted as needed) gridded datasets for use as observed data for the FFRD project are required for delivery to the shared project directory.

docs.title: Normalization of Gridded Precipitation Data for Study basin
docs.text:
The need for normalization of raw observed gridded precipitation data over the study must be assessed. If large discrepancies in precipitation volumes between datasets exist, normalization may be required.

To assess the need for normalization, the difference in total mass of precipitation over the POR for the AORC and PRISM datasets are compared. If a large total volume of precipitation difference exists, the AORC data should be normalized based on PRISIM dataset. A 50 percent volume difference can be used as a guideline to trigger a need to normalize the data.

docs.title: Compute Mass of Precipitation
docs.text: For AORC and PRISM datasets, extract subbasin-averaged time series using the Grid-to-Point tool within the HEC-Vortex utility in HEC-HMS. Reference the Grid to Point Converter Tutorial for guidance on this process. The Mean statistic should be selected to get a single observed value averaged over the spatial domain of each subbasin. Using the Tools | Math Functions | Accumulation Operator in HEC-DSSVue, create accumulated mass curves for each of the precipitation time-series datasets that were extracted for each subbasin.

docs.title: Normalization of Precipitation Data
docs.text:
When the criteria for normalization of the observed data is met, proceed with normalizing the POR AORC dataset to correct the volume so that it aligns with the PRISM dataset. Note the time-window and normalization interval to best target the sections of the dataset with suspected data inaccuracies. Reference the Normalizing Precipitation Data Tutorial for guidance on this process. Produce a new DSS file containing normalized AORC precipitation dataset and provide to the HEC-HMS modeler(s).

Because the boundary condition data is the basis for future SST modeling, any normalization of precipitation data would be needed for the observed data in the study basin but also across the entire SST domain for consistency and the storm catalog from the transposition region for the FFRD study must also be normalized (discussed in other areas of the SOP).

docs.title: Quality Control
docs.text:
QC is an important step before beginning the modeling for both historic event simulation and POR simulations. The gridded datasets should be inspected for errors including but not limited to holes in the grids, missing time steps, far outlying values, time-shifted data, and mismatched values and units. The quality checks safeguard against missing or erroneous data use in the model calibration process. Quality checks also prevent potentially time-consuming model simulation errors caused by erroneous meteorologic data.

Use the Image-Exporter tool in the HEC-Vortex utility to convert a DSS record into a *.tiff raster that can be inspected in QGIS or ArcGIS Pro to check for missing data. Next, the HEC-Vortex Sanitizer tool can be used to explicitly address missing data, screen for minimum/maximum values, and fix DSS part names, amongst others. If there is a shift needed with the time and date of gridded records or a discrepancy in the start time (Part D) or end time (Part E) of a record, the HEC-Vortex Time-Shifter tool can be used to make adjustments to the time of records. For unit conversions, the HEC-Vortex Calculator tool can perform simple operations on all records in the DSS file. Reference the HEC-Vortex Utility Guide for additional details and guidance on each of these tools.

docs.title: Job Aid 6—Standard Naming Conventions
docs.text:
For successful integration of the models, all models and directories must use consistent naming conventions. This Job Aid defines the standard naming conventions for watershed level model data as well as for each constituent model. The more detailed naming conventions of folders and key files in the standard directory structure is provided in the FFRD demo directory, which is referred to in Job Aid 07, Model Library Use.

A comprehensive naming convention table will be created during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase and provided to modelers prior to the start of the Calibration Phase and will follow the requirements and guidelines in the sections below. This table will reinforce full-basin consistency, avoid duplication, and serve as the key reference for model linking that will occur during the Conformance Phase.

docs.title: Global Naming Convention Requirements
docs.text:
• Naming conventions will use only valid American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII) characters.

  • Uppercase characters will never be used (i.e. use all lower-case characters).

  • Spaces will never be used.

  • Hyphens (-) will be used for separating words to improve human readability.

  • Field Delimiters: Underscores (_) will be used as a universal field delimiter to separate individual fields contained within folder names, file names, and model element names.

  • Pre-defined formulas for the sequence of fields in the names for the vast majority of model elements are defined in the sections below and shall be followed.

docs.title: General Structure of Names
docs.text:
Rules for naming conventions are outlined in the sections that follow. For each object included, a formula is provided to instruct users on the proper naming in a format that clearly and consistently defines the fields included in each name.

The rules are presented using the following terminology:

  • fields (separable parts of the name)

  • delimiters (the character used to separate individual fields)

  • formulas (derivation of the object names build from fields and delimiters)

  • examples.

Literal text that does not vary is shown in the formulas in between single quotations (‘literal-text’). In some cases, an optional field is included in formulas to account for edge cases and are included in square brackets.

Tables 1 and 2 include terms, abbreviations, and delimiters that are referenced in the detailed section that follows.

Table 1. Commonly Used Terms in the Creation of Names for Files and Model Elements (sheet 1 of 3)

Name/ Abbreviation Description Options/Examples Archive-date Date appended for files, models or data that has been superseded and retained for reference. 20210115 base Indicator of modeled baseflow that is produced by the hydrologic model and input as a boundary condition to the hydraulic model basin-name Name of overall study basin big-horn trinity bc Indicator of a boundary condition in the hydraulic model. event-name Name of the event. For historic event used for calibration or validation, format is based on the month and year when it occurred (mmmYYYY). For Continuous simulations descriptive text for the time span is used. For synthetic events or events created for stress testing, other format may be used. apr1996 2x-apr1996 por wy2009to2011 pmf sst event-start-date Start date of simulation in UTC time. Format: YYYYMMDD 20210203 data-interval Field describing the time interval used for hydrologic time series in the hydrologic model. 1hr 15min 1day data-type Type of observed gridded data precip (precipitation) temp (temperature) swe (snow water equivalent) cc (cold content) ds Downstream dem Digital elevation model (DEM) gage-id ID assigned by gage operator. 12105900 (USGS) 8594900 (NOAA) gage-operator Operator of the observed data gage usgs noaa usace hms-project-use Descriptor for HMS project filenames for use when multiple hms projects are created. calibration continuous por synthetic hw Headwater

Table 1. Commonly Used Terms in the Creation of Names for Files and Model Elements (sheet 2 of 3)

Name/ Abbreviation Description Options/Examples event-type Type of hydraulic simulation. calibration validation stress-test breach synthetic hydro-enforced Indicates that DEM is hydro-enforced to remove artificial impediments to stream flow such as roadways with culverts or bridges. ic Indicator of an initial condition point in the hydraulic model lb Left Bank merged Indicator that a HEC-RAS Terrain has been exported into a single raster such that it does not include RASMapper formatted terrain modifications. model-unit

Name of the hydraulic modeling unit.

Due to character length limitations in certain software, it is necessary that each model-unit string be less than 14 characters.

nid National Inventory of Dams nid-id The unique identifier of a dam in the National Inventory of Dams. tx05966 dam-outlet-descriptor Short descriptor of a specific outlet from a reservoir. spill gates rim nld National Levee Database nld-system-id The unique identifier of a levee system in the National Levee Database 505000356 nld-segment-id The unique identifier of a levee segment in the National Levee Database 5604350001 nsi National Structure Inventory nsi-version-year Versioning of the National Structure Inventory (NSI) dataset. price-level Price versioning used for the NSI. other-structure-id A field in the National Inventory of Dams that is used when a single dam includes multiple separable structures. other-type-description Description of structure type used for unique 2D Connections that are not associated with dams or levees bridge railroad diversion roadway

Table 1. Commonly Used Terms in the Creation of Names for Files and Model Elements (sheet 3 of 3)

Name/ Abbreviation Description Options/Examples pt-purpose Indicator used for hydraulic model Reference Points to convey the purpose of the point (specific to levees). us (upstream) ms (midstream) ds (downstream) br-hw (breach headwater) br-tw (breach tailwater) por Period of Record rb Right Bank reference-name Short names useful for referencing features to provide an alternative to numeric ID’s or provide additional context. May be abbreviations of long names. lost-creek trinity-river white-rock trinity-bay resolution Resolution description of a DEM or Terrain. 4ft 12ft 100ft storm-duration Duration of storm from database of events for a given project / duration. Format: 00hr 72hr storm-rank Rank of storm from database of events for a given project / duration. Format: r001 r026 storm-type Type of storm as described in section 5. st2 st3 structure-source Source of levee, dam, or other structure data. nld nid unique-id Unique ID used for HMS model elements. Usually paired with an identifier of the element type (s for subbasin, j for junction, r for reach). See naming formulas in the section for application. Increasing values from downstream to upstream. 010 020 030

docs.title: Basin-Name Examples
docs.text:
Since the FFRD work is based on the Hydrologic Unit Code (HUC)-4 level of the National Hydrography Dataset, or subregions, the basin-names referred to within this report generally relate to the subregion names. All delimiters in basin names shall be dashes. No periods, apostrophes, commas, or other symbols shall be used. Many HUC-4 basins are named with a dash to separate different major rivers within the basin. For this convention those dashes will remain, but all spaces will also be substituted with dashes. Table 2 lists example HUC-4 basins and how they shall be named to follow this convention.

Table 2. Example Hydrologic Unit Code 4 Basin Naming

HUC-4 Subregion Code HUC-4 Subregion Name FFRD basin-name 0702 St. Croix-Trout-Kettle st-croix-trout-kettle  1302 Middle Rio Grande middle-rio-grande 1703 Columbia-Lower Snake columbia-lower-snake 0307 Altamaha-St. Marys altamaha-st-marys

docs.title: Project Repository Name
docs.text:
The FFRD watershed study is organized within the Model Library within a repository named using the FFRD descriptor followed by the basin name text. The basin name will typically match the HUC-4 name in the Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD).

  • formula: ‘ffrd’ _ basin-name

  • examples: ffrd_big-horn, ffrd_trinity

docs.title: Digital Elevation Model Names
docs.text:
The base DEMs created during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase will be named according to the formulas below. Which formula to use is dependent on whether the delivery is provided as a single DEM for the full study basin or broken out into smaller pieces according to the hydraulic model units.

The naming convention for full study basin DEMs provided for the entire basin will follow the formula below.

  • Formula: ‘dem’ _ basin-name _ resolution [ _ ‘hydro-enforced’]

  • Example: dem_big-horn_30m, dem_big-horn_1m

The naming convention for the DEM produced for individual hydraulic model units follows the formula below.

  • Formula: ‘dem’ _ model-unit _ resolution [ _ ‘hydro-enforced’]

  • Example: dem_west-fork-creek_1m, dem_east-fork-creek_1m

docs.title: RAS Mapper Terrain Names
docs.text:
The terrains created by RAS Mapper produced for each model unit will follow the formula blow.

  • Formula: terrain _ model-unit _ resolution

  • Example: terrain_cedar-creek _4ft

docs.title: Storm Names
docs.text:
Each storm in the storm catalog is stored in an individual HEC-DSS file, which is named based on the storm start date, the duration, data type, storm type, and the total cumulative precipitation ranking of the storm.

Note

r is used in place of t, which is commonly used as a parser for time in ISO formats.

  • formula: event-start-date _ storm-duration _ storm-type _ storm-rank

  • example: 19760102_72hr_ st4_r001

docs.title: Storm DSS Path Names
docs.text:
HEC-DSS files use a path name internally to organize data in a tidy way. There are some required path name parts and some free-form parts to a valid .DSS path. A path has six parts labeled A through F. The C, D, and E parts have strict requirements for grid data. A and B are free form but have a small character limit. F is usually the catch-all path part due to its longer character limit.

The convention for the storms catalog data is as follows:

/\$CRS\$resolution/\$basin/PRECIPITATION/\$start-date/\$end-date/\$data-source/

  • A part: The Coordinate Reference System (CRS) should be SHG for the Standard Hydrologic Grid. The resolution should match the source precipitation data resolution (e.g., 4K for 4-kilometer resolution).

  • B part: Use the same short basin name that is used in the file name.

  • C part: For gridded precipitation, always use PRECIPITATION.

  • D part: For gridded data, this part indicates the data’s recording start time in ddBBBYYYY:HHMM format, where BBB is the first three letters of the month name, and dd is a zero-padded date.

  • Each 1-hour precipitation grid has its own path name. A storm .DSS file will contain 72 path names. The D part of the first path will match the start date in the filename.
  • E part: For gridded data, this part indicates the data’s recording end time in the same format as the D part. For hourly data, this should be one hour later than the D part.
  • The E part of the last path will match the end date in the filename.
  • F part: indicate the source of the precipitation data (e.g., AORC).

An example of a valid .dss path is below.

/SHG4K/TRINITY/PRECIPITATION/06OCT1982:0000/06OCT1982:0100/AORC/

docs.title: Project Name
docs.text:
The HEC-HMS project name is based on the basin name. In cases where multiple hydrologic models are created for a single study, an optional field may be added using an integer starting with 1, for each model created. Project names and titles must be identical.

  • formula: basin-name [ _ integer ]

  • examples:

  • trinity

  • rock-river_1

  • rock-river_2

docs.title: Project Components
docs.text: An HEC-HMS project is defined using several files which contain information pertaining to the land surface processes/parameters, meteorologic conditions, computational settings, and simulations.

docs.title: Basin Models
docs.text:
Basin Models must be named using the historic event time period and/or purpose.

  • formula: event-name

  • examples:

  • apr-1997

  • por

  • sst

docs.title: Meteorologic Models
docs.text:
Meteorologic Models must be named using the historic event time period and/or purpose.

  • formula: event-name

  • examples:

  • apr-1997

  • por

  • sst

docs.title: Control Specifications
docs.text:
Control Specifications must be named using the historic event time period and/or purpose.

  • formula: event-name

  • examples:

  • apr-1997

  • por

  • sst

docs.title: Computes
docs.text:
Computes (i.e., simulation runs, forecast alternatives, etc.) must be named using the historic event time period and/or purpose.

  • formula: event-name

  • examples:

  • apr-1997

  • por

  • sst

docs.title: Shared Component Data
docs.text: Hydrologic simulation requires a wealth of data that is used as initial conditions, boundary conditions, or parameters. Time series, paired data, gridded, and terrain are all examples of shared component data.

docs.title: Time-Series Data
docs.text:
Time series data in the hydrologic model must be named to match the location and time step of the data.

  • formula: location _ time-step

  • examples:

  • trinity-river-at-dallas_1-day

  • bridgeport-lake-inflow_1-hour

  • shasta-lake-elevation_1-hour

  • sayers-dam-outflow_15-min

docs.title: Paired Data
docs.text:
Paired data in the hydrologic model must be named to match the location and type of data.

  • formula: location _ data-type

  • examples:

  • big-bear-lake_storage-outflow

  • big-bear-lake_elevation-storage

  • delaware-rv_r020_storage-outflow

  • wb-susquehanna-rv_r150_xs

docs.title: Grid Data
docs.text:
