Gridded data in the hydrologic model that is being used during the calibration phase must be named to match the data source and historic event time period.

  • formula: data-source _ data-type

  • example: aorc_apr-1990

docs.title: Hydrologic Terrain
docs.text:
Terrain data in the hydrologic model that is being used during the calibration phase will be sourced directly from the 30-meter resolution DEM created during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase and the name will not be altered when loaded in the HEC-HMS project.

  • formula: data-source _ horizontal-resolution

  • example: ned-30m

docs.title: Basin Elements
docs.text: Basin elements consist of subbasins, reaches, reservoirs, junctions, diversions, sources, and sinks. These elements are used to describe the physical properties of the watershed and the topology of the stream network. Basin elements are organized within basin models.

docs.title: Subbasins
docs.text:
Subbasins must be named using a reference name following the HEC-HMS convention, suffixed with an s, followed by the HEC-HMS unique element ID. The unique ID for subbasins will follow a numbering system that increases from downstream to upstream, starting at 010 and incrementing by 10 (e.g., 010, 020, 030, etc.).

When a subbasin element is used as a comparison to observed data at a gage, the description field in the model should be used to document the authoritative name of the gage.

  • formula: reference-name _ ‘s’ unique-id

  • example: white-rock-ck_s040

docs.title: Reaches
docs.text:
Reaches must be named using a reference name following the HMS Convention, suffixed with an r, followed by the HMS unique element ID. The unique ID for reaches will follow a numbering system that increases from downstream to upstream, starting at 010 and incrementing by 10 (e.g., 010, 020, 030, etc.). When multiple reaches are used between two junctions, numbers should be incremented by 1 (e.g., 010, 011, etc.).

When a reach element is used as a comparison to observed data at a gage, the description field in the model should be used to document the authoritative name of the gage.

  • formula: reference-name _ ‘r’ unique-id

  • example: trinity-rv_r070

docs.title: Junctions
docs.text:
Junctions are complex and require special consideration. In general, junctions should be named using a reference name following the HEC-HMS convention, suffixed with a j and the HEC-HMS unique element ID. In cases when the junction is directly associated with the outlet of a subbasin, the same unique ID should be used for both the subbasin and junction. This will work in many cases where unnamed tributaries join main stems of larger water bodies. In cases where larger water bodies connect, the plus sign can be used to identify the confluence using a second optional reference name. For cases where streams and rivers flow into reservoirs, in should be used as the second reference name. Likewise, junctions that represent outflow from a reservoir should use out as the second reference name.

When a junction element is used as a comparison to observed data at a gage, the description field in the model should be used to document the authoritative name of the gage.

  • Primary delimiter: underscore (universal for all FFRD models and filenames)

  • Secondary delimiter: Optional plus-sign for additional reference names

  • formula: reference-name [+ reference-name] _ ‘j’ unique-id

  • examples:

  • west-fork_j090

  • elm-fork+clear-ck_j050

  • nid_tx05966_in_j070

  • nid_tx05966_out_j010

docs.title: Reservoirs
docs.text:
Reservoirs will be named using ‘nid’ followed by National Inventory of Dams Identifier (NID ID). the name may also include the human-readable dam name and “Other Structure ID” from the NID as the following fields. These fields are sourced from the NID. Note that guidance for modeling methods for individual dams will be created as part of the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase.

The reservoir description field will include the authoritative NID “Name” field.

  • formula: ‘nid’ _ nid-id [ _ dam-name ] [ _ other-structure-id ]

  • examples:

  • nid_tx05966

  • nid_mn00584_springfield-dam_s002

  • nid_mn00584_springfield-dam

docs.title: Diversions
docs.text:
Diversions must be named using a reference name following the HMS convention, suffixed with a d, followed by the HEC-HMS unique element ID

  • formula: reference-name _ ‘d’ unique-id

  • example: lost-creek-diversion_d001

docs.title: Sinks
docs.text:
Sinks must be named using a reference name following the HEC-HMS convention.

  • formula: reference-name

  • example: trinity-bay

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
Sources must be named using a reference name following the HEC-HMS convention and prefixed with source.

  • formula: ‘source’ _ reference-name

  • example: source_trinity-bay

Figure 1. Hydrologic Model Calibration Phase Element Naming Convention Example for HEC-HMS Basin Model.

docs.title: Watershed Name
docs.text:
The HEC-ResSim watershed name is also the name of the directory containing all elements of the model—it is essentially the model name. When creating a new HEC-ResSim model for this FFRD work, it shall be named according to the following:

  • formula: “ffrd_”+basin-name

  • examples:

  • Trinity River Basin = “ffrd_trinity”

  • Rock River Basin = “ffrd_rock-river”

  • Altamaha-St. Marys = “ffrd_altamaha-st-marys”

  • Rio Grande-Elephant Butte = “rio-grande-elephant-butte”

If an existing watershed is used, the watershed name can be changed within the file system by changing three items in coordination while HEC-ResSim is not running: the main directory name, the project file (.wksp) name, and the first line of the .wksp file.

docs.title: Alternative Names
docs.text:
HEC-ResSim alternative names are limited to only ten characters, so the naming must be succinct.

For the Calibration Phase modeling, several alternatives will be created, including the guide curve operations, the pulse flow test, and the calibration events. Each alternative shall include an informative description. The calibration events shall be named using the following formula:

  • Formula: [first letter of the month during which the event begins]-[4-digit year]

  • Examples:

  • April 1990 = “a-1990”

  • October-November 2015 = “o-2015” or “o-n-2015”

For the Conformance/Production Phase models, the HEC-ResSim alternative name will use a standard name:

  • ‘fema_ffrd’

docs.title: Run Names
docs.text:
The modeler does not create run names, but scripting with HEC-ResSim and HEC-ResSim output uses run names that are based on the ten-character alternative name appended with a zero for normal runs. HEC-ResSim has the option to create trial versions of runs, which are numbered sequentially, however trials shall not be used in the FFRD modeling. If the alternative name is fewer than ten characters, HEC-ResSim appends dashes until it gets to ten characters before appending the final zero character.

  • Examples: “a-1990----0”, “o-2015----0”, “o-n-2015--0"

docs.title: Simulation Names
docs.text:
Every simulation shall have a short description to indicate its purpose. For example, “Calibration Event for Apr-May 1990”, “conservation of mass test run”, or “FEMA Future of Flood Risk Data cloud compute simulation”.

For the simulations of calibration events in the Calibration Phase model, the HEC-ResSim simulation name will follow the following formula:

  • Formula: “c_” alternative name (the “c” stands for calibration)

  • Examples: “c_a-1990”, “c_o-2015”, “c_o-n-2015”

For the Conformance/Production Phase models, the HEC-ResSim simulation name will use a standard name:

  • ‘fema_ffrd’

docs.title: Reservoir Names
docs.text:
Where possible, reservoir names will directly correspond with the reservoir element names applied in the hydrologic model, using the description and formula provided in Section 1.6. If a large, complex pre-existing model is used for the study, the level of effort required to rename the reservoirs is not justified, and instead a lookup table shall be provided in .json form within the main watershed directory.

The reservoir description field shall always include the authoritative NID Name field associated with the primary dam on the pool. The description shall also include any alternate, local, or familiar names used to reference the reservoir and dam.

  • examples: nid_tx05966

docs.title: Dam Names
docs.text: Dams are sub-elements of reservoirs, and their names will matter in watersheds that have reservoirs with diverted outlets or multiple dams. Name these sub-elements “dam”, or in cases with multiple dams at a reservoir, use append dam with a short descriptor, e.g., “dam-aux”. This descriptor is referred to in other sections of this document as ‘dam-outlet-descriptor’.

docs.title: Project File Name
docs.text:
The modeling unit name should be based on the largest stream in the model unit and will commonly match a HUC name that may approximately be coincident with the model unit. The model unit will be defined during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase.

  • formula: model-unit

  • example: lower-new

docs.title: Project Description Field
docs.text:
All models created for FFRD will include a thorough description field providing users with pertinent information. The following items are listed in the HEC-RAS model description field with each item on a new line using a colon to separate the item name and response:

  • Program and Version: HEC-RAS 6.X.X September 20XX

  • Status of Model: Interim Review, Final Review, Final Draft, or Final

  • Primary Basin: Primary Basin Name

  • Modeling Unit: Modeling Unit Name

  • Date of last modification: MM/DD/YYY

  • Model Developed by: Company/Agency Name

  • Vertical datum and units: NAVD 88, feet

  • Horizontal datum and units: NAD 1983, feet

  • Horizontal Projection: USA_Contiguous_Albers_Equal_Area_Conic_USGS_version

docs.title: Geometry Name
docs.text:
All models utilize a single geometry named using the modeling unit name (identical to the geometry).

  • formula: model-unit

  • example: lower-new

docs.title: Unsteady Flow Name
docs.text:
The flow file for Calibration Phase modeling efforts will be named to match the project name, with a filename extension in the form of .u0X. The name of each unsteady flow event will be named according to the calibration event name of the event. The format for historic unsteady flow names for historic events is mmmYYYY. If historic events are increased with a multiplier for the purposes of a stress test the field is prefixed with a #x-mmmYYY format and considered a descriptive name.

  • formula: event-name

  • examples: apr1997, 2x-apr1997, sst

docs.title: Plan Name
docs.text:
The plan file name for Calibration Phase modeling efforts will be named to match the project name, with a filename extension in the form of .p0X. The name of each plan will be named based on the scenario type and the calibration event name of the event. The plan file name for conformance/production phase models will be a fixed name, “sst”.

Calibration Phase

  • formula: event-type _ event-name

  • examples:

  • calibration_apr1997

  • validation_jun1988

  • breach_apr1997

  • stress-test_2x-apr1997

    Conformance and Production Phase

  • formula: event-type (fixed)

  • sst

docs.title: Plan Short ID
docs.text: The short plan name must be identical to the plan name.

docs.title: Terrain Names
docs.text:
The hydraulic model terrains are created using RASMapper software using the full resolution DEM created during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase as the basis. The terrains will be named using the model unit and resolution as in the formula below. Note that the formula includes an optional field to indicate terrains that have been exported into a single raster and include no RASMapper based terrain modifications (i.e. ‘merged’). This name is the file name for the .hdf and .vrt files. No standard is set for the underlying .tif files that make up the terrain, but human readability should be considered.

  • Formula: model-unit _ ‘terrain’ _ resolution [ _ ‘merged’ ]

  • Examples:

  • little-fork-creek_terrain_1m

  • little-fork-creek_terrain_3m_merged

docs.title: Two-Dimensional Flow Area Element Names
docs.text:
Two-dimensional (2D) flow area names will be identical to the project file name established in the naming convention table and used directly. Optionally, names may include an integer field, starting with 1, to distinguish when multiple areas are used in the model. If more than one 2D flow area exists in the model, the integer must be used for all elements.

  • formula: model-unit [ _ integer]

  • example: lower-new_1

docs.title: Boundary Condition Names
docs.text: Multiple distinct boundary condition line uses are called for in this SOP, and the naming formulas will vary for each based on the categories below.

docs.title: Inflow Boundary Condition from Upstream Basins
docs.text:
The inflow boundary condition (BC) lines that supply inflow from upstream basins have names that descriptively indicate the link to an upstream basin and are intentionally repeated to be identical to the reference line from the upstream source. An optional integer field may be added so that multiple locations for passing flow between models can be distinguished when needed. The formula below will be followed.

  • formula: model-unit (upstream) _ ‘to’ _ model-unit (downstream) [ _ integer ]

  • examples:

  • mount-creek_to_lower-new

  • baldhill_to_sunny-valley_1

docs.title: Internal Boundary Condition from Subbassin Baseflows
docs.text:
The inflow BC lines that are sourced from HEC-HMS subbasin base flows include an indicator that they are BC lines, the reference name, ‘s’ and unique ID from the HMS subbasin that supplies the flow.

  • formula: ‘bc’ _ reference-name _ ‘s’ unique-id _ ‘base’

  • example: bc_clear-crk_s230_base

docs.title: Internal Boundary Condition for Dam Outflows
docs.text:
The flow hydrograph boundary condition (storage area/2D area connections) that are internal to the HEC-RAS model extent and are sourced from either observed flows, HEC-ResSim simulated outflows, or HEC-HMS simulated outflows are named according to the formula below.

  • formula: ‘nid’ _ nid-id _ [dam-outlet-descriptor]

  • example:

  • nid_tx05966

  • nid_ms02541_spill

  • nid_ms02541_gates

docs.title: Downstream Outflow Boundary Condition
docs.text:
The outflow BC lines include a BC line indicator, the model unit name, an outflow indicator and an optional integer so that multiple outflows can be distinguished if necessary.

  • formula: ‘bc’ _ model-unit _ ‘out’ [ _ integer ]

  • example: bc_lower-new_out_1

docs.title: Excess Precipitation (Meteorological) Boundary Condition
docs.text: No name is applied to Meteorological Data boundary condition in HEC-RAS. In a desktop HEC-RAS model, the path to the source of the data is a simple .dss path link included in the plan file.

docs.title: Initial Condition Point Names
docs.text:
Initial conditions points can be used to set the initial water surface elevations at the start of simulations and will commonly be associated with reservoir pools. When they are used to set an initial pool level, they will be named with an type identifier, a descriptive name for the reservoir and an optional integer. When initial conditions points are used for other purposes, no specific formula is required.

  • formula: ‘ic’ _ ‘nid’ _ nid-id [ _ reservoir-name ] [ _ integer]

  • examples:

  • ic_nid_ms58374

  • ic_nid_tx00776_homme-dam_1

docs.title: Storage Area/Two-Dimensional Connection Names
docs.text: Internal storage area connections are used to represent dams and levees as well as other unique features. The names of these structures will use and indicator of the type of structure followed by a numeric identifier according to the naming formulas and descriptions below. Note that a character limit of 32 applies for all storage area/2D connection names. Additionally, requirements for the description fields are also included for each type of structure below.

docs.title: Dams
docs.text:
Dam names will directly correspond with the element names applied in the hydrologic and/or reservoir operations models, using the description and formula provided in Section 1.6.

The reservoir description field will include the authoritative NID Name field.

  • examples: nid_tx05966, nid_mn00584_s002

docs.title: Levees
docs.text:
2D connections representing levees will be named using the ‘nld’ followed by the National Levee Database System ID (System ID). The name will also include an integer field for cases when the levee system is broken into multiple structures (in order to allow multiple breaches or other reasons). When the integer field is used, it should be applied starting at 1 on the upstream and increasing downstream.

The description field will include the authoritative name of the levee system found in the NLD (NLD System Name) on the first line, and a list of all NLD Segments IDs and NLD Segment Names that make up the system.

  • formula: ‘nld’ _ nld-system-id [ _ integer ]

  • examples: nld_3005000153, nld_505000356_2

  • Description Example:

Prairie du Pont / Fish Lake System

2 Segment System

5604160001 - Fish Lake Drainage & Levee District No. 8

5604350001 - Prairie du Pont Levee and Sanitary District

docs.title: Other Structures
docs.text:
Other types of structures modeled using storage area/2D connections will be named using a ‘conn’ prefix, followed by a suitable ID, followed by a suitable descriptor of the structure type. The description field should be used to document the authoritative name of the structure and other useful information.

  • formula: ‘conn’ _ other-id _ other-type-description

  • examples: conn _1_bridge, conn _4_rr-embank, conn_3_ diversion, conn_10_roadway

docs.title: Reference Line Names
docs.text: Reference lines are used for several distinct purposes. Naming conventions for reference lines for each these purposes are detailed below.

docs.title: Observed Data Gage
docs.text:
Reference lines that capture modeled output at observed stream gage locations must be named with an identifier indicating the purpose of the reference line (‘gage’), an indicator of the gage operator, and the gage operator’s ID. The gage operator ID will be sourced from the authoritative databases (commonly hosted by USGS or NOAA). Lists of the gages and complete observed data will be documented during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase and provided to the model developer. Optionally, the gage name or an abbreviation can be appended to improve human readability.

  • formula: ‘gage’ _ gage-operator _ gage-id [ _ reference-name]

  • examples:

  • gage_usgs_12105900_gr-at-auburn

  • gage_usgs_12105900

  • gage_noaa_8594900

  • gage_noaa_8594900_washington-dc

  • gage_usace_1290183026_green-peter-outflow

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Elements
docs.text:
Reference lines are included in the hydraulic model at all relevant hydrologic model elements such as headwaters subbasin outlets, junctions and reservoirs to allow for direct comparison of modeled results across each model. These reference lines will be named identically to the corresponding hydrologic model element.

  • Formula: see Subsections 1.6.4.1.1, 1.6.4.1.3, and 1.6.4.1.4.

  • Examples:

  • s-grn-50

  • white-rock-ck_s040

  • trinity-river_r040

  • west-fork_j090

  • west-fork_j090

  • elm-fork+clear-ck_j052

  • lewinsville-inflow_j099

  • hhd-reservoir-out_j010

  • trinity-river_r040

  • white-rock-ck_s040

  • nid_tx05966

  • nid_mn00584_s002

docs.title: Outflow Linking Locations
docs.text:
Reference lines will be created to capture outflows from the models that will be passed to downstream models (note that outflow boundary condition lines will not be used for this purpose). The reference lines that pass flow to downstream model units will have names that descriptively indicate the link to the downstream model and are intentionally repeated to be identical to the inflow boundary condition name of the downstream model. An optional ‘integer’ field may be added so that multiple locations for passing flow between models can be distinguished when needed.

  • formula: model-unit (upstream) _ ‘to’ _ model-unit (downstream) [ _ integer ]

  • examples:

  • mount-creek_to_lower-new

  • baldhill_to_sunny-valley_1

  • baldhill_to_sunny-valley_2

docs.title: Other Reference Lines
docs.text:
Reference lines for other purposes should use ‘ref-ln’ prefix followed by a location name.

  • formula: ‘ref-ln’ _ location-name

  • example: ref-ln_sunrise-bridge

docs.title: Reference Point Names
docs.text: Reference points are used in the model to capture water surface elevations at stream gages and loading conditions along levees. They also may be used for other miscellaneous reasons.

docs.title: Observed Data Gage
docs.text:
Reference points will be included at each stream gage location and placed near the river centerline. Note that there is a 32-character limit for reference point names in HEC RAS. The ‘reference-name’ field is optional and may be left out if the character limit does now allow.

  • formula: ‘ref-pt’ _ ‘gage’ _ gage-operator _ gage-id [ _ reference-name]

  • examples:

  • ref-pt_gage_usgs_12105900_gr-at-auburn

docs.title: Levee Reference Points
docs.text:
Reference points will be included along storage area/2D connections at levees located at the upstream end of the levee system, mid-point of the levee system, and downstream end of the levee system. Additionally, and two reference points will be included at each levee breach location (riverside/headwater and landside/tailwater). An integer value should be appended when more than one breach location is included for the levee system.

  • formula: ‘nld’ _ nld-system-id _ pt-purpose [ _ integer ]

  • examples:

  • nld_3005000153_us (Upstream)

  • nld_3005000153_ms (Midstream)

  • nld_3005000153_ds (Downstream)

  • nld_3005000153_br-hw (Breach headwater)

  • nld _3005000153_br-tw (Breach tailwater)

  • nld _5009000634_br-hw_2 (Breach headwater)

docs.title: Other Reference Points
docs.text:
Reference points for other purposes should use ‘ref-pt’ prefix followed by a location name.

  • formula: ‘ref-pt’ _ location-name

  • example: ref-pt_sunrise-bridge

Figure 2. Hydraulic Model Element Naming Convention Example for Boundary Conditions, Storage Area Connections, Reference Lines/Points, and 2D Flow Area.

docs.title: Full Basin Structure Inventory
docs.text:
The structure inventory file for the entire study basin will be delivered in .gpkg format. The naming convention will include hydraulic modeling unit basin identifier, NSI version, and the year of the price level, as shown in the formula below

  • formula:

  • basin-name _ ‘nsi’ _ nsi-version-year _ price-year _ ‘pricelevel‘ [ _archived-date ]

  • examples:

  • : trinity_nsi_2022_2024_pricelevel.gpkg

docs.title: Model Unit Structure Inventory
docs.text:
Structure inventories are also provided for each hydraulic model-unit, which will be named following the formula below.

  • formula:

  • model-unit _ ‘nsi’ _ nsi-version-year _ price-year _ ‘pricelevel‘ [ _archived-date ]

  • examples:

  • little-creek_nsi_2022_2024_pricelevel.gpkg

  • fast-river_nsi_2022_2023_pricelevel_20240612.gpkg

docs.title: Job AId 7—Model Library Use
docs.text: This document defines key details regarding the use of Model Library specifically for the FFRD initiative. The Model Library application allows access to view and interact with datasets stored in the cloud, independent of the specific bucket, and meets all USACE security protocols. The Model Library supports the FFRD program by providing a place for team members to store, share, and review models as well as providing a platform for staging models for cloud compute.

docs.title: Model Library Key Information
docs.text:
The Model Library is a general tool used for projects other than FFRD. Key background information can be found via the links below.

Table 1. Model Library Links

Model Library URL https://model-library.sec.usace.army.mil/ Model Library User’s Manual https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/model-library/ Model Library Support mlhelp@usace.army.mil

docs.title: Current Model Location
docs.text:
FFRD projects have many team members contributing throughout the process and various models and datasets will be updated multiple times throughout their development. Strong control of model versions is critical to avoid potential errors of oversights.

The key tenant of FFRD model data management is that the current version of each model will always remain in a single, stable location.

docs.title: Archive Process for Superseded Model Data
docs.text: To maintain current model data in a stable location, user action to intentionally archive the current model prior to replacing it with updated version is required. Within the ‘calibration’ and ‘conformance’ directories, each model has its own subdirectory. The ‘archive’ directory will store the older models as they become out-of-date. Older models will be moved to the archive directory using the rename action on the subfolder level and entering, “archive\model-name-YYYYMMDD” as the location to move to. This move process is described at the Move a File or Directory, Model Library user’s manual page.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
The full formal ffrd directory structure is documented as a blank template on Model Library via the repository named FFRD-Folder-Structure-Demo. The intended contents of each directory and subdirectory are included as read-me files within this directory.

Figure 1. FFRD Folder Structure Demo Directory

docs.title: User Account Management Actions
docs.text: The following subsections provide common user account management actions for FFRD.

docs.title: Team Members: Gaining Access to Model Library
docs.text:
The process for gaining access to the Model Library is initiated by entering the URL (https://model-library.sec.usace.army.mil/) into a web browser. The general workflow for creating an account is included in the Model Library documentation, which can be accessed from the help button (Figure 2) in the upper right corner of the page.

Figure 2. Model Library Help Button

Once access is gained, legitimate user verification by the Model Library administrators may be necessary. If access has not been granted, inquire with the help desk via email at mlhelp@usace.army.mil.

After new account verification is complete, users will need to be added to the FFRD modeling group in Model Library. Reach out the team lead if access is needed.

docs.title: Technical Lead: Grant Group Access to Team Members
docs.text:
The technical lead will grant access for team members to the project repository. The technical lead must be already added to the “FEMA-FFRD” group and assigned as a group administrator by the Model Library administrators.

To add a team member to the group:

  1. Select the drop-down button on the upper right of the browser

  2. Select “Manage Groups”

  3. Select the FEMA-FFRD card

  4. Select the Select new user drop down button and find team member

  5. Select blue edit button next to new team member

  6. Apply the appropriate permissions (Read and Write for most team members)

  7. Save

  8. Verify user has access via offline communication

If any technical issues or questions arise, support can be attained via email at mlhelp@usace.army.mil.

docs.title: Technical Lead: Create New FFRD Repository
docs.text:
At the beginning of an FFRD project a new repository will be created in the Model Library by the technical lead, and empty folders will be created according to the standard directory structure.

An FFRD demo repository is available in the Model Library to aid in the creation of a new project repository. This demo repository is named, ‘ffrd-folder-structure-demo’ and includes all of the directories and sub directories needed for a new repository. Note that additional basin name specific folders must be added and read-me files are included in the demo directory to guide the format for those folders.

To create a new repository for a new project, follow these steps:

  1. Download the demo directory to your computer

  1. Unzip the downloaded directory

  2. Add custom, basin-specific subdirectories as indicated in the read-me files

  3. Click Add a Model Repository from the Model Library Splash Page

  1. Use the FFRD SOP standard naming for the repository (e.g., ‘ffrd-trinity’)

  2. Set Program field to “FFRD”

  3. Set Repository Group to “FFRD”

  4. Set Repository Location to “FFRD-Computable”

  5. Set Repository Purpose to “Watershed”

  1. Open newly created repository

  2. Click on “Tools – Upload Files”

  3. Drag the local directory from your computer into the upload tray in the browser.

docs.title: Job Aid 8—Dam Representation
docs.text: This job aid describes dam physical and operational model representation specifically for FFRD models. The guidelines for dam and reservoir modeling methods are included in SOP Volume II and the guidelines for scoping of specific methods to apply for each dam in a basin is included in a separate job aid. This job aid document aims to provide the technical detail and processes required for implementation.

docs.title: Coordinated Dam Modeling Approach Across System of Models
docs.text:
Consistency of the model approach across the HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim, and HEC-RAS models for each dam is important for FFRD projects. The SOP utilizes the Dam Model Scoping Table, Model Linking Register, and Name Table to ensure each model can be developed based on coordinated approach.

A common dataset that contains the available physical dam information, reservoir capacity information, and curated historic observed data is provided prior to model development and calibration activities and will be leveraged throughout model development.

docs.title: Initial Pool Elevations
docs.text:
For each storm event, initial pool elevations for reservoirs should be set consistently across each model (HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim, and HEC-RAS). The initial pools should be set according to standard techniques available in users manuals for each software package.

For HEC-RAS models, initial conditions (IC) points are only required for dams scoped for Type A, B, or C modeling. For all HEC-RAS IC points, the fixed during warmup option should be selected so that the pool level at the start of the simulation is consistent with the selected value.

Historic event modeling will leverage any available observed pool data as initial pool elevations. When no observed data is available, estimating the average pool level is acceptable and LiDAR elevations that capture the pool water surface may be used as an approximation.

Stochastic events will vary initial pool levels for each event for certain dams according to the Dam Model Scoping Table. The method for selection pool elevations for stochastic simulations for each event is documented in other sections of the SOP.

docs.title: Reservoir Storage Capacity
docs.text: The reservoir storage capacity for each reservoir is an important aspect of the model representation for each modeled dam and must be consistent across the HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim, and HEC-RAS models. All aspects of this representation are described in the sections below.

docs.title: HEC-HMS Storage Capacity
docs.text: A tabular Elevation-Storage Relationship represents the HEC-HMS storage capacity. It should be input such that is based on the best available data and is consistent with the RAS Mapper Terrain.

docs.title: HEC-ResSim Storage Capacity
docs.text: The HEC-ResSim storage capacity is represented by a tabular Elevation-Storage Relationship. It should be input such that is based on the best available data and is consistent with the RAS Mapper Terrain.

docs.title: HEC-RAS Storage Capacity
docs.text: The HEC-RAS storage capacity is represented by the RAS Mapper Terrain, which must account for underwater bathymetry. The RAS Mapper Terrain is generated by incorporating the best available data into the base terrain through GIS processes. Steps to compare RAS Mapper Terrain-based storage with Tabular estimates applied to HEC-HMS and HEC-RAS are needed to ensure consistency.

docs.title: Storage Capacity Consistency Check
docs.text:
After the RAS Mapper based terrain has incorporated bathymetry to represent the storage, it is critical that the terrain’s elevation-volume relationship is validated against any known elevation-volume data. This could be a published elevation-volume curve, tabular data from historic documents, or National Inventory of Dams (NID) storage values. Elevation-volume curves can be computed for a given area within RAS Mapper or in the geometric schematic editor.

To compute a RAS Mapper terrain-based storage, a separate new geometry should be created for this purpose. A new 1D storage area should be created within this new geometry with a name corresponding to the reservoir of interest. The boundary of this storage area should be close to the perimeter of the water body as indicated by the LiDAR terrain digital elevation model (DEM) (see the note below regarding automatic generation of reservoir outline polygons). This geometry with a storage area over the reservoir needs to be associated with the terrain that has bathymetric terrain modifications applied. Right-clicking the storage area allows for computing and plotting elevation-volume data for the reservoir.

The elevation-volume curve computed by HEC-RAS from the terrain should be compared with available elevation-volume data. The terrain should be modified until there is an appropriate fit between the terrain surface’s volume and available reference data.

The reservoir outline polygon needed to produce the storage capacity table can be created through an automated process. GIS geoprocessing tools, such as Contour, can be used to automatically generate an outline of the reservoir surface (assuming the reservoir is a flat surface in the LiDAR based DEM). If using the contour geoprocess, the base contour can be specified as slightly higher than the LiDAR indicated water surface elevation (e.g., base contour of 0.1 feet higher than LiDAR water surface). A large contour interval can also be used by the contour geoprocess so that only a single contour is created at the desired elevation near the LiDAR water surface. A contour type of contour is appropriate for this purpose (the output polyline feature can then be converted to a polygon using the Feature to Polygon geoprocess). The Processing Extent options of the Geoprocessing Environments can be used to limit the extent where contours are calculated. Some manual cleanup may be required to remove contours which do not correspond to the reservoir of interest. The Simplify Polygon geoprocess with a tolerance of approximately 1 to 2 feet can be used to reduce the number of vertices in a reservoir outline polygon.

docs.title: Storage Capacity Source Data
docs.text: There are several sources of reservoir bathymetry data, many of which are discussed below. Note that authoritative input data for the project will be collected during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase and posted to the ‘basin-data’ directory for the project. These datasets will not be available for all reservoirs, and therefore they have been roughly listed in order of their importance. Judgement will be needed to ensure the best available data to estimate reservoir storage is being applied for the project. For example, if recent bathymetric surveyed data is available, it should be utilized. If survey is not available, historic documentation should be used to estimate bathymetry, and so on. It may be necessary to use information from multiple data sources to best approximate bathymetric data.

docs.title: Survey Data
docs.text: Surveyed bathymetric data can be the most accurate representation of reservoir bathymetry. If this data is available and reliable, it should be used as the primary source of reservoir storage model inputs. The format of this survey data will vary and may come in the form of a surface dataset (e.g., triangular irregular networks [TIN], raster, etc.), individual survey points, surveyed transects or cross-sections, or other formats. The sections below describe the typically available data, followed by processes that may be used for application of the selected source data on an individual dam basis.

docs.title: Historic Documentation
docs.text: Historic documents that describe the storage volume of reservoirs may be available. These documents may be available from the reservoir’s construction, or more recent studies. Common examples of these documents include Water Control Manuals, Reservoir Regulation Plans, Reservoir Sedimentation Studies, As-Built documents, and other design documentation. Ideally, these documents may provide elevation-volume or elevation-area data (either tabularly or graphically), which describes how the reservoir’s storage varies by elevation. If elevation-varied storage information is not available, storage volumes may be specified at given elevations of interest, such as conservation pool, spillway crest, top of dam, etc.

docs.title: National Inventory of Dams
docs.text: The NID provides a national database of dams and reservoirs and includes some basic information on their storage volumes. Normal and maximum storage are provided as attributes for all reservoirs in the NID. According to the NID Data Dictionary, maximum storage is defined as “the total storage space in the reservoir below the maximum attainable water surface elevation, including any surcharge storage.” Normal storage is defined as, “the total storage space in a reservoir below the normal retention level, including dead and inactive storage and excluding any flood control or surcharge storage.” For approximation purposes, it can generally be assumed that the reservoir storage below the LiDAR indicated water surface elevation in the DEM is equivalent to the NID normal storage, unless available data indicates otherwise.

docs.title: Historic Topographic Mapping
docs.text: Historic topographic mapping may be available from the period prior to the reservoir’s impoundment, providing topographic contours for the terrain beneath the reservoir. For very old reservoirs, this information may not be available, however historic topographic maps are available widely across the United States from the USGS beginning in the 1940s. The accuracy and detail of these maps varies and may be limited, but they do provide some estimate of the bathymetric surface. Data from these maps can be converted to DEMs and corrected to match a published elevation-volume curve where data is available.

docs.title: Terrain Indications
docs.text: When all other data discussed above is unavailable, LiDAR DEM terrain data may be useful for approximating bathymetry. For example, the storage volume of dry dams (dams which do not impound water permanently) may be accurately represented by the terrain DEM without additional information. For dams which do impound water but have no other information available regarding their storage, the elevation of the dam’s toe on the downstream side is likely a reasonable approximation for the deepest portion of the reservoir. The reservoir’s elevation is also well defined around the lake water’s edge, where depths are zero. A rough approximation regarding the reservoir’s volume can be estimated from this information alone. LiDAR DEMs may show locations where tributaries enter the reservoir body, these tributaries likely continue beneath the reservoir’s surface and any estimates of the lake’s bathymetry should attempt to capture these features. The bathymetry beneath the lake’s surface likely resembles the surrounding area, which should be well described by available LiDAR DEMs. These surrounding areas can provide context information on things like valley wall slope, pre-impoundment river channel width and meander characteristics, valley shape, and many other geomorphic features which can inform bathymetric approximations.

docs.title: RAS Mapper Terrain-Based Storage Capacity
docs.text:
There are multiple methods that may be used to incorporate reservoir bathymetry data into the RAS Mapper terrain to accurately represent the best available reservoir capacity information. In the sections below, typical workflows and processes for incorporating reservoir bathymetry are presented. The selection of specific method for each reservoir must be made based on the available source data and by applying judgment according to engineering best practices.

Regardless of the method applied, care must be taken to ensure any required vertical datum conversions are properly applied to the input data such that the reservoir storage representation meets the FFRD vertical datum standard.

docs.title: Survey Data Approach
docs.text: When an array of survey points is used as the source data, a TIN is a simple method of interpolating elevations between known points, which preserves the exact elevation at the known points. This approach is appropriate for converting point-based bathymetric survey data into a raster format which can be merged with a DEM at elevations above the reservoir pool. Compared to other interpolation methods, TIN creation has few required parameters and does not rely heavily on user judgment. However, the spatial distribution of input data points can cause errors in estimation, which will be discussed in the next section. This guide references tools in ArcGIS Pro, however, similar methods may be available in other GIS software.

docs.title: GIS Processing Steps
docs.text:
First, if necessary, a datum conversion should be applied such that the vertical datum of the bathymetric survey data matches the vertical datum of the DEM that it will be merged with. Next, the bathymetric survey data should be imported into ArcGIS Pro as a point feature class, using the XY Table to Point tool if the data was delivered in tabular format. Next, using a DEM as input, a polygon of the approximate reservoir extents at the time of the bathymetric survey should be generated. This can be done using the Contour tool. To create a single contour polygon, the base contour should be set to the desired pool elevation, and the contour interval should be set to a very large number beyond the range of the DEM values.

Figure 1. Creating a Single Contour Polygon

This reservoir extent polygon will be used as a constraint on the TIN creation. To ensure that no observed points are clipped out, the contour elevation should be set to several feet above the elevation at the time of data collection.

Optionally, to ensure that the slope of the TIN is logical, a thalweg line can be applied during TIN creation as a breakline. A breakline forces the process to consider areas on opposite sides of the breakline as independent from each other. Thalweg data may be provided with survey data or may be created using other methods. Breaklines are useful for constraining and refining interpolation between survey points and may be required to adequately reflect the reservoir’s bathymetry.

With point data, a reservoir extents polygon, and (optionally) a polyline of the thalweg, the Create TIN geoprocessing tool can be used. The point data should be input as Mass Points with the height field set to match the elevation field in the Attribute Table. The reservoir extents polygon should be used as a Hard Clip. The thalweg should be used as a Hard Line. The output coordinate system should match the DEM, and the Constrained Delaunay option is up to the user.

Next, the TIN to Raster geoprocessing tool can be used to convert the TIN into raster format. The sampling distance should be Cell Size, and the Sampling Value should match the cell size of the DEM (this should be precise, and may need to be copied and pasted from the DEM’s Properties dialog box). Using the Natural Neighbor method versus the Linear method results in very similar storage capacity relationships, but the Natural Neighbor method is more computationally robust, producing a smoother raster surface. Finally, in the Environments tab, the Snap Raster should be set to the DEM to enable seamless merging of the bathymetry raster and the DEM.

Next, as an optional step, the resulting bathymetric surface can be merged with the LiDAR based DEM to produce an elevation-storage relationship for all reservoir pools. This step should be done carefully to minimize any breaks in the data or large jumps in elevation, which can occur if there is a discrepancy between the bathymetric data and the DEM due to the uncertainty and slight estimation error that exists in all terrain data. The recommended approach is to create a contour at the exact pool elevation which existed during the bathymetric survey and use Extract by Mask to mask the TIN to Raster result to these extents. The Snap Raster should be the DEM when using Extract by Mask. Then, Mosaic to New Raster can be used to merge the result with the DEM, setting the Mosaic Operator such that the values from the bathymetry raster will be carried forward wherever they exist, and the values from the DEM will be carried forward outside the extents of the bathymetry raster. In the Environments tab, the Processing Extent of the Mosaic to New Raster tool can be set to a display extent that covers the entire reservoir, which is necessary to reduce file size if the source DEM covers a much larger area than needed. If the result is unsatisfactory, a different reservoir pool extent could be used in Extract by Mask to mask the TIN to Raster result, and Mosaic to New Raster could be applied again using the modified TIN to Raster result.

docs.title: Special Considerations and Potential Error Sources
docs.text:
For the greatest accuracy in TIN creation, the ideal situation is for known data points to be plentiful and randomly distributed through space. This condition would require the use of multibeam survey equipment. However, bathymetric data is sometimes collected along defined paths in a reservoir using a single beam echo sounder, which leaves large gaps between collected data. Figure 2 shows an example of this condition, which requires special considerations to be made during interpolation.

Figure 2. Large Gaps between Collected Data due to Single Beam Echo Sounder Data Collection

In this example, data was collected in cross sections and transects, with very small point spacing along the cross section and very large point spacing between cross sections. This distribution of points creates error in the final result. This error occurs because, in a reservoir, elevations are typically changing very gradually parallel to the flow direction of the original stream channel, but elevations change rapidly perpendicular to flow. This feature of the data is called anisotropy. In TIN interpolation, there is no way to correct for this condition, so interpolated points are generated based on nearby values in all directions. After the TIN was converted to a raster, the result was that elevations were overestimated in areas far from observed points. Figure 3 shows the locations of cross sections where the elevations are correct, with overestimated elevations (in darker shades of green) in between the cross sections.

Figure 3. Cross Sections with Correct Elevations with Overestimated Elevations between Cross Sections

The overestimation in this example was more severe due to the narrow, steeply-sloped valley in which this reservoir exists. A wider reservoir with flatter slopes would be less prone to this particular error. Other approaches, such as the Topo to Raster geoprocessing tool can provide a more suitable interpolation between data points in some cases, but each approach has limitations. In general, the result of the TIN creation process should be carefully examined for reasonableness. If there is clear overestimation or underestimation of elevations, another approach should be considered. Additional breaklines can be used to guide interpolation and reduce error. In some cases, if no one suitable approach can be found, engineering judgment may be necessary to combine the results of multiple approaches.

docs.title: Elevation-Storage Tabular Data Approach
docs.text:
Many reservoirs have elevation-volume data available which describes how the reservoirs storage varies with water surface elevation. Figure 4 shows an example of such data. Occasionally, this volume data is provided in elevation-area format, describing how the reservoir’s surface area varies with water surface elevation. Either of these formats can be converted to the other with some assumptions. Figure 5 shows an example calculation showing the conversion from surface area to storage volume, and Figure 6 shows the reverse process of converting storage volume to surface area. Note that if surface area is calculated from volume as shown in Figure 6, that surface area corresponds to the average elevation data shown in column D of the figure, and not the elevation data shown in column A.

Figure 4. Example Elevation-Volume and Elevation-Area Curves

Figure 5. Example conversion from Surface-Area to Storage-Volume

Figure 6. Example conversion from Storage-Volume to Surface-Area

docs.title: Approximate Approach
docs.text:
When elevation-volume data is available, but a bathymetric terrain surface is not, HEC-RAS terrain modifications can be used to modify the LiDAR DEM terrain surface and approximate the given elevation-volume curve. This approach involves using multipoint polygon terrain modifications and control points to approximate a bathymetric surface in RAS Mapper. This approximate bathymetry is iteratively modified until an acceptable match is achieved between the published elevation-volume data and the elevation-volume computed from the terrain surface. This process is similar to the one described in Detailed Approach section below. Figure 7 displays an example comparison of what can be achieved by iteratively approximating an elevation-volume curve by using HEC-RAS terrain modifications. Additional refinement of terrain modifications could be performed to better improve the fit between the two curves. Refer to the ‘Storage Capacity Consistency Check’ section for additional details on how to compute an elevation-volume curve from a terrain surface within HEC-RAS.

Historic contour data can be used to inform the shape of the bathymetric terrain, but is not always necessary. This approximate approach differs from the approach discussed in Detailed Approach section in which the terrain surface is corrected to closely match published elevation-volume data. While this approximate approach may not perfectly reproduce the curve, it is an efficient process and is appropriate for smaller impoundments that do not pose a high breach risk to downstream communities. Engineering judgement and team consensus will be required to determine which bathymetric estimation approach is appropriate for a given reservoir and when a given match between modeled and published volume estimates is acceptable.

Figure 7. Example comparison of Published and Approximated Elevation-Volume Curves

Terrain modifications in RAS Mapper take advantage of vector editing tools to create points, polylines, and polygons to modify an existing HEC-RAS terrain. These tools are useful for incorporating bathymetry into a terrain surface, as well as many other uses. Additional information regarding the use of terrain modifications is disused in this document, but also in the RAS Mapper User Manual.

docs.title: Detailed Approach
docs.text:
For large reservoirs which pose significant breach risk to downstream communities, it may be merited to pursue a detailed approach to estimate reservoir bathymetry that reproduces a published elevation-volume curve accurately. This detailed approach may also be required for reservoirs where HEC-ResSim operations are being simulated. This is required because HEC-ResSim specifies elevation-volume relationships using published tabular data, and any disagreement between reservoir storage in HEC-RAS and HEC-ResSim will result in disagreement of computed water surfaces in the reservoir.

The detailed approach consists of creating an approximate bathymetric terrain surface and then correcting that surface such that it reproduces a published elevation-volume curve. These two steps are described below.

docs.title: Creating a Bathymetric Surface
docs.text:
There are several ways to create an approximate bathymetric terrain surface. One, which has been employed successfully on the FFRD pilot projects, is to generate a terrain surface from historic topographic maps from prior to the reservoir’s impoundment, which are widely available across the U.S. . Note that for historic topography to be useful in this process, the historic maps must include contour data in the reservoir pool, which is common in maps created prior to reservoir impoundment.

The process to convert historic topographic mapping to a terrain which can be utilized directly in HEC-RAS is described in Table 1. This process involves tracing topographic map features such as contour lines and stream centerlines. It may be possible to automate this procedure such that manual digitization of contour lines is not required, however for smaller reservoirs where contour data is not particularly dense, manual digitization often seems to be the most efficient option.

The process is designed around the use of ArcGIS Pro and utilizes either the 3D Analyst Tools or Spatial Analyst License, specifically the Topo to Raster geoprocessing tool. It is recommended to review available documentation for the Topo to Raster tool prior to use to understand its data requirements and limitations. Some resources regarding the Topo to Raster tool include:

  • https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/tool-reference/3d-analyst/how-topo-to-raster-works.htm

  • https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/tool-reference/3d-analyst/topo-to-raster.htm

  • https://support.esri.com/en-us/knowledge-base/why-does-the-topo-to-raster-tool-generate-inaccurate-el-000012375

This process should produce a terrain surface DEM that can be directly incorporated into HEC-RAS, however the terrain surface generated will likely not agree with other sources of elevation-volume data, such as that commonly available in water control manuals. The next section describes how a terrain surface can be corrected to improve agreement between a terrain surface and given elevation-volume data.

docs.title: Correcting a Bathymetric Surface to Reproduce an Elevation-Volume Curve
docs.text:
Elevation-volume, and specifically elevation-area relationships, can be used to modify a given terrain surface such that it reproduces the input elevation-volume relationship. It should also be noted that this volume correction process can be applied to a diverse range of initial terrain surfaces, and not only surfaces created from historic topographic mapping as discussed in the previous section. Initial terrain surfaces should resemble the true bathymetric surface as well as is practical for best results. The volumetric correction process can be performed in a script or spreadsheet using linear interpolation methods to return values from a given elevation-area curve corresponding to a specified contour surface-area. If done in Excel, a custom linear interpolation function is very useful.

The steps to perform a volumetric correction process on a terrain is described in Table 2. This process generates elevation contour polygons from a terrain surface (step 6) and calculates the surface area associated with each contour (steps 7 through 9). Once the area of each terrain contour is known, the corresponding elevation can be looked up from published elevation-area data (step 10). A linear interpolation script is useful for this task; this is the corrected elevation attribute. Now each terrain contour has attributes of surface area, elevation (as computed from the historic bathymetry estimate), and a corrected elevation looked up from published data.

These elevation-corrected contours can be used as input data to generate a new terrain surface (steps 11 through 12). The elevation-volume relationship of the new surface should be verified (step 13). If needed, the process can be iteratively performed to improve the fit between published and modeled volume data (step 14). Finally, the process should create a terrain surface suitable for use in HEC-RAS, which reproduces elevation-volume relationships that will be used in the paired HEC-HMS and HEC-ResSim models (step 15).

As noted previously, this volumetric correction approach relies on elevation-surface-area relationships. Refer to Section 1.3.6.2, and specifically Figure 6, for an example calculation of how to convert an elevation-volume dataset to an elevation-area dataset. Note that the surface-area calculated using this process corresponds with the average elevation data shown in column D of Figure 6, not column A. Also refer to section 1.3.4 for the process to verify a terrain surface’s elevation-volume relationship in HEC-RAS (step 13).

Figure 8 displays several of the incremental steps of the detailed approach to approximating a bathymetric surface. Part (a) of this figure illustrates an example historic topographic map, including contour lines and stream centerlines, as well as a reservoir outline generated from LiDAR data. Part (b) illustrates the LiDAR DEM itself, which includes no bathymetric information. Part (c) shows the DEM resulting from this process, and Part (d) illustrates the excellent comparison which can be achieved between a terrain surface and a published elevation-volume dataset.

Figure 8. Example components of detailed approach for estimating a bathymetric surface.

Table 1. Steps to Create a Terrain Surface from Historic Topographic Mapping using ArcGIS Pro

# Steps and Process Input Files Output Files 1 Create outline of reservoir at LiDAR water level. Use simplify polygon geoprocessing tool to remove excess vertices. The Contour geoprocess (with a very large contour interval and base contour slightly above the LiDAR indicated water surface) can be useful in automatically generating a shapefile outline of the reservoir surface. LiDAR Surface SimplifiedReservoirOutline.shp 2 Digitize stream centerline through bathymetry. Upstream to downstream. Historic Topo Map StreamCenterline.shp 3 Digitize bathymetric contours into lines and assign elevations from contour map as an attribute (use historical topo or estimate from terrain). Additional contour lines can be estimated beyond what is shown in the historic mapping to add resolution for better interpolation. Historic Topo Map HistoricTopoContourLines.shp 4 Use Topo to Raster with inputs of the stream CL (as a Stream), Contours (as Contours, assign the elevation field from attribute tables), and reservoir outline (as Boundary). Select appropriate cell size. Provide a value for the Smallest Z value to be used in interpolation, this should likely be the lowest value of the elevation-storage curve, but it must be lower than the lowest contour from above.

SimplifiedReservoirOutline.shp

StreamCenterline.shp

HistoricTopoContourLines.shp

HistoricTopoRasterSurface.tif

Note: This first output raster should resemble the topo map and bathymetric surface, however it does not reflect the published elevation-storage curve. Steps below are used to correct the raster to match the storage curve.

Table 2. Steps to Correct a Terrain Surface to Match Specified Elevation-Area Data (sheet 1 of 2)

# Steps and Process Input Files Output Files 6 Use the Contour geoprocessing tool to generate contour lines from the first Topo to Raster surface. For contour type use the contour shell option. Contour at a relatively dense interval; Around 1-foot contour interval should be good. Smaller contour intervals provide more definition of the terrain surface. HistoricTopoRasterSurface.tif

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

7 Create 3 fields: NumParts (long), Area_Acres (double), and ElevCorrct (double). Then for each contour polygon feature, calculate the number of parts for each contour.

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

Same as left, but with number of parts calculate 8 Examine the number of parts for each contour interval. Use judgement to remove any contours which have a large number of parts. This is also the time to modify any contours to add definition to the bathymetry. Consider removing duplicate contours along the perimeter of the bathymetry

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

Same as left, but with unnecessary polygon parts and contours removed. 9 For each contour polygon feature, calculate the area within the contour (in acres).

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

Same as left, but with surface areas calculated 10 For each contour polygon (that shares the same elevation), use the surface area to lookup the corresponding elevation from the elevation-area curve. Copy this data into the ElevCorrct attribute field.

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

Elevation-Area curve

Same as left, with elevations from elevation-area curve 11 Use Create TIN geoprocessing tool to generate a surface from the corrected elevation contour data. Inputs are HistoricTopo_RegeneratedContours_1ft.shp as Hard_Line line type input using ElevCorrct as Height field. Also SimplifiedReservoirOutline.shp as Hard Clip type with Elev as Height Field (assuming reservoir boundary was developed from contour data of equal elevation, if not, assign <None> for the height field).

HistoricTopo_Regenerated…

Contours_1ft.shp

SimplifiedReservoirOutline.shp

HistoricTopo_Corrected_TIN

Table 2. Steps to Correct a Terrain Surface to Match Specified Elevation-Area Data (sheet 2 of 2)

# Steps and Process Input Files Output Files 12 Use TIN to Raster geoprocessing tool to convert TIN to a .tif raster. Use Floating Point data type, Natural Neighbors Method, and Cell Size sampling distance with a sampling value (i.e., raster cell size) appropriate for your model. 5–10 feet seems appropriate. HistoricTopo_Corrected_TIN HistoricTopo_CorrectedElev-Storage.tif 13 Import corrected bathymetric surface into HEC-RAS and overlay with a storage area that has the same outline as the reservoir. Compute the storage-elevation curve and compare to the published storage curve. Verify that the curve matches the terrain. If not, check above and sort out the issues. 14 If elevation-storage curve matches published data, continue to next step. If match is not sufficient, steps 6 through 13 can be iterated using the corrected raster as an input. This should improve the fit with the elevation-volume curve. 15 Combine into HEC-RAS terrain and model.

docs.title: Storage Approximation with Basic Storage Attributes
docs.text:
In many cases, complete elevation-volume data may not be available for a given reservoir. In these instances, the NID may have some limited information on the reservoir’s volume. One attribute of the NID database is the Normal Storage. For approximation purposes, it can generally be assumed that the reservoir storage below the LiDAR indicated water surface elevation in the DEM is equivalent to the NID Normal Storage, unless available data indicates otherwise. This single storage value can be used to estimate and validate a complete elevation-volume curve generated using HEC-RAS terrain modifications. Other terrain indications, discussed in Section 1.3.5.5, can be useful in approximating bathymetric terrain features.

The process involves iteratively creating HEC-RAS terrain modifications to estimate a bathymetric surface until the elevation-volume curve intersects the normal storage volume indicated in the NID at the water surface elevation indicated by the LiDAR terrain data. Figure 9 displays an elevation-volume curve computed in HEC-RAS using this approximation technique as well as the published normal storage point from the NID. Note that in this case the curve compares relatively well to the published value after only a single attempt to estimate bathymetry. The published NID value does indicate slightly more volume that estimated by the terrain (approximately 4 percent additional storage at LiDAR surface elevation of 519.3 feet). In this case, the terrain modifications could be refined to increase volume slightly to achieve better agreement between the terrain and NID volume.

One convenient way to achieve better agreement between the terrain and NID volume is to calculate the vertical difference in elevation between the elevation-volume curve and the NID storage value. In the example shown in Figure 9, the curve is approximately 0.2 feet above the published storage value. To adjust this curve, all the terrain modification control points can have their assigned elevations lowered by 0.2 feet and the curve should then intersect the published storage volume. This vertical adjustment assumes the overall shape and curvature of the elevation-volume data is reasonable.

Figure 9. Example Comparing Approximated Elevation-Volume Curve to Published National Inventory of Dam Normal Storage

Figure 10 displays the estimated bathymetry and terrain modifications used to create this elevation-volume curve. A multipoint polygon terrain modification was added around the reservoir rim, shown in blue. The Lower Terrain Value modification method was selected, such that modifications could only reduce terrain elevations and never add to them. The Perimeter Profile Mode was set to Use Terrain Elevations OR Control Points Override option, so that individual control points (shown as blue circles) could be added to define the lake’s bathymetry. Each control point is labeled with the selected lake bottom elevation. Note that this same terrain modification approach is applicable to other approaches to bathymetry estimates discussed above.

Figure 10. Example Terrain Modifications Used to Approximate Bathymetry

docs.title: Storage Approximation with No Supporting Data
docs.text: In cases where there is no known volume information on a reservoir, such as when the NID is missing a normal storage attribute, there is still a need to estimate bathymetry if breach analysis will be performed on that reservoir. In that case, the approach utilized in Section 1.3.6.3 should be followed, although there will be no known storage point to validate the bathymetry estimate against. Best judgement should be used to approximate bathymetry in these cases. Refer to Subsection 1.3.5.5 for more information on how features from a LiDAR based DEM can inform bathymetric estimates.

docs.title: Reservoir Outflow
docs.text: Operational releases from reservoirs are a key aspect of model representation. For FFRD models, the method applied for each dam in each of the models (HEC-HMS, HEC-ResSim, and HEC-RAS) will be guided by the selections made in the Dam Scoping Table. Modeling development decisions should always be made with an understanding of the interaction of other models in the FFRD system. The sections below provide additional modeling guidance of the implementation of the selected methods.

docs.title: HEC-HMS Reservoir Outflows
docs.text: For dams that are selected to represent a reservoir element in HEC-HMS, the following guidelines, techniques, and troubleshooting tips are offered. Note that the Dam Scoping Table will provide direction for HEC-HMS modeling using Simple Reservoir Operations, Complex Operations, or no inclusion in the HEC-HMS model.

docs.title: Un-modeled Reservoirs
docs.text: To account for the attenuation of flows through reservoirs not modeled in HEC-HMS, HEC-HMS may account for their presence with adjustments to the unit hydrograph parameters, increasing the time of concentration or storage coefficients. This approach can improve historic event calibration in HEC-HMS and improve the accuracy of HEC-HMS output data that passes to HEC-ResSim and HEC-RAS models.

docs.title: Simple Reservoir Operations
docs.text: Simple reservoir operations include one to one storage and discharge relationships, uncontrolled outlets, and ungated spillways. Typically, if the outlets and spillways have simple rating curves not dependent on inflow or seasonality, the dam can be modeled with simple procedures in HEC-HMS. A single outflow curve is the simplest hydraulic routing method available in HEC-HMS. If outlets are gated or controlled, simple operations can still be used by analyzing observed data and estimating an average outflow for a given elevation or storage condition. Guidance on creating a single outflow curve can be found in the Outflow Curve Option for an HEC-HMS Reservoir Element guide. Storage-discharge and elevation-storage relationships should be uniform across the HEC-ResSim, HEC-HMS, and HEC-RAS models (to the extent possible).

docs.title: Complex Reservoir Operations
docs.text: For more complex reservoir operations, the use of HEC-ResSim to model operating schedules, rules, and downstream constraints may be scoped for the FFRD project, however in some cases rule-based operations in HEC-HMS may by scoped for the project to provide an accurate and more efficient approach. When HEC-HMS rule-based operations are scoped for use as the authoritative reservoir outflow model, the following guidelines should be used.

docs.title: Storage Objective Patterns
docs.text: Storage patterns can be used based on published reservoir schedule guide curve or developed based on observed historical elevations converted to storage. Taking the 50 percent exceedance value of a duration analysis of the observed period of record of daily elevations can serve as a guide curve of storage objectives. The relationship should be input to the HEC-HMS model as a Paired Data–Storage Pattern table of calendar days and corresponding volume target.

docs.title: Zones
docs.text: Zones should be established to implement rules and store objective patterns that may change throughout the range of elevations. A Paired Data–Daily Elevation Pattern is needed to define the maximum elevation of the zone. This can be a constant elevation, or the zone elevation limits can vary throughout the calendar year.

docs.title: Release Rules
docs.text: Simple minimum and maximum release rules should be utilized first before adding release rate of change rules. Typically, this would include maximum release rules in a Zone to manage downstream capacity constraints or minimum release rules to better match gradual release patterns and smooth our spike releases. Release rate of change rules can be used. It is recommended to test shorter continuous simulations (approximately two water years) to see how the release rules perform and add complexity as needed.

docs.title: Downstream Constraints
docs.text: Downstream flow constraints are very computationally expensive and using a downstream constraint rule should be avoided in HEC-HMS. If a downstream flow constraint is prescribed in reservoir operations, this constraint should be managed to the extent possible with a maximum flow release from the reservoir that takes into account additional downstream flows that will accumulate at the downstream constraint point.

docs.title: Gated Outlets
docs.text: Gated outlets should be added as General Outlets. The minimum curve should be set to zero and the maximum curve will represent the maximum discharge possible or prescribed for a given elevation. The minimum time between adjustments should be sufficiently short so that the reservoir does not bottom out in between adjustments and not so short that the reservoir pool begins to oscillate unstably. It is recommended to start with a minimum adjustment time of 60 minutes and adjust as needed.

docs.title: Potential Modeling Obstacles
docs.text: Modeling reservoirs in HEC-HMS can be a trial-and-error process, the modeler should start with the simplest modeling method and add complexity as needed to best represent reservoir operations. During that process there are some common pitfalls.

docs.title: Reservoir Bottoming out
docs.text: The modeler should check that the minimum time between adjustments is not too long. If this is set too large, the gate settings may not be able to adjust to the prescribed storage pattern quickly enough. This is especially true for reservoirs with very low storage volumes near the bottom of the pool. Another potential workaround is to set a fairly low minimum release near the bottom of the pool or a constant storage pattern to ensure minimal or no releases occur during times where available storage is very low.

docs.title: Long Runtimes
docs.text: Avoid using downstream constraints and, to the extent possible, any rules that necessitate a decision between two outlets on where to make releases. This would include adding main and auxiliary outlets and having a minimum or maximum release rule from auxiliary and main outlets.

docs.title: Abrupt spikes in releases or lack of attenuated releases
docs.text: Spikes in releases can be smoothed by applying a maximum rate of change release rule or by instituting a minimum release rule to allow releases to be more attenuated instead of, for instance, peaking at 1,500 cfs outflows and immediately dropping to 0 cfs outflows once the guide curve is matched.

docs.title: HEC-ResSim Reservoir Outflows
docs.text: For dams that are selected for operations modeling in HEC-ResSim, the FFRD core standards are described in SOP Volume II and the guidelines, techniques, and troubleshooting tips that follow supplement those standards.

docs.title: HEC-ResSim Scoping of Approach
docs.text:
In the reservoir operations model, various levels of detail are available depending on the reservoir characteristics. As reservoirs and their operations increase in size and complexity, additional modeling techniques are required. Reservoir modeling decisions made during the scoping and data preparation phase of the FFRD project. The following topics will be clearly scoped and communicated through a combination of the Dam Scoping Table, Name Table, Model Linking Register, and Scoping and Data Preparation Report.

  • HEC-ResSim modeling approach for reservoir routing operations (detailed reservoir operations model, hydrologic model, or not at all)

  • HEC-ResSim modeling approach for each unique outflow feature at each dam (i.e., spillways separate from other outlets?)

  • HEC-ResSim Modeling approach for overtopping surfaces (dams and ungated spillways)

  • Determination of any locations where special treatment or coordination is needed between the hydrologic, hydraulic, and/or reservoir-operations model for consistency in modeled inflow, outflow, and reservoir elevation.

docs.title: HEC-ResSim Model Domain
docs.text: The HEC-ResSim model domain covers all reservoirs operating based on conditions elsewhere in the watershed. Even if each reservoir is operating independently of the others, a single HEC-ResSim model is preferred, however, in special cases it may be necessary to create several smaller headwater HEC-ResSim models for small reservoirs that cannot be modeled at the same timestep as larger reservoirs downstream in the system due to model instabilities and mass balance issues that occur when small reservoirs use too large of a timestep.

docs.title: Modeling of Overtopping Surfaces
docs.text: Dam overtopping, including for events larger than design storms, must be accounted for in the HEC-ResSim model and in Modeling Linking Register.

docs.title: Diversions
docs.text: When included in the scope, HEC-ResSim diversions should be added at major canal and diversion systems. These can either be connected to a downstream location or used to remove water from the system entirely. In heavily irrigated watersheds with many small diversions, they are lumped to the nearest downstream junction.

docs.title: HEC-RAS Reservoir Outflows
docs.text:
The guidelines for scoping and selection of hydraulic model treatment for dams in included in Job Aid 01, Scoping Processes, and full descriptions of each model treatment type are included in SOP Volume II. Specifics of modeling techniques for each type of model treatment are included in Section 8 of the SOP. Table 3 provides a high-level summary of the treatment types. HEC-RAS techniques and troubleshooting tips for dam outflow representation are also offered below.

Table 3. Hydraulic Model Dam Modeling Treatment Types

Hydraulic Modeling

Type A

Operation Modeled outside of Hydraulic Model

HEC-RAS Element: 2D Connection

+Outflow Blocked with high crest and no outlets (outflow sourced from another model or observations)

+Outflow time series sourced from observed data or simulation from other model

+Pool Bathymetry Terrain Modifications

+Terrain Modifications for breaching

Type B

Operation Modeled by Hydraulic Model

HEC-RAS Element: 2D Connection

+Outflow representing operation procedures for the dam. (i.e. may utilize features for gates, outflow curves, elevation-controlled gates, spillways, overtopping, etc.)

+Pool Bathymetry Terrain Modifications

Type C

Simplified Outflows Modeled by Hydraulic Model

HEC-RAS Element: 2D Connection

+Approximate representation of outflow features

+Pool Bathymetry Terrain Modifications

Type D

Basic Representation in Hydraulic Model

HEC-RAS Element: Breakline

+Approximate Low Flow Outlet Terrain Modification (notch)

Type E

Not Modeled

HEC-RAS Element: No modeling element

docs.title: Reservoir Routing Consistency
docs.text:
For dams modeled with the Type A treatment consistency of the reservoir routing results between HEC-RAS and the model sourced for outflows (HEC-ResSim or HEC-HMS) could be carefully evaluated. The FFRD model approach includes an inherent difference between the modeling of inflows to the reservoir between the models as HEC-RAS routes HEC-HMS-generated excess precipitation and baseflows over a 2D grid while HEC-HMS applies the unit hydrograph techniques to transform runoff. While the calibration process ensures close alignment between the model results, differences that persist may result in reservoir routing problems in HEC-RAS.

When the reservoir inflow from HEC-ResSim or HEC-HMS is out of sync with HEC-RAS simulated inflow, the HEC-RAS reservoir pool elevation hydrographs can diverge from the other models. If these differences are large, it is possible that the specified outflows for use in HEC-RAS could cause the reservoir to go dry and result in stability issues. A related issue that can arise is that a breach may be triggered based on the sampled trigger elevation for a stochastic event in the HEC-ResSim model, while the HEC-RAS model may not reach the trigger elevation resulting in an inaccurate routing of the breach outflows.

These pitfalls are avoided by ensuring that HEC-HMS and HEC-RAS calibration efforts are coordinated such that flood routing for the historic events are well aligned. Other recommended checks during the conformance phase of the project include a comparison of simulated minimum pool and peak pool elevations between the models and spot check comparisons of event-level pool elevation hydrographs.

docs.title: Dam Breach Representation
docs.text:
The workflows and other details regarding the dam breach approach are included in SOP Volume II and a separate job aid. However, the handling of reservoir breaching in HEC-HEC-ResSim for stochastic events requires special consideration, and an understanding of the interactions with the other models in the system is needed and described here.
