For dams modeled as Type A in the hydraulic model, the release of water through the breach is calculated by the hydraulic model based on the dam breach parameters (size and timing of breach opening), however a separate specified outflow boundary condition is also directly linked to the simulated HEC-ResSim releases. If not addressed, the HEC-RAS model could release water based on breach outflow and large HEC-ResSim operational releases that to not account for the falling pool caused by the breach.

To address the issue, HEC-ResSim will breach by setting outflows to equal inflows when a pool above that trigger elevation is reached. The breach trigger elevation is sampled for each event and shared between HEC-ResSim and HEC-RAS. In the HEC-RAS model during that same event, the breach will be triggered at the same instant based on the HEC-RAS routed pool elevation and release a larger volume of water through the breach. Actual outflow that the HEC-RAS model will see is HEC-ResSim Releases and HEC-RAS computed breaches.

There are a few imperfections in this method:

  • HEC-RAS and HEC-ResSim Simulated Pool Hydrographs may not track perfectly due to the timing of inflows. HEC-ResSim received inflows routed via HEC-HMS, while HEC-RAS simulates 2D flow over the mesh using via HEC-HMS excess precipitation and baseflow outputs

  • If pool hydrographs do not track well together, breaches will not trigger at the same time.

  • Breaches at upstream dams may trigger breaches at downstream dams. HEC-ResSim will not be able to account for this fact since the large volume in a reservoir is not actually released after a breach.

  • After a breach occurs, HEC-RAS may be releasing too much flow especially if inflows are high at time of breach.

  • It is possible HEC-RAS numerical stability issues will occur if HEC-ResSim inflows are forced out of a reservoir that is dry after a breach has released all water.

docs.title: Job Aid 9—Levee Representation
docs.text:
This job aid provides guidance for representation of physical levee structures within FFRD hydraulic models.

This job aid also discusses considerations related to levee breaches. The levee data lead also provides draft breach locations prior to the start of the Calibration Phase. Since HEC-RAS only allows a single breach per storage area/2D connection, the breach locations will impact the segmentation of levees that have more than one breach scoped.

docs.title: Levee Alignment and Profile Data
docs.text: The following subsections discuss levee alignment and profile data in the FFRD program.

docs.title: National Levee Data Refinement Effort
docs.text: As described in on other SOP materials, the National Levee Database (NLD) data is the official source for FFRD levee modeling. The NLD data for the study basin will undergo a data refinement effort prior to or as a part of the FFRD Scoping and Data Preparation phase, to ensure the dataset is adequate for direct use in FFRD modeling. This NLD data refinement effort is described by reference documents stored directly in the NLD.

docs.title: Sources of Levee Data
docs.text:
As stated previously, the NLD provides a near comprehensive list of levee structures to be included in the hydraulic model and, in many cases, the NLD will be the best available data source. However, there are cases where NLD data may be missing, incomplete, outdated, or otherwise incorrect. Levees are unique and dynamic structures which can change or be modified overtime. Levee raises, setbacks, erosion, or other modifications are examples where the NLD may not reflect the best understanding of the levee structure. For this reason, it is important for the hydraulic modeler to verify accuracy of levee data. This verification can be done through discussion with the local USACE district, through comparison with historic and current aerial imagery, and through comparison with other available data. Other potential sources of for levee data include risk assessments, district modeling and surveys, as-built drawings, and digital elevation models (DEMs) of the terrain, as well as other sources. If a discrepancy is found between NLD data and other sources, modeler judgement and discussion with the technical team lead may be needed to determine the best source of levee data.

One data source that is widely available for comparison with NLD data is a high-resolution DEM of the terrain. This terrain data can be used for verification of NLD crest profile data, however it may not accurately depict specific portions of the levee, such as floodwall sections or closures. If issues are found with the NLD data and a decision is made to use terrain elevations to define the structure crest, it may be necessary to modify the NLD structure centerline to correspond with the high ground indicated by the terrain. Often the NLD centerline will not perfectly align with the terrain’s high ground and maintaining the NLD centerline while recutting the levee profile’s crest data from the terrain can underestimate the true levee crest elevation.

docs.title: Communication with Levee Data Lead
docs.text: If NLD data is not adequate and the modeling team finds an alternative improved data source, it shall be annotated in the Calibration Phase report in the levee system-by-system table. It will also be communicated with the levee data lead for possible NLD data refinement.

docs.title: Direct Use of National Levee Data Base Profile and Alignment
docs.text:
Levee data should be adopted from the best available data source, which in many cases will be the NLD. Levee centerline data is available as shapefiles through the NLD and this alignment should be adopted unless better alignment data is available. This NLD alignment may be drawn upstream-to-downstream or downstream-to-upstream. Unless there is a compelling reason to reverse this directionality, the direction of the NLD structure should be maintained. If structure direction is reversed, this and its rationale should be documented in both the hydraulic model report and described in the HEC-RAS Connection Data Editor description box.

The levee centerline data is provided by NLD for the full levee system. This centerline shapefile can be split at levee segments so that each segment is represented by its own connection within the hydraulic model. It is generally best practice to create a small gap between storage area/2D connections which would otherwise touch. This small gap (on the order of 1-foot wide) improves 2D face meshing and can improve stability in some circumstances. When these gaps are added between structure segments, it may also be necessary to add a corresponding gap to the structure crest profile data, as discussed below. An additional technique, which can improve 2D cell meshing, is to underlay a 2D area breakline beneath the storage area/2D connection structures representing a levee system. This breakline can particularly improve meshing in instances where it spans the small gaps that can be placed between levee segments.

Note that depending on the direction the levee structure is drawn, and the side of the river the levee sits upon, the flow over the structure may be computed as either positive or negative. Figure 1 illustrates how HEC-RAS determines flow to be either positive or negative depending on the directionality of a levee structure.

Figure 1. Positive/Negative Flow Convention for Levee Structure Connections

In addition to levee centerline data, levee crest profile data is also available for many levees from the NLD. This data is accessed through the levee system’s Profile tab and can be downloaded as a comma separated value (CSV) file. These crest profile .csv files are typically formatted as x-coordinate, y-coordinate, distance (also known as stationing or m-value), and elevation (z-value). The distance (station, m) and elevation (z) values are of interest for defining the levee crest profile in HEC-RAS. By default, the stationing provided in the NLD .csv file should correspond to the direction of the centerline shapefile discussed previously. Once a connection structure is created in the HEC-RAS model, tabular XY coordinates and station-elevation data can be entered in the HEC-RAS weir/embankment data editor. An appropriate weir crest width and discharge coefficient should be entered in this editor, refer to the HEC-RAS User Manual for additional guidance on selecting weir discharge coefficients. If a structure’s centerline data was modified (e.g., reversed or split), a corresponding modification should be made to the profile crest station-elevation data.

docs.title: Levee Model Configuration Details
docs.text:
Levee data should be adopted from the best available data source, which in many cases will be the NLD. Levees should typically be modeled as internal storage area/2D connections. Refer to the Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions, for guidance regarding naming convention of levee structures. The HEC-RAS connection data editor includes a text description box, which should be utilized by the modeler to describe the source of levee data and any assumptions made regarding the levee structure. This text description should include the source of the levee centerline alignment, NLD ID number, NLD system and segment names, the source of crest profile data, and any other pertinent data or assumptions.

Levee structures should typically be incorporated into the HEC-RAS model at a segment-by-segment basis, this is especially true of long levee systems. Shorter hydraulic connections are more conducive to evaluation and troubleshooting of hydraulic model results compared with long connections. HEC-RAS is limited to a single breach location per connection, and therefore shorter segment-by-segment connections allow for potential breaching of each segment and multiple breaches in a multi-segment levee system.

docs.title: Overflow Computation Method
docs.text:
HEC-RAS prompts the hydraulic modeler to select the overflow computational method to calculate discharge over the structure, with the options of either the Normal 2D Equation Domain or Use Weir Equation.

2D equations more accurately calculate overflow for levees that do not have significant height above the surrounding terrain, are subject to full submergence during floods, and will generally be more stable than the weir equations but may overestimate overtopping discharge when there is a significant head difference over the levee structure. Weir equations better represent tall levees where the levee is expected to act as a non-submerged weir during flood conditions.

Weir equations should be used for FFRD models. 2D equations are not recommended for FFRD models because they are not fully compatible with the simplified physical breach method.

docs.title: Reference Points and Levees
docs.text: Reference points are required near levees to ensure adequate water surface elevation information is written out by the model in the vicinity of levees. The requirements for these saved output locations including reference points near levees are described in Section 8.3 of SOP Volume II. There are also specific naming convention requirements for these reference points defined within Job Aid 06, Section 1.6.

docs.title: High Ground
docs.text:
In some cases, the levee centerline provided by the NLD may not capture high ground that is not technically part of the levee system but effectively acts as part of the levee structure. Figure 2 displays an example where the levee centerline, shown in pink, does not capture high ground created by a bridge abutment. Levee systems should be inspected for instances such as these, and if there is adjacent high ground which acts as a water impounding surface, it should be included in the hydraulic model as part of the levee structure or as a separate breakline.

Figure 2. Example of High-Ground Adjacent to Levee Centerline

docs.title: Closures
docs.text: Many levee systems include closure structures which are not permanently installed but are closed during times of floods to prevent floodwater intruding through railroad crossings or other features. These features are intentionally excluded from the crest profile data in NLD but should be included in hydraulic models as part of the levee crest. Locations of closure structures are available within the NLD as part of the structure’s Features tab. Without more detailed information, the crest of these closures should be interpolated from surrounding crest profile data.

docs.title: Interior Drainage Features
docs.text: Levee systems frequently include interior drainage features such as gravity drain culverts, pump stations, gated pipes, and other features. Information on these features can be gathered from the NLD or other available sources. These features should be included in the hydraulic model with the best available data or approximated when detailed information is not readily available. Interior drainage culverts often utilize flap-gates to prevent backflow from the river to the interior side of the levee. The flap-gate feature in HEC-RAS can be used to approximate these culverts’ behavior and the diagram shown in Figure 1 may be useful for determining whether the no positive flow or no negative flow flap gate option should be used. Pump stations can also significantly impact inundation near levees and where information is available, these pumps should be incorporated into the modeling. Detailed information regarding the pump stations may be unavailable, and in these cases, engineering judgement should be used to best approximate the impact of the pump station. Terrain modifications or other modeling techniques may be required to approximate some of the underground pipe networks which feed interior drainage systems.

docs.title: Levee Breach Data
docs.text:
Levee breaching will be performed using the hydraulic model and levees should be incorporated into the modeling with this use-case in mind. If breach locations are known, additional 2D cell refinement may be required in areas where breach floodwaters are expected. Mesh refinement behind levees should take care to balance model runtime objectives with mesh resolution and detail.

Additional detail regarding breach location, breach parameter and system response curve selection are covered in separate SOP materials.

docs.title: Job Aid 10—Dam System Response Development
docs.text: This job aid describes the procedures for collecting dam performance data, scoping of the dam breach modeling methodology, producing system response inputs, and communication with the team to incorporate dam breaches into the modeling system.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: To incorporate dam performance into FFRD, dams where the evaluation of breaches may have a significant impact on flood risk characterization must be identified, a system response curve must be estimated, and a breach scenario must be incorporated into the hydraulics and hydrologic models and cloud-compute inputs.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: The following key roles are required to incorporate dam breaches into an FFRD project:

docs.title: Dam Data Lead
docs.text: The dam data lead is the USACE expert responsible for the development and delivery of system response probability (SRP) curves for dams. They also contribute to dam database refinement efforts and the scoping of dams for breach assessment in the FFRD model. Additionally, they review and evaluate breach results from stochastic model simulations.

docs.title: Hydraulic Modeler
docs.text: The hydraulic modeler is responsible for developing the model that fully accommodates dam breaches as scoped.

docs.title: Reservoir Operations Modeler
docs.text: The reservoir operations modeler is responsible for ensuring that the operations model accounts for breach events that may occur for reservoirs included in the reservoir operations model in by adjusting to appropriate post-breach operations releases.

docs.title: Cloud Compute Lead
docs.text: The cloud compute lead incorporates hydraulic and hydrologic models and SRP relationships into cloud compute for the project and ensures that results are shared with the team for review.

docs.title: Procedural Overview
docs.text:
The following key tasks are performed to establish the required model elements, breach locations, and system response curves for FFRD dam breach modeling:

  1. Prior to an FFRD Study: An effort to systematically refine and improve the quality of the National Inventory of Dams database within the basin is performed prior to the FFRD project. Guidelines for these separate efforts are not included in this SOP.

  2. Scoping and Data Preparation Phase:

  1. Ensure the adequacy of National Inventory of Dams data to support scoping of model approach for dams, including the selection of subset of dams to consider for breaching. FFRD project specific assumptions may be necessary to support the study and will be documented in FFRD reporting and available as feedback to the NID data managers.

  2. Select dams within the study that will be considered for breach assessment and document the selection within the Dam Model Scoping Table. Criteria for selection of dams to breach is included in Job Aid 01, Scoping Processes.

  3. Consolidate all existing risk assessment data for dams scoped for breach.

  1. Calibration Phase:
  1. Develop SRP curves for each dam included in the scope for breach. Development of dam SRP curves for the basin may include a combination of the sources as described in sections below.

  1. Establish crest elevation and toe elevation for each breach to be applied in SRP curves and hydraulic modeling.

  2. Upload SRP curve data to Model Library in established directory location and format for incorporation into cloud compute.

  3. Develop hydraulic and hydrologic models to accommodate sampled breach elevations during the conformance phase. This includes aspects of HEC-RAS modeling and HEC-ResSim modeling.

  1. Conformance Phase: Review and evaluate resulting sampled breach elevations and number of breach events that occur within the single realization conformance phase computation. Address any issues.

  2. Production Phase: Review and evaluate breach results from multi-realization event computations.

  3. Final Reporting Phase: Ensure proper documentation of breach inputs and results. Note that interim reporting is integrated into each FFRD project phase, and the end-of-phase reports will provide much of the technical documentation of dam breach inputs and results.

docs.title: Dam Breach Site Selection
docs.text:
Dam breaches for FFRD should be consolidated into a single representative breach location, which is generally considered adequate for flood risk characterization at basin scale.

Unique dam configurations or other circumstances may warrant a more complex treatment of certain dams, which must be coordinated with the full project team.

docs.title: Intervention
docs.text: Intervention, sometimes referred as flood fighting, will be accounted for in the system response probabilities resulting in lower probabilities of breach.

docs.title: Consolidation of Existing Risk Assessment Information
docs.text: During the scoping and data preparation phase of the FFRD project, the dam data lead is responsible for collecting all existing dam risk assessment information.

docs.title: System Response Probability Curve Development for Dams
docs.text: An SRP curve will be provided for all dams scoped for inclusion in the FFRD study. It is recognized that relatively limited data exist to support SRPs estimates. The sections below provide guidance for SRP development based on type of input data available.

docs.title: Dams with Existing Quantitative Risk Assessment Study
docs.text: Dams with a current existing Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) will leverage the performance data directly. FFRD models typically include a single representative breach location and SRP for all potential failure modes, so consolidation of the existing data may be necessary.

docs.title: Dams with Existing Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessment Study
docs.text: Dams with a current existing Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessment (SQRA) will leverage the performance data directly. FFRD models typically include a single representative breach location and SRP for all potential failure modes, so consolidation of the existing data may be necessary.

docs.title: Dams with other Existing Risk Assessment or Performance Information
docs.text: Other risk assessment or performance evaluation other than those described in the sections above should be evaluated on a case-by-case basis for the relevance of the existing data for FFRD modeling. When applicable, this existing information may be leveraged for FFRD SRP development.

docs.title: Dams with Existing Dams Screening Tool Risk Screening
docs.text: Performance assessments from finalized and approved Dam Screening Tool (DST) based risk screenings should be leveraged directly for FFRD SRP curves. FFRD models typically include a single representative breach location and SRP curve for all potential failure modes, so consolidation of the existing data may be necessary.

docs.title: Dams with No Existing Study
docs.text: Most dams in the United States do not currently have existing risk assessment studies to support SRP curve development for FFRD purposes. Additionally, no generalized, widely accepted default approach for approximate default performance of dams exists. For these dams, the two options described below will be applied at the judgement of the dam data lead for the FFRD project in coordination with the technical lead and project manager for the study. The time and cost to leverage more sophisticated approaches should be considered and weighed against other constraints.

docs.title: Rapid System Response Probability Assessment with Dam Safety Tool Approaches
docs.text: A rapid SRP assessment leveraging existing physical dam data based on methods documented within the DST may be applied for FFRD use. When applied, the Dam Data Lead is responsible for review and vetting the results.

docs.title: Simplistic System Response Probability Approach
docs.text: A simplistic SRP based on step-function primarily driven by overtopping of the dam may be applied for FFRD use. This approach typically would include a 0 percent probability of breach for all loadings below a overtopping depth threshold (such as 2 feet) and a 100 percent probability of breach for any loading over the selected threshold.

docs.title: Hydraulic Model Breach Parameters
docs.text: Guidance for selection of breach parameters for FFRD are included in SOP Volume II, Section 8.

docs.title: Review Evaluation of Breach Results
docs.text: A single realization of stochastic events is simulated by cloud compute during the Conformance Phase. Breach results from the Conformance Phase must be reviewed by the dam data lead to ensure that breach trigger elevations are being sampled appropriately and the number of breach events that occur at each breach location falls within the expected range. Similarly, the multi-realization results from the projection phase must be reviewed and evaluated.

docs.title: Job Aid 11—Levee System Response Development
docs.text: This job aid describes the procedures to collect levee performance data, scope the levee breach modeling methodology, produce system response inputs, and communicate with the project team to incorporate levee breaches into the modeling system.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: To incorporate levee performance into FFRD, one or more breach locations must be identified, system response probability (SRP) curve(s) must be estimated, and a breach scenario must be incorporated into the hydraulic and hydrologic models and cloud-compute inputs.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: The following key roles are required to incorporate levee breaches into an FFRD project.

docs.title: Levee Data Lead
docs.text: The levee data lead is the USACE expert responsible for the development and delivery of SRP curves for levees. They also contribute to levee database refinement efforts. Additionally, they review and evaluate breach results from stochastic model simulations.

docs.title: Hydraulic Modeler
docs.text: The hydraulic modeler is responsible for developing the model that fully accommodates levee breaches as scoped. Additionally, the hydraulic modeler confirms the levee control location (LCL) (initial overtopping locations) for each levee system based on modeled water surface profiles.

docs.title: Cloud Compute Lead
docs.text: The cloud compute lead incorporates hydraulic and hydrologic models and SRP relationships into cloud compute for the project and ensures that results are shared with the team for review.

docs.title: Procedural Overview
docs.text:
Establishing levee two-dimensional (2D) structures, breach locations, and SRP curves for FFRD modeling have the following main steps:

  1. Prior to FFRD Study: Refine and improve the quality of the National Levee Database within the basin prior to the FFRD project. Guidelines for these separate efforts are not included in this SOP. This effort includes improvement to the levee alignments, features, profiles, and leveed areas.

  2. Scoping and Data Preparation Phase:

  1. Identify which levee systems/segments are included in the study area and what levee data is available.

  2. Consolidate and evaluate existing levee risk assessment data

  3. Select number of breach location for each levee system

  4. Identify and document preliminary LCL breach locations

  5. Identify levee breach quantity and location(s) per guidance. Data is needed when Calibration Phase starts. Note that the breach location—particularly the LCL—may be adjusted during Calibration Phase.

  1. Calibration Phase:
  1. Verify and finalize breach locations, including assessment of LCL locations with the support of hydraulic model results

  1. Define levee breach sampling scheme

  2. Develop SRP curves for each breach location and upload to project directory

  1. Conformance Phase: Review and evaluate resulting sampled breach elevations, and number of breach events that occur within the single realization conformance phase computation. Address any issues.

  2. Production Phase: Review and evaluate breach results from multi-realization event computations.

  3. Final Reporting Phase: Ensure proper documentation of breach inputs and results. Note that interim reporting is integrated into each FFRD project phase, and the end-of-phase reports will provide much of the technical documentation of levee breach inputs and results.

docs.title: Identifying Levees in the Study Area
docs.text: The National Levee Database will be used as the authoritative listing of levee systems in the study basin, and every system will include at least one breach location for the FFRD project. NLD data refinement efforts that occur prior to the start of the FFRD project will ensure that the levee inventory is of high quality for project use. The Levee Data Tracking table will provide insight on the previously completed data refinement activities.

docs.title: Levee Breach Quantity and Location Selection
docs.text:
Breach location selection and quantity is a critically important but challenging aspect of levee modeling. The location and number of breaches impacts the potential inundation area in the FFRD results.

The levee data lead determines the breach quantity. The breach quantity will be set by a combination of many factors including levee mileage, number of levee segments, and risk assessment data availability. The breach quantity can be adjusted based on engineering judgment. The breach location will follow a general hierarchy for most critical areas to define but will also rely on engineering judgment in some cases. Documentation of applied judgement is required (See Subsection 1.5.1).

The following subsections outline the procedures. Selecting breach quantity and location can be expected to be iterative with the HEC-RAS modeling team; particularly with identifying a LCL, which is the location on the levee system expected to experience overtopping first. The initial breach locations are needed by the hydraulic modelers ahead of the calibration phase. The calibration model runs may yield a different understanding of the LCL location that will need to be adjusted.

docs.title: Levee Breach Quantity
docs.text:
Breach quantity is a balance of the level of detail required to display a representative picture of flooding and the inundation characteristics of the levee. Long levee reaches, levee systems with steep terrain, and/or short hydrograph floods present a theoretical need to increase the number of breach locations to provide a more comprehensive breach inundation. For example, a breach location at the downstream end of a long levee system will not incur inundation to the upper reaches of the leveed area. If available information indicates this is the most likely scenario for levee performance to contribute to the system, it is acceptable. In the absence of risk assessment data, there is uncertainty in making the selection.

When using levee risk assessment information, the breach quantity will be scaled to accurately represent what is known about the levee and not mischaracterize levee performance by over manipulating the input data. For example, combining multiple system responses from different Potential failure modes (PFMs) and locating them at a LCL or frequently loaded location will increase the annual probability more than intended. Similarly, arbitrarily splitting input data to apply to a larger number of breach locations may result in levee performance mischaracterization by the modeler and increase computation demands. Best practice is to add breach locations for the most significant contributors to the expected annual probability of failure; essentially where the levee was expected to have performance concerns. This approach will best yield results consistent with how risk assessors intended. PFMs with a lower probability of occurrence compared to the governing PFMs are not expected to trigger in the modeling and contribute to FFRD products. Levee specific performance information of levee features including earthen embankments and floodwalls can be applied directly. The quantity of breaches will vary but typically will have a breach location for overtopping and at least one breach per feature (earthen embankment, floodwall, etc.). This approach increases the likelihood that FFRD results will be aligned with published risk assessment results.

When more detailed risk assessment information is not available, a minimum number of breaches should be implemented based on levee length to improve chances of characterizing inundation area from levee breaches.

Table 1 provides the recommended minimum number of levee breaches per levee length. The breach quantity can be adjusted based on engineering judgment. The actual number of breaches may increase based on the underlying risk assessment data supporting the evaluation. When siting multiple breach locations, breach locations should be spaced somewhat evenly and prioritize another breach location in the upstream increment of the levee system. For example, if a levee is recommended to have two breach locations and the LCL is located in the lower half of the levee, a representative, prior-to-overtopping location should be sought in the upper half of the levee system. Engineering judgment is required (See Section 1.5.2).

Table 1. Recommend Minimum Number of Breaches for Future of Flood Risk Data Modeling

Levee Length Minimum Number of Breaches \<4 miles 1 4\<x\<8 miles 2 >8 miles 3

docs.title: Levee Breach Location
docs.text:
Once the target quantity of breaches per levee system is determined, the breaches can be located. Breach location selection is generally based on current USACE Mapping, Modeling, and Consequences (MMC) Production Center’s Technical Manual for Levees. Locations are identified using the following prioritized considerations:

  1. Breach location at the incipient overtopping location, also known as the LCL, as understood at the time of model setup including using initial without breach model runs. Note that this will likely require hydraulic modeling team input from initial calibration runs and be finalized iteratively.

  1. Known areas of poor expected performance based upon risk assessment results.

  2. Locations of maximum levee height. Do not select areas with infrequent loading or those significantly lower than typical overtopping annual exceedance probability (OT AEP). Refer to loading function, as available. Taller levee sections have more potential susceptibility to various PFMs and would yield significant breach inflows.

  3. Location in the upstream third of the levee system.

  4. High population or structure density areas adjacent to the levee.

While this list is provided in priority order, combinations of factors may present a stronger case than a rigid hierarchy. For example, a representative breach location adjacent to a population center in the upstream third of the levee may be considered a more critical location than a maximum height levee at the downstream end of the levee. FFRD steering committee guidance stated that FFRD flood hazard data is important for land use planning as it is for current structures; therefore, locating breaches to inform flooding to populated areas is a minor consideration overshadowed by the need to model most likely overtopping and breach locations. Engineering judgment is required. Location selection rationale must be documented.

docs.title: Multiple Breach Locations for a Levee System
docs.text: The SRP curves should be as representative of each unique breach location identified. For example, a single breach location at the LCL will have a simplified assumption that all critical prior-to-overtopping performance is combined and applies at the same location most likely to overtop. For levee systems with multiple breaches assigned, breaches will be assigned performance data that reflects the reason for selecting the location. For example, a levee system that has a LCL and two likely breach locations will have the breach locations represent the system response for those failure modes and the LCL represents the remainder of the performance evaluated during the risk assessment.

docs.title: Breach Locations from Risk Assessments
docs.text:
USACE risk assessments help inform levee performance during loading. The assessment may or may not document a likely breach location. Further, establishing breach locations from USACE risk assessment reports often requires manual searching through reports including Qualitative Risk Assessment (QRA), Semi-Qualitative Risk Assessment (SQRA), and Levee Screening Too (LST) 1.0 risk assessments. Table 2 provides information on risk assessment breach location sources and their characteristics.

Table 2. Breach Location Sources

Risk Assessment Type Breach Location Specified In a Database QRA Yes No SQRA Yes No LST (Current) Yes Yes LST 1.0 Sometimes No

The use of risk assessment information for locating breach locations is not without limitations. The information on likely breach locations contained in risk assessments may become out-of-date as levee improvements are made or incipient overtopping locations may not match FFRD modeled locations. The former is a limitation generally accepted following coordination with the local USACE district. The latter should be adjusted iteratively as the hydraulic modeling advances.

docs.title: Breach Location Selection for Levees with Levee Screening Tool 1.0 Data
docs.text:
Screening-level risk assessment data is available in the LST. LST 1.0 data is available from the “Legacy Screening” round (Figure 1). The levee user is seeking information that will inform a breach location(s) and this can be done by focusing on what scenario drove risk (overtopping or prior to overtopping breach). Reference the annual probability of breach field and the annual exceedance probability (AEP) top field for a comparison.

If the overtopping likelihood is more likely, the LCL should be selected for single breach levee systems. Information on the LCL may be discussed in the LST print-all found in the Levee Senior Oversight Group (LSOG) meeting folder. Additional supporting documents, including hydraulic and hydrologic memorandums or water surface profile versus crest comparison data may be available. Not all screenings will specifically identify an LCL, in those cases tentatively select a localized low point to be validated by HEC-RAS modeler runs.

If the prior to overtopping condition is more likely, evaluate which performance mode was assessed as most likely to occur and locate the justification language in the print-all or PowerPoint presentation. Seek references to a specific location that was the primary concern during the risk assessment. Not all screenings will specifically identify a performance weak point, in those cases tentatively select a breach location following general hierarchy guidance.

Figure 1. Location of Levee Screening Tool 1.0 Legacy Data in the Levee Screening Tool

docs.title: Team Communication
docs.text:
Selected levee breach locations for the project are stored in a Levee Breach Locations geospatial file. This file is established during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase of the project and adjusted as necessary through the subsequent phases of the project. This file documents breach locations, names, key attributes, and rationale for the selection of each location.

A template for this Levee Breach Locations file is included by Appendix C of the SOP.

The levee data lead is responsible for creation of this file during the scoping and Data Preparation Phase and will be available to the hydraulic modelers at the start of model development activities during the Calibration Phase. The hydraulic modelers must collaborate with the levee data lead to make necessary adjustments.

The team may also use a spreadsheet to manage interim communication of breach locations and attributes to gain a consistent understanding of breach elevations, selection criteria, and scenario specific details.

Table 3 displays the recommended minimum attributes used breach location documentation.

Table 3. Levee Breach Fields Used in Spreadsheet and Attribute Tables to\ Document Approach (sheet 1 of 2)

Levee Breach Field Description FID Feature ID in the geospatial shapefile NLD System - Levee Name Levee System Name in the National Levee Database NLDID Levee System ID in the National Levee Database Length in Miles Levee system length in miles. Used in identifying a target breach quantity for FFRD modeling. Number of Segments Number of segments in the system. A segment is a subcomponent of the levee system that in most cases designates a portion owned by a separate entity. Used in identifying a breach location for FFRD modeling if NLD - Segment Name Levee Segment Name in the National Levee Database Tier Identifies the risk assessment data tier (1-4 integer) SRP Source Identifies the highest fidelity risk assessment type available to develop system response curves. (QRA, SQRA, LST, LST 1.0, LLID) Location Description & Rationale User entered justification on breach quantity and location selection. In particular, documenting judgment applied in selecting the locations. Location Summary Short descriptor on the breach location selection criteria. (e.g. LCL, erosion, or Upstream 1/3). Feature Identify the levee feature that the breach has been located (embankment, floodwall, closure). This field informs the breach parameters to be used (Section 1.7.5). FFRD Hydraulic Model Unit Used to track which HEC-RAS modeling domain for each breach location. FFRD Hydraulic Model Unit Lead Used to identify which HEC_RAS modeling team to contact for information. Location Short Name Optional field for documenting team nomenclature for breach location. Long Longitude of breach location (decimal degrees, GCS North American 1983 datum) Lat Latitude of breach location (decimal degrees, GCS North American 1983 datum) X (ft) X coordinate of breach location (ft, GCS North American 1983 datum)

Table 3. Levee Breach Fields Used in Spreadsheet and Attribute Tables to\ Document Approach (sheet 2 of 2)

Levee Breach Field Description Y (ft) Y coordinate of breach location (ft, GCS North American 1983 datum) RAS 2D Connection Name The RAS 2D connection name assigned. A key identifier used across the HEC-RAS model, elevation trigger, and coded results output data. HW or TW Identifies if the RAS headwater or tailwater are relevant to the riverside slope of the levee. Station Nearest station in the HEC-RAS model to the breach location. Top Elevation of the levee crest/crown/top at the breach location. (ft, NAV88 datum). Used to define system response curve effective elevation range. Toe Elevation of the levee toe at the breach location. (ft, NAV88 datum). Used to define system response curve effective elevation range. Height Height of the levee at the breach location. (ft)

docs.title: Consolidation of Existing Risk Assessment Information
docs.text:
The SRP curves developed for FFRD modeling are uniquely developed and dependent on the understanding of the subject levee’s expected performance during flood loading as determined by a risk assessment. The National Levee Database data can identify if a risk assessment was completed (LSAC Rating field) and if a higher-level risk assessment was completed (Has QRA/SQRA (Yes/No) field). The levee data lead identifies all sources of levee performance data used to develop SRP curves.

Table 1.2. Risk Assessment Types and Description

Risk Assessment Type Description QRA USACE performed risk assessment with lowest degree of uncertainty. System response curves are typically an output product. SQRA USACE performed risk assessment a moderate degree of uncertainty. System response curves are typically not an output product. LST USACE performed screening-level risk assessment completed using the USACE developed Levee Screening Tool. Risk assessments typically have a high degree of uncertainty. System response curves are an output product. LST 1.0 USACE performed screening-level risk assessment completed using the USACE developed Levee Screening Tool’s former model (pre-2023). Risk assessments typically have a high degree of uncertainty. System response curves are not an output product. Levee Loading and Incident Data (LLID) USACE dataset of levee performance during flood events for the USACE portfolio of levees and non-federal levees active in PL84-99 rehabilitation program. Levees without a levee-specific risk assessment are assigned “average” system response based on this data.

Higher-level risk assessments such as SQRA and QRA can be found on the Corps of Engineers Dam and Levee Safety (CEDALS) folder on ProjectWise. Only finalized and posted by the project’s district, risk assessments with a signed memorandum will be used as a data source.

LST data is available from the Levee Screening Tool web application. LST 1.0 was decommissioned and the export data is available from the NLD manager and others. Most data is accessible in the current LST.

The risk information datasets are managed and accessible by USACE. The data continues to expand and be updated through both routine and nonroutine Levee Safety Program activities.

docs.title: System Response Probability Curve Development
docs.text:
An SRP curve represents the conditional probability of breach for a levee location. Figure 2 shows an example of a levee SRP curve plotted in terms of elevation versus probability of breach. The system response curve, a cumulative distribution function, provides a conditional probability of breach for prior-to-overtopping and overtopping events. The SRP curve begins at the origin (0,0) and increases monotonically.

The relationship of system response and annual probability is important to understand prior to developing SRP curves. Figure 3 provides an illustrative example of the area under an SRP curve and what that equates to in an annualized value. The curve is simplified to show p(f) of 1.0 at flood frequencies (AEP) exceeding the crest. The x-axis is the AEP of a flood loading event and the y-axis is the conditioned probability of levee breach. In the figure, the area under the entire system response curve is the annual probability of inundation (API). The area under the portion of the curve prior to overtopping AEP is referred to as the annual probability of breach prior to overtopping (APFPTOT) and the remaining portion of the curve represents the overtopping contribution to the API.

{APF\ }_{TOT} = \ {APF}_{PTOT}\  + \ {API}_{OT}

Figure 2. Example of a Levee System Response Curve: Elevation (feet) versus Probability of Breach. Dashed line indicating the overtopping elevation. The dashed line in the figure indicates the overtopping elevation for the breach location.

Figure 3. Example System Response Curve with key annualized probability terms represented.

A few key terms are important to define to build the understanding of how SRP curves are developed:

PFM: A PFM is a mechanism that once initiated potentially could progress to breach of a levee system or inundation of the leveed area.

Probability of Failure (p(f) or p(fail)): The probability of levee breach conditioned upon a given water surface elevation, percent levee height, or flood frequency.

Annual Probability of Failure Prior to Overtopping (APFPTOT): The annual probability of failure (APF) represents the likelihood, in any given year, that a levee will breach considering the full range of potential loads to which it could be subjected (LST Technical Reference Manual). The APFPTOT is a summation of only the likelihood of breach for loading below the levee crest.

API: The API is the APFPTOT plus the annual probability of overtopping with and without breach.

OT AEP: The probability that levee overtopping will occur in any given year considering the full range of possible annual floods.

SRP curve development utilizes risk assessment information and/or USACE levee loading and incident data. The development process is dependent on if the levee has been assessed with a risk assessment or not. Further, if the levee does have a risk assessment, the type of risk assessment informs SRP curve development.

docs.title: Tiered Approach to System Response Probability Curve Development
docs.text:
SRP curve development uses existing risk assessment information and/or USACE LLID. The SRP curve development procedure varies depending on the type of performance data available. The following represents the tiers of data that are available to characterize a levee’s expected performance given flood loading (in order of highest fidelity).

  1. QRA

  1. SQRA

  2. LST

  3. Legacy LST (LST 1.0)

  4. No Risk Assessment

QRA and LST risk assessments produce SRP curves for each PFM evaluated. FFRD SRP curves developed from these sources are combined, distributed, and/or down-scaled appropriately for the levee breach location scheme.

SRP curves developed from SQRA and LST 1.0 will use the risk assessment output values of APF for each PFM. An SRP curve is iteratively generated using the risk assessment APF as a target and adjusting log-normal shape parameters until the computed APF matches the target APF. FFRD SRPs developed are then combined, distributed, and/or down-scaled appropriately for the levee breach location scheme.

Levee systems without levee-specific performance data will be assigned a system response of an average levee based on the USACE LLID. FFRD SRPs developed are then combined, distributed, and/or down-scaled appropriately for the levee breach location scheme.

docs.title: Intervention
docs.text: In most modern risk assessment procedures, the ability for to detect and intervene to arrest a breach mode during a flood is already included. For legacy levee performance data, including LST 1.0, an intervention correction is applied using LLID parameters. The LLID data for average levee performance including intervention will be applied for all levees without levee-specific data.

docs.title: Overtopping Performance
docs.text:
For FFRD, the levee overtopping performance is considered using levee risk assessment for levee-specific assessment or USACE loading and incident data where it is not available.

Table 4 provides a matrix of risk assessment type and the source of overtopping system response. Table 5 portrays the average overtopping system response based on the USACE levee loading and incident data. Levee performance during overtopping varies based on levee geometry, soil type, and erosion protection measures. Without readily available levee information, the average performance is considered acceptable simplification. However, in some case the assumption of active intervention may not be appropriate.

Table 4. Overtopping Levee Performance Source

Risk Assessment Type Overtopping Performance QRA Levee Specific SQRA Levee Specific LST (Current) Levee Specific LST 1.0 Average Performance No Risk Assessment Average Performance

Table 5. Average Levee System Response during Overtopping

Loading Probability of Breach OT+0.5 foot 57% OT+2 foot 68% OT+3 foot 76%

Source: LST Technical Manual DRAFT. Figure 129. Probability of Breach during Overtopping for Different Depths and Soil Types. “All Levees” data.

docs.title: Multiple Overtopping Locations on the Same System
docs.text: In consideration of multiple levee breach locations, every breach location assigned will include an overtopping system response. This will model a realistic scenario in which all potential areas of overtopping have a likelihood of breaching from overtopping. USACE subject matter experts (SMEs) concluded this approach creates a situation for the model to perform its own competing risk calculation, minimizing the length effect concern. Theoretically, as the initial overtopping location begins to overtop and possibly breach, the river stage at the other breach locations is less likely to achieve an elevation needed to trigger a breach. Thus, each of the individual breach locations of a particular levee system are competing against each other for the chance to breach. Once one is successful, the probability of another occurring drops so as not to introduce length effect issues of the probability of failure approach.

docs.title: Starting with a Quantitative Risk Assessment
docs.text:
From a QRA report, the probability of failure for a given percent loading, flow, or event AEP is typically available directly, but this information needs to be manually collected and then assigned to the breach location setup previously determined. Breach location assignment may involve combination of one or more PFMs and the addition of expected overtopping performance. If loading function information is available at multiple locations and varies significantly, the PFM specific system response should be converted to an AEP versus p(f) format before combining.

Figure 4 provides an example of input data collected from a USACE QRA report. In this particular example, the x-axis is provided in river flow and will need to be converted to percent levee height or AEP depending on if the system response for this PFM is a standalone breach location or requires combination with other PFMs. In order to convert the axis, the user will collect information from the report that best provides an understanding of the flood loading function and coincident loading reference points on the levee.

Figure 4. Example System Response Probability Curves for a Single Performance Mode Included in a USACE Quantitative Risk Assessment Report

Combination of likelihood of breach for multiple PFMs into a single breach location is accomplished with simple addition using the formula below. This calculation assumes the PFMs are mutually exclusive. The combination also assumes that the frequency of flood loading is common at a given percent loading in order to arrive at the intended APF for the system. For example, combination of two SRP curves for different PFMs at a 50 percent loading must mean that the 50 percent loading at each of the locations corresponds to approximately the same frequency of flood loading (i.e., 50 percent loading at both locations is equivalent to a 1 in 50 AEP).

{P(fail)}_{Total\ } = \ {\sum p(fail)}_{i}

docs.title: Starting with a Semi-Qualitative Risk Assessment
docs.text:
In most cases, a semi-quantitative risk assessment will not include SRP curves for each PFM. The output users will be looking for from SQRA will be loading functions and the evaluated PFMs and their respective elicited APF with intervention. The general process for developing SRP curves from this data is to set the APF as a target and develop a SRP curve where the area under the curve match that APF target. The shape of the curve is influenced by log normal shape parameter (sigma) that are from USACE LLID. USACE maintains a spreadsheet for this computation. The detailed steps are:

  1. Evaluate hydraulics and hydrologic data in the report. Locate and enter one or more flood loading functions required to represent the flood loading for the PFMs. Input data needed: Stage and AEP for each loading curve. Enter the incipient overtopping point to document the cutoff point for APF calculation.

  1. Locate and enter the APF with intervention for each elicited PFM as the target APF for the calculated SRP for each PFM. All credible breach modes from the SQRA report will be used to ensure that the total APF for the system is considered. Assign each PFM to a standard PFM type to associate the LLID shape parameters.

  2. Perform log-linear interpolation of each percent loading function to a standardized set of nodes to normalize all PFM entries to a common set of AEP range so SRPs can eventually be combined.

  3. Set an iterative computation of SRP at each AEP node by varying shape parameter (σ), sigma ln, until the computer APF of the created SRP curve matches the target APF from the SQRA report.

  4. Repeat for all credible PFMs.

  5. Combine all or portions of the calculated SRP to match the desired distribution of PFMs at the breach locations. A system represented by only a single breach location will sum all calculated PFM SRP curves. A levee system represented by multiple will use specific PFM SRP curves directly and sum the remainder at the LCL, for example.

Figure 5 shows the calculation spreadsheet setup with orange cells indicating entry. Fail mode is selected from a set dropdown list. User selects the loading function that applies to this PFM. User enters the elicited PFM APF with intervention from the SQRA report. Finally, an iterative approach is used to change sigma ln until the computed APF matches the target APF. The prior to overtopping SRP curve is now created for this PFM. The expansion of percent levee height is a log-linear interpolation of the input flood loading curve. The calculation of SRP is done as a cumulative density function with a log-normal distribution for the given percent levee height ordinate with mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ). Finally, the computation of APF is computed as the area under the SRP curve using a Riemann sum trapezoidal approximation:

APF = \sum_{}^{}{\left( x_{i} - x_{i + 1} \right) \times \left( y_{i} + y_{i + 1} \right)} \div 2

Figure 6 and Figure 7 illustrate the computation of APF and the Riemann sums midpoint method.**

Figure 5. Example System Response Curves Computation Sheet for a Single PFM.

Figure 6. Illustration of APF computation from a system response curve (from Levee Screening Tool Application and Technical Manual)

Figure 7. Illustration of Riemann sums trapezoidal approximation (from Levee Screening Tool Application and Technical Manual)

docs.title: Starting with a Screening Level Risk Assessment
docs.text:
Screening level risk assessment data is available in the LST. Once a multi-disciplinary team completes a screening, the LST maintains a database information on levee performance (PFMs), representative breach locations assigned to those PFMs, and representative loading functions assigned to those PFMs. The LST uses a standard set of PFMs that are assessed depending on the features of the levee (e.g., earthen embankment, floodwall, and/or closure structure). Overtopping with breach for the incipient overtopping location is also assessed.

Within the LST, a large database export can be accomplished with administrator privileges in the Reports tab on the main banner. SRP curves can be extracted on a levee-by-levee basis within the Computations tab of a levee segment. Figure 8 provides an example of the levee segment computation export with SRP curve in a consistent AEP format and also provides percent levee height versus p(fail) curves for each PFM. In this example, the levee has an earthen embankment and floodwall backward erosion piping breach mode assessed with differing loading curves. The embankment reaches 100 percent of the levee height at a more frequent loading than the floodwall section. This is important to recognize when considering breach setup and combination of PFMs.

Figure 8. Levee Screening Tool Levee System Response Curve Results for Two of the Evaluated Probable Failure Modes

For each levee segment, the common-cause adjusted SRP results will be used. The LST will present SRP curves by PFM, all prior-to-overtopping PFMs combined, and overtopping with breach. Combination and splitting the SRP curves is required based upon the breach quantity setup by the user for the subject levee system.

docs.title: Starting with a Screening Level Risk Assessment (LST 1.0)
docs.text:
The most prevalent levee performance on levees in the United States comes from the first version of the LST (LST 1.0) with a screening level risk assessment completed for the entire USACE portfolio.

The input data needed to develop SRP curves from this data set is the LST 1.0 database export file from October 2023. The file contains levee toe and crest loading frequency (AEP) and performance indexes for each PFM. The user should understand that it is typical that only a single generalized loading curve representing the most frequent loading toe AEP and the incipient overtopping frequency are available. A segment may have a separate loading function for embankments, floodwalls, and/or closure structures if those features are present. Further, the levee performance was based on a relative risk Bayesian updating approach. Conversions of the performance data are included to correct the performance estimates to expected magnitudes and shape parameters from the USACE Levee Loading and Incident data as well as corrections to account for intervention. Screenings were performed on levees at the segment level. One or more segments may comprise a levee system. The following procedures are for a combined SRP curve at the segment level. Single breach setups for a multi-segment system will combine the curves as previously described in the p(fail) versus AEP format. Multiple breaches assigned to a single-segment will require splitting relevant PFMs to each breach location as appropriate.

Given that all input data is being pulled from a single dataset, the process is mostly automated once computational setup is achieved. USACE maintains a spreadsheet for this computation. The detailed steps are:

  1. Enter the NLD segment ID of the subject levee segment in the Loading tab

  1. Extract the LST flood loading data. Toe and crest AEP estimates are available for the segment and additional entries for floodwall loading and closure structure loading, if applicable.

  2. Select Yes for every applicable PFM. The LST used a standardized set of PFMs applicable to a feature type (embankment, floodwall, and closure structure).

  3. Assign the correct loading function to each PFM.

  4. On the Combined Curve tab, the target APF will be pulled from the LST 1.0 data. The intended LST 1.0 field is the performance index. There is a performance index associated with each evaluated PFM from the standardized suite of LST PFMs.

  5. Iteratively adjust the sigma ln shape parameter until the calculated APF for the SRP curve you are creating matches the target APF.

  6. Repeat for all credible PFMs.

  7. The spreadsheet will perform the computation to account for intervention (Figure 9).

  8. Combine all or portions of the calculated SRP (adjusted for intervention) to match the desired distribution of PFMs at the breach locations. A system represented by only a single breach location will sum all calculated PFM SRP curves. A levee system represented by multiple will use specific PFM SRP curves directly and sum the remainder at LCL, for example.

Figure 9. Unsuccessful Intervention Probabilities Used to Adjust LST 1.0 Performance Data to Account for Intervention.

docs.title: No Risk Assessment is Available
docs.text:
The levee system or segment performance has not been assessed with a risk assessment, average levee performance parameters are applied based on the USACE LLID (Levee Screening Tool Application and Technical Manual [September 2024] Appendix A: USACE Levee Loading and Incident Data). The LLID data uses historic performance during flood events to provide expected performance given flood loading.

The SRP curve is generated using methods and computations described above with the following LLID breach probabilities and log-normal shape parameters (Table 6). Each levee segment will have a SRP curve computed using these curve parameters with performance modes that apply the levee features (embankment, floodwall, closure structure) as viewable in the National Levee Database alignment line type.

Table 6. Probability of Breach and Shape Parameters Derived from LLID (from Levee Screening Tool Application and Technical Manual)

Potential Failure Mode Probability of Breach at 100% Loading Mean Shape Parameters (Log-Normal) (µ) Standard Deviation Shape Parameters (Log-Normal) (σ) Backward Erosion and Piping (BEP) Embankment 0.0426 0.3536 0.2055 Internal Erosion (IE) Embankment 0.0426 0.3547 0.2060 Embankment Slope Instability 0.0088 0.5781 0.2435 Embankment Riverine Erosion 0.0090 3.2805 1.3858 Floodwall Backward Erosion and Piping (BEP) 0.0096 2.3064 0.9858 Floodwall Instability 0.0324 0.6203 0.3360 Closure Structure Failure or Misoperation 0.0419 0.9446 0.5464

docs.title: System Response Probability Curve Conversion
docs.text:
During SRP curve creation, the format most likely will need to change axes formats from initial AEP versus p(f). In addition, the required a standard number of ordinates on the FFRD curves may require an expansion of contraction of a calculated curve. Finally, the FFRD HEC-RAS model requires an elevation versus p(fail) format using the elevations at the breach location (North American Vertical Datum 1988 datum). As Figure 10 illustrates, the AEP axis is then converted to a percent height and finally to a usable format for FFRD to the breach location selected in an elevation versus p(f) format.

Figure 10. System Response Probability Curve Format Conversion Process

  1. The SRP curve generation process requires establishment of a related percent levee loading relationship to the flood loading. This step is to revert the AEP axis back to a percent levee height scale. This is available in all USACE prepared computational spreadsheets. The user must ensure that the scale represents the percent loading for the intended breach location.

  1. The following step is to convert to the standardized number of ordinates. Currently, the SRP curve generation spreadsheets are setup to generate an SRP curve with 10,000 ordinates to conform to FEMA insurance pricing requirement; however, depending on the development method the raw curve may have a different number. Regardless, the user will convert the input SRP curve to a standard number of ordinates following Table 7. A USACE prepared spreadsheet is available to perform this computation. Computationally, the input SRP curve is log-linearly interpolated into the final format.

  2. The final step is to convert percent height into elevations at the breach location. The levee toe and crest elevation are documented in the breach location files (See Section 1.5.2.4).

$Elevation = Percent\ Loading \times (Crest\ Elevation - Toe\ Elevation) + Toe\ Elevation$

Table 7. Prior to Overtopping Probability Curve Format Template.

Number Percent Levee Height Probability of Failure 1 0 # 2 0.05 # 3 0.1 # 4 0.15 # 5 0.2 # 6 0.25 # 7 0.3 # 8 0.35 # 9 0.4 # 10 0.45 # 11 0.5 # 12 0.55 # 13 0.6 # 14 0.65 # 15 0.7 # 16 0.75 # 17 0.8 # 18 0.85 # 19 0.9 # 20 0.95 # 21 1 #

docs.title: System Response Allocation, Combination, and Formatting
docs.text:
SRPs representing performance for a specific PFM or levee segment are then combined to allocate system response to the defined breach location scheme. If the breach scheme identifies the need for a prior-to-overtopping breach location and the risk assessment source has identified a critical flaw at this specific location, the full system response for that breach mode is applied. If no critical location is identified, the combined performance modes evaluated in the risk assessment are down-scaled and applied evenly to all breach locations. The combination computation applies De-Morgan’s rule for statistically independent and not mutually exclusive PFMs when the risk assessment data is unadjusted (Table 8).

$Probability\ of\ Failure = 1 - \left( 1 - p(fail) \right)^{N}$

Table 8. Summary of Adjusted and Unadjusted System Response by Risk Assessment Type

Risk Assessment Type Adjusted or Unadjusted Combination Method QRA Unadjusted De-Morgan’s Rule SQRA Unadjusted De-Morgan’s Rule LST (Current) Adjusted Summation LST (v1.0) Adjusted Summation

When levee SRPs need to be distributed to multiple identified breach locations, an adjustment to De-Morgan’s rule is applied to the conditional probability of failure for the system (P(fail)) to statistically down-scale the system response the number of breach locations (NLocations).

Probability\ of\ Failure = 1 - \left( 1 - P(fail) \right)^{{1/N}_{Locations}}

Once the SRP is developed for the breach location scheme, the data is converted to the correct stage range from levee toe elevation to crest elevation. The overtopping response is added using De-Morgan’s rule to the prior-to-overtopping system response, assuming that prior-to-overtopping at 100 percent of the levee height remains constant. Finally, if the probability of breach has not reached 1.0, the SRP curve is completed by interpolating from the overtopping response.

docs.title: Combining Overtopping performance to Prior to Overtopping
docs.text:
Risk assessments typically document overtopping differently and include a direct SRP curve that expresses probability of failure as a function of depth over the crest of the levee at that location. Therefore, this portion of the SRP curve will be added after the SRP curve is in the elevation versus p(f) format.

The elicited overtopping performance typically has a maximum range (i.e., only elicited to 2 feet over the levee crest height). For the purposes of FFRD, the overtopping performance will be extrapolated to 99.9 percent chance of breach and then given a straight line 100 feet above the levee crest elevation to ensure that the sampled elevation range exceeds the potential FFRD hydraulic model elevation.

The final step when combining prior to overtopping and overtopping portions of the SRP is to consider that prior to overtopping PFMs have a non-zero chance of breach, even during overtopping. For example, a levee may still breach from internal erosion during overtopping so it will be combined with likelihood of overtopping with breach; however, tailwater conditions as the leveed area fills ultimately cause the internal erosion SRP to plateau. To account for this, the prior to overtopping probability of failure is held as a constant during the addition of the overtopping performance probability of failure. In reality, this is not a constant, but this approach is used as a simplification to not ignore this contribution.

docs.title: Breach Characteristics
docs.text: Guidance for selection of breach parameters for FFRD are included in SOP Volume II Section 8.

docs.title: Format for Levee System Response Probability Curve Delivery
docs.text:
The SRP curves are compiled, quality control reviewed, and then uploaded to Model Library as a single .CSV file following the format as Figure 11 shows. The file includes the 2D connection name in HEC-RAS model to link the system response trigger elevation sampled from the system response curve to the water surface modeled in HEC-RAS. The remaining common data fields are data identifiers including the National Levee Data system and segment ID numbers, and the basis for the system response curve (i.e., risk assessment source, potential failure mode, or default performance). The system response includes prior to overtopping and overtopping performance.

The provided SRPs formatted as .CSV files will be used as an input for the cloud-compute actions that sample breach trigger elevations for the stochastic simulations.

A template for use in creation of the required SRP format for delivery in included in Appendix C, named system-response. Note that no commas are allowed in the header information fields.

The levee SRP .CSV file should be initially delivered to the project directory in the ‘[basin-name]>calibration>system-response’ path.

Figure 11. Combined Levee System Response

docs.title: Review Evaluation of Breach Results
docs.text: A single realization of stochastic events is simulated by cloud compute during the conformance phase. Breach results from the conformance phase must be reviewed by the dam data lead to ensure that breach trigger elevations are being sampled appropriately and the number of breach events that occur at each breach location falls within the expected range. Similarly, the multi-realization results from the projection phase must be reviewed and evaluated.

docs.title: Status of This Document
docs.text:
This document is provided as a raw draft for early delivery, but has not undergone complete content review, editing or finalization. It is possible some draft content is duplicated elsewhere or has fallen out-of-sync with the current SOP.

When complete, this document will provide focused guidance and best practices for calibration of the full system of FFRD models. The guidance will include considerations for feedback loops between storm events, hydrology, and hydraulics for event-based modeling.

Considerations for comparison of multi-year simulation results vs. long term observed data will be provided.

This job aid provides guidance and best practices for model calibration throughout the FFRD modeling process. The first section focuses on event-based calibration preformed during the calibration phase of the basin study. Selection of historic calibration events, gages, and targets, as well as calibration within the probabilistic space are discussed elsewhere in the SOP and Job Aids.

The second section focuses on calibration of the full watershed modeling system during the conformance phase. This includes ensuring the integrated set of models is able to accurately simulate historic events, and that simulated results over a long-term simulation reasonably match observed data at long-term gages sites.

docs.title: Calibration Phase
docs.text: Model calibration is performed with the intent of developing event-specific modeling results which can be used by other models in the modeling sequence. Additionally, acceptable model parameters that can be used within probabilistic/hypothetical simulations are required and obtained through historical event simulations. Due to the nature of the FFRD modeling environment, results from one model can serve as inputs to another model. This is the case for hydrologic model results which serve as the primary inputs for the reservoir and hydraulic models. Due to these dependencies, there may be cases where iteration is required between models to achieve acceptable model calibration results. In these cases, coordination between the hydrologic modeler, hydraulic modeler, reservoir operations modeler, and/or technical team lead may be required to achieve successful calibration across the suite of FFRD models.

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Calibration
docs.text:
The performance of the hydrologic model must be evaluated by comparing computed results against observed results at each primary, secondary, and tertiary location. Model parameters should be altered in consistent and tractable ways in order to minimize the differences between computed and observed hydrograph shape, peak flow rate, and discharge volume.

Additionally, if the watershed is hydrologically impacted by snowmelt, model parameters should be altered in consistent and tractable ways in order to minimize the differences between computed and observed snow water equivalent (SWE). Generally speaking, matching the shape and magnitude of observed SWE will result in acceptable streamflow calibration. However, it is possible to closely match observed SWE accumulation / melt and still under- or over-predict runoff rates. Therefore, it is preferred to more closely match runoff rates in lieu of SWE when the two observed data sets result in different parameters to achieve acceptable calibration.

docs.title: Primary Hydrologic Model Calibration Parameters
docs.text:
Parameters that are used to calibrate an HEC-HMS model include subbasin specific parameters such as initial deficit and constant loss rate. Other parameters, such as subbasin area and percent impervious cover, must not be used as calibration parameters without physical justification. Parameters that are commonly varied during model calibration are highlighted within Table 2.1.

Table 2.1. Calibration Parameters and Approach

Process Parameter Calibration Approach Infiltration Initial Deficit This parameter is varied to match the observed initiation of runoff. Infiltration Maximum Deficit This parameter is varied to match the initiation of runoff after prolonged periods with little to no precipitation during continuous simulations. Physical justification must be provided if using varying values for multiple historic events. Infiltration Constant Loss Rate This parameter is varied to match the observed runoff peak and volume. Infiltration Percent Impervious Cover In general, this parameter is not varied during model calibration except when necessary to correct for a lack of standing water or large changes in impervious area throughout time. Physical justification must be provided if using varying values for multiple historic events. Runoff Transform Time of Concentration (Tc) This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape, primarily on the rising limb. This parameter is also varied to match the observed time of peak flow. Runoff Transform Storage Coefficient (R) This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape, primarily on the receding limb. This parameter is also varied to match the observed peak flow magnitude. Baseflow GW 1 Initial Discharge This parameter is varied to match the observed discharge at the beginning of the simulation. Baseflow GW 1 Fraction This parameter is varied to match the observed runoff volume. Baseflow GW 1 Coefficient GW 1 is conceptualized to represent interflow (i.e., fast-responding portion of baseflow). Therefore, this coefficient must be set to a smaller value than the GW 2 coefficient. This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape. Baseflow GW 1 # of Steps This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape. Baseflow GW 2 Initial Discharge This parameter is varied to match the observed discharge at the beginning of the simulation. Baseflow GW 2 Fraction This parameter is varied to match the observed runoff volume. Baseflow GW 2 Coefficient GW 2 is conceptualized to represent the slow-responding portion of baseflow. Therefore, this coefficient must be set to a larger value than the GW 1 storage coefficient. This value is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape. Baseflow GW 2 # of Steps This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph shape. Streamflow Routing Length This parameter should be estimated using physically measurable characteristics and should not be varied during model calibration within Muskingum-Cunge routing reaches. Physical justification must be provided if using varying values for multiple historic events. Streamflow Routing Slope This parameter should be estimated using physically measurable characteristics and should not be varied during model calibration within Muskingum-Cunge routing reaches. Physical justification must be provided if using varying values for multiple historic events. Streamflow Routing Manning’s “n” values This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph translation and attenuation within Muskingum-Cunge routing reaches. Streamflow Routing Cross Section Shape This parameter should be estimated using physically measurable characteristics and should not be varied during model calibration within Muskingum-Cunge routing reaches. Physical justification must be provided if using varying values for multiple historic events. Streamflow Routing Storage-Outflow Relationship This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph translation and attenuation within Modified Puls routing reaches. Streamflow Routing Number of Steps This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph translation and attenuation within Modified Puls routing reaches. Streamflow Routing K This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph translation within Muskingum routing reaches. Streamflow Routing X This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph attenuation within Muskingum routing reaches. Streamflow Routing Number of Subreaches This parameter is varied to match the observed hydrograph attenuation within Muskingum routing reaches. Snowmelt PX Temperature This parameter is varied to match the observed SWE accumulation. Snowmelt Base Temperature This parameter is varied to match the observed SWE depletion. Snowmelt Wet Meltrate This parameter is varied to match the observed rate of SWE depletion during times when rainfall rates are greater than or equal to the Rain Rate Limit. Snowmelt Rain Rate Limit This parameter is varied to match the observed SWE depletion during times of both rainfall and no rainfall. Snowmelt ATI-Meltrate (Dry) This parameter is varied to match the observed rate of SWE depletion during times when rainfall rates are less than the Rain Rate Limit. Snowmelt ATI-Coldrate This parameter is varied to match the observed timing of the start of SWE melt. Snowmelt Snowpack Water Capacity This parameter is varied to match the observed runoff generation from the melting snowpack. Snowmelt Ground Melt This parameter is typically set to zero and not varied during model calibration. Physical justification must be provided if using a non-zero value.

**

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Calibration Targets
docs.text:
Targets for model calibration are discussed within the main Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document. This includes statistical metrics required for primary, secondary, and tertiary gages. These statistical metrics are applied to event-based stage and flow hydrographs.
