When adequate observed data is available (i.e., at a minimum, daily average streamflow), summary statistics must be used to quantify model performance at all primary, secondary, and tertiary locations (Moriasi, et al., 2007). Required statistical metrics include Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE), Ratio of the Root Mean Square Error to the Standard Deviation (RSR), Percent Bias (PBIAS), and Modified Kling-Gupta Efficiency (MKGE). In addition to summary statistics, model calibration includes a graphical comparison of the modeled stage and/or flow with observed data at all gage locations with available data. These comparisons are included in the calibration process and model adjustments are made as needed.

Observed data that is available for use will vary by location and time period. When available, 15-, 30-, or 60-minute observed streamflow must be used. An example of model calibration using this type of data is shown in Figure 1. When this data is not available, daily average and instantaneous peak streamflow should be used instead, as shown in Figure 2. For locations and/or time periods that utilize daily average observed streamflow, the statistical metrics must be computed using a daily time step. Finally, when all other data is not available, instantaneous peak streamflow can also be used. The use of this data requires integration of regional knowledge for parameter estimation. An example of model calibration using only instantaneous peak streamflow is shown in Figure 3.

Figure 1. Model Calibration Using 15-, 30-, or 60-minute Observed Streamflow

Figure 2. Model Calibration Using Daily Average and Instantaneous Peak Observed Streamflow

Figure 3. Model Calibration Using Instantaneous Peak Observed Streamflow

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Calibration Within Ungaged Basins
docs.text: Look for other sources of observed data. This may require additional coordination with the USACE district partners for USACE or locally managed gage data from city, county or state agencies. If available, stage data can be used in conjunction with a reservoir operations and/or hydraulics model in an iterative process to estimate a flow. More details on this method are discussed in Coordination between HEC-RAS and HEC-HMS modelers during Calibration Process Job Aid document. In the absence of all data, rely on regional parameter estimates.

docs.title: Use of Reasonable and Commonly Accepted Model Parameters
docs.text:
Efforts must be made to ensure that regionally consistent and tractable model calibration techniques are applied. Parameter values based on physical properties can be estimated using GIS tools and can give more reasonable starting estimates since they are physically based. Calibrated constant loss rates can be compared to published infiltration rates based on the predominant hydrologic soil group. Additional scrutiny should be given to any singular event or subbasin with transform parameter relationships (R/Tc+R), constant loss rates, or baseflow coefficients that fall far outside the trend of other subbasins or events. The resources below provide helpful guidance on acceptable parameter values:

  • Applying HEC-HMS GIS Parameter Estimation Tools.

  • Applying the Deficit and Constant Loss Method

  • Estimating Time of Concentration and Storage Coefficient

docs.title: Step 1 – Calibrate Initial Baseflow
docs.text:
Initial baseflow should be varied to match the initial flow on the observed discharge hydrograph. Typically, the linear reservoir baseflow method should be parameterized to provide all initial baseflow from GW layer 2 when using multiple layers. An example of this model calibration step is shown in Figure 4.

Figure 4. Modifying Initial Baseflow

docs.title: Step 2 – Calibrate Initiation of Runoff
docs.text:
Next, modify Loss parameters (Initial Deficit and Constant Rate) and Baseflow parameters (GW 1 Fraction and GW 2 Fraction) to approximately match the initiation of runoff and observed discharge volume, as shown in Figure 5.

Figure 5. Modifying Loss and Baseflow Parameters to Approximately Match Runoff Initiation and Observed Discharge Volume

docs.title: Step 3 – Calibrate Hydrograph Shape
docs.text:
Next, modify Transform method parameters (Tc and R) to approximately match the peak discharge, time of peak discharge, and overall shape of the observed discharge hydrograph. For basins containing several smaller reservoirs that are not feasible to model according to the reservoir modeling guidance, the attenuation due to these structures may need to be addressed through changes to the transform parameters. Additional details on this process can be found in FFRD Small Dams Investigation Report. An example demonstrating this step is shown within Figure 6.

Figure 6. Modifying Transform Parameters to Approximately Match Peak Discharge, Time of Peak Discharge, and Overall Shape of the Observed Discharge Hydrograph

docs.title: Step 4 – Iteratively Modify All Parameters to Achieve Acceptable Statistical Metrics
docs.text: Finally, Loss, Transform, Baseflow, Reach Routing, and Snowmelt (if necessary) parameters should be iteratively modified to achieve acceptable model performance.

docs.title: # Loss Parameter Considerations
docs.text: Deficit and Constant loss parameters should be modified to match the initiation of runoff, observed runoff volume, and peak of the observed discharge hydrograph. Loss parameters should be modified in a regionally consistent and tractable manner. For each subbasin, ensure that the Maximum Deficit and/or Percent Impervious parameter do not vary from one historic event to the other. If they do vary, an adequate physical justification must be provided. Loss parameter modifications should be made in conjunction with baseflow and transform parameters. The following resource provides helpful calibration guidance: Applying the Deficit and Constant Loss Method.

docs.title: # Transform Parameter Considerations
docs.text: Modified Clark transform parameters should be modified to match the observed hydrograph shape and peak discharge magnitude and timing. Transform parameters should be modified in a regionally consistent and tractable manner. Dimensionless coefficients of the transform parameters (e.g., R / [Tc + R]) should be used for regional comparisons. Care should be taken so that the computed direct runoff hydrograph peak occurs prior to the baseflow hydrograph for each subbasin. Also, Tc must be greater than the computational time step while R must be greater than 0.5 * computational time step for all subbasins. Transform parameter modifications should be made in conjunction with the loss and baseflow method parameters. The following resource provides helpful calibration guidance: Time of Concentration and Storage Coefficient Impacts to the Hydrograph.

docs.title: # Baseflow Parameter Consideration
docs.text:
Linear Reservoir baseflow parameters should be modified to match the shape of the receding limb of the observed discharge hydrograph as well as the observed runoff volume. Baseflow parameters should be modified in a regionally consistent and tractable manner.

Within FFRD HEC-HMS models, the linear reservoir baseflow method is typically defined using two groundwater (GW) layers. However, a third layer may be required depending upon physical considerations of the modeling domain in question. The GW 1 layer must be conceptualized as conveying interflow or quick-responding subsurface water while GW 2 layer must be conceptualized as conveying slow responding baseflow. As such, GW 1 parameters must never be used that result in GW 1 outflow preceding direct runoff provided by the transform method. Similarly, GW 2 parameters must never be used that result in GW 2 outflow preceding outflow from GW 1.

Baseflow parameters should be modified in conjunction with the loss and transform method parameters. The following resource provides helpful calibration guidance: Applying the Linear Reservoir Baseflow Method.

docs.title: # Reach Routing Parameter Consideration
docs.text:
Reach routing parameters should be modified to match the observed hydrograph translation and attenuation. Reach routing parameters should be modified in a regionally consistent and tractable manner. Care must be taken to produce outflow hydrographs for all routing reaches that are devoid of oscillations. When using the Modified Puls method, storage-outflow relationships must be extended to avoid errors when simulating events that are significantly larger in magnitude than the historic events chosen for calibration. The following resources provide helpful calibration guidance:

  • Applying the Muskingum Routing Method

  • Applying the Muskingum-Cunge Routing Method

  • Using an HEC-RAS Model to Create Storage-Discharge Curves for Mod-Puls Reaches in HEC-HMS

docs.title: # Snow Parameter Considerations
docs.text: Snow parameters should be modified to match the observed accumulation and melt of SWE in a regionally consistent and tractable manner. Subbasin average SWE time series should be used to calibrate snow parameters. These time series are extracted from observed snow grids as discussed within Job Aid 5 – Gathering and Summarizing Observed Gridded Data and Job Aid 6 – Gathering Observed Gage Data. Initial snow state variables (e.g., initial SWE) should be set using the same observed snow grids, when available. Care should be taken so that the wet melt rate is always greater than or equal to the dry melt rate for antecedent condition for each subbasin. If a groundmelt rate greater than 0 is used for any subbasin, an adequate physical justification must be provided. The following resource provides helpful calibration guidance: Calibrating Gridded Temperature Index Parameters.

docs.title: # Reservoir Modeling Considerations
docs.text:
During the historic event calibration phase, it is acceptable to use the Specified Release reservoir method. However, in preparation for the Conformance and/or Production phases, all reservoirs included within the hydrologic model must utilize the Outflow Curve and/or Rule-Based Operations methods. Simpler reservoir routing methods and parameterizations should be favored to minimize computational burdens. Observed data can be applied to help the model target a storage pattern when using the Rule-Based Operations method or to build an outflow discharge curve when using the Outflow Curve method. The following resources provide helpful reservoir modeling guidance:

  • Using the Specified Release Reservoir Method

  • Using the Outflow Curve Reservoir Method

  • Using the Rule-Based Operations Reservoir Method

docs.title: Reservoir Operations Model Calibration
docs.text:
~~Hydrology and Hydraulics~~

[add content regard calibration and model adjustments commonly used to ensure reservoir operations model matches observed data when appropriate]

docs.title: Role of Calibration for Reservoir Operations Modeling
Primary Model Calibration Parameters
docs.text:
Various model parameters are available within the hydraulic model to achieve a satisfactory calibration to observed data. The following list includes many of the available parameters but should not be considered exhaustive. The parameters are roughly listed in order of their influence; however, many scenarios are unique and what may be an influential parameter in one scenario may have little impact in another. Care should be taken to ensure selected model parameters fall within commonly accepted ranges unless otherwise justified.

  • Channel Bathymetry and Terrain Modification

  • Background and refined mesh resolution

  • Breaklines and Refinement Regions

  • Hydraulic Structures

  • Stream Channel Roughness Coefficients

  • Floodplain Roughness Coefficients

  • Upland Land Cover Roughness Coefficients

  • Model Timestep

  • 2D Equation Set (e.g. diffusion wave, shallow water equations, etc.)

  • Boundary Conditions (including upstream, downstream, and precipitation)

  • 2D Cell Alignment/Orthogonality

  • Hydraulic Property Table Tolerances

Care should be taken to ensure selected model parameters fall within commonly accepted ranges unless otherwise justified. If calibration metrics are found to be difficult to achieve, the underlying cause for the poor fit must be explained, discussed with the team, and documented in reports. This potentially includes discussion of limitations of the calibration statistics and how the model compares with published rating curve data.

docs.title: Calibration targets
docs.text:
Targets for model calibration are discussed within the main Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document. This includes statistical metrics required for primary, secondary, and tertiary gages. These statical metrics are applied to event-based stage and flow hydrographs, however elevation-discharge rating curves should also be evaluated at each gage location. While the rating curve does not require a statistical comparison with observed data, it can provide much information on model performance and a good match to published rating curve is desirable. Additional sources of observed data, such as high-water marks and satellite flood imagery, can be valuable for model validation as they may be available in locations away from established stream gage sites.

In addition to the standard model calibration statistics, model calibration includes a graphical comparison of the modeled stage and discharge with observed data at all gage locations with available data. The modeled stage and discharge are compared with the published rating curve, flow measurements, and annual peak flow data at all gage locations. These comparisons are included in the calibration process and model adjustments are made as needed. Figure 2 provides an example graphical comparison of modeled versus observed water surface elevation and flow hydrographs. Figure 3 provide an example of a graphical comparison between modeled and observed elevation-discharge rating curves.

Figure 3.1. Example Graphical Comparison of Model Results and Observed Data

**

Figure 3.2. Example Graphical Comparison of Model Results with Published Rating Curves and Observed Measurements

docs.title: Ungaged Basins
docs.text:
Observed data may be limited or unavailable for some areas or some event time frames, this constraint makes calibration more difficult as modeled timeseries data cannot be compared with observed timeseries. In these cases, other data may be valuable for calibrating or validating hydraulic model results. Specifically high-water marks, USGS field measurements, satellite or aerial flood imagery, and other data sources can be valuable sources of observed data in ungaged basins. Note that hydraulic models should not be calibrated to flow regression equations, as the frequency of a modeled storm may not translate well to the flow frequency of a specific stream of interest. These regression equations can be compared to model results but are not a valuable calibration data source.

The USGS Flood Event Viewer (available at https://stn.wim.usgs.gov/FEV/) is one central source of flood event data, including high-water marks and rapid-deployment stream gages. Many other local databases of high-water marks may exist as well.

Satellite imagery may be available and provides a spatial snapshot in time from a flood event. Some sources for satellite imagery include the USGS Earth Resources Observation and Science Center (https://www.usgs.gov/centers/eros) and USGS Earth Explorer (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/). Additional discussion is included in Chapter 3 of the main SOP document.

docs.title: Use of Reasonable and Commonly Accepted Model Parameters
docs.text:
If calibration is difficult or impossible to perform using reasonable and commonly accepted model parameters, this may indicate a larger structural problem with the model. It may be possible to force a model to match observed data for a single event with parameters that are outside of the range that would be considered normal for that stream, but the model may not work well on a range of events. It is recommended to stay within a realistic range for model parameters.

For example, if extreme values of channel roughness are required to reproduce observed data, the model may likely be missing some aspect which is impacting model results. In this case, reexamine 2D cell meshing and other factors discussed throughout this document for potential issues. Also consider factors such as gage data reliability, datum conversions, backwater influence, hydraulic structure influence, and/or changed hydraulic conditions which may not be captured by modeling. For extreme floods, it is possible that floodwater flow may flank around the gage location and not be accurately reported in gage records. Similarly, within the hydraulic model, it is possible that reference lines used to report modeled flow and rating curves may not extend fully across the floodplain and may therefore not be capturing all the flow which passes the gage location.

For some model parameters, commonly accepted values may not exist and are actively being researched. This is the case for overland Manning’s roughness coefficients, which are used to define upland areas that experience very shallow flow induced by rainfall. Unlike stream channel and floodplain roughness coefficients which are well understood and documented, there are not established standard values for Manning’s coefficients for very shallow precipitation-driven overland flow. This presents a problem for FFRD hydraulic model development, where overland roughness coefficients are required to describe the rainfall-runoff response across the watershed. Engineering judgement is required for selecting these values and guidance is being developed as the FFRD modeling process matures.

docs.title: Techniques for Hydraulic Model Calibration
docs.text:
There are a variety of approaches which can be taken when calibrating a hydraulic model, refer to other resources such as the HEC-RAS user manual for additional information on this topic. One approach to model calibration which has been successful on the FFRD pilot projects is discussed below and may be useful for hydraulic modelers. The approaches described below are listed in the recommended order they should be performed, however some calibration efforts may require iteration between steps to achieve a satisfactory calibration.

In addition to the steps described below, the following document from the HEC-RAS User Manual provides information on calibration of unsteady flow hydraulic models. While this document is written assuming the use of a 1-dimentional hydraulic model, rather than the 2-dimentional modeling required for FFRD models, many of the principles and concepts still apply and should be considered: https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/rasdocs/rasum/latest/performing-a-1d-unsteady-flow-analysis/calibration-of-unsteady-flow-models

docs.title: Stage-Discharge Rating Curves as Calibration Aids
docs.text:
One of the primary tools useful for performing hydraulic model calibration is graphical examination of the stage-discharge rating curve. An example of such a rating curve is shown in Figure 3 and compares model results versus published USGS data and measurements. Focusing on a rating curve during calibration removes the hydrograph timing variable from consideration and can improve calibration efficiency.

The lower portion of the rating curve defines the flow and stage characteristics of the stream channel while flows are contained within the banks of the river. Using Figure 3 as an example, the in-channel portion of the rating curve is below an elevation of approximately 1,533-ft and a flowrate of approximately 2,000-cfs. The upper portion of the rating curve describes flows which are out of the river channel’s banks and can be heavily influenced by floodplain characteristics. For many riverine systems, the elevation-discharge rating curve will exhibit an inflection point near the top-of-bank elevation where flows exceed channel capacity and begin to activate the floodplain. Terrain DEMs and existing model results can also be used to identify bankfull elevations and channel capacities.

When calibrating a hydraulic model, care should first be taken to calibrate model results to flows contained within the river channel. Assuming global model parameters are reasonable (such as computation timestep), focusing on the lower in-channel portion of the rating curve constrains the model parameters available for calibration. In general, reasonable in-channel model parameters can be selected and set first, before moving forward to calibrate the model to the higher out-of-bank portion of the rating curve. This approach reduces the variables used to calibrate the high-flow portion of the rating curve, increasing efficiency and reducing iteration.

docs.title: 2D Cell Alignment and Spacing
docs.text: It is generally a best practice for 2D cell faces to be aligned with the direction of flow as best as is practicable. This can be done through a combination of breaklines, refinement regions, and manual manipulation of 2D cell points. 2D cell face alignment with flow has benefits of computational efficiency and stability, and it can also impact modeled water surface elevations and velocities. 2D cell faces should be aligned to flow prior to detailed calibration efforts. This alignment is also an opportunity to ensure terrain features near the channel are appropriately captured by the 2D cell faces. Features such as levees (both natural floodplain deposits and human-made levees), as well as other distinct features along banklines can be captured using breaklines and refinement region boundaries. These is also a convenient time to adjust cell spacing within the stream channel. Due to the large number of streams present in FFRD hydraulic modeling units, it may not be practical to align all 2D cells to stream flow. Larger streams and those near developed areas should be prioritized for cell alignment, this includes streams identified as key and secondary streams.

docs.title: Estimation of Channel Bathymetry
docs.text:
If high-quality channel bathymetry is available for a given reach, this simplifies calibration efforts associated with that reach as bathymetry does not need to be estimated. If bathymetry is not available, the terrain DEM likely represents the river channel with the water surface filled-in to what the water level was when LiDAR data was collected. For small streams where flow depths are usually small, there may not be a need to approximate terrain bathymetry as it is already well enough defined by the terrain DEM. However for larger rivers, when the channel bathymetry is not reflected in the terrain, channel conveyance can be significantly underestimated and there is a need to approximate the bathymetry. Bathymetry estimation is one of several parameters available for calibrating modeled results to a published rating curve.

When channel bathymetry needs to be approximated, the HEC-RAS terrain modifications can be used. These linear channel modifications require several parameters be estimated, namely: a profile of channel invert elevations, channel shape, and channel width. One way to approximate the channel invert elevation profile is to export a terrain profile from a stream centerline drawn on the terrain DEM which does not have bathymetry estimated. This stream profile exported from the LiDAR based DEM can then be lowered by a specified height to estimate the depth of water under the LiDAR based DEM stream surface. To inform this depth of water, the lowest portion of the elevation-discharge rating curve can be used. As an example, in Figure 3, the channel invert is approximately elevation 1,528-ft, as this is the elevation corresponding to zero discharge. If the terrain DEM indicates a channel invert of 1,531-ft with no bathymetry estimated, the flow depth required to reach the channel invert indicated by the rating curve is 3-ft. Therefore 3-ft can be subtracted from the stream elevation profile which was based on the LiDAR terrain. While this approach of subtracting a constant height from the LiDAR elevation profile is simplistic, it can be an appropriate technique in the absence of more detailed bathymetric information.

For many streams, the channel’s cross-sectional shape can be approximated as a trapezoid. The bottom width and side slopes of this trapezoid can be estimated from examination of LiDAR based terrain and aerial imagery. An example of a channel modification to estimate stream bathymetry is shown in Figure 3.3. In this example, channel bottom width and side slopes were estimated from a LiDAR DEM, and the channel station-elevation profile was adjusted to achieve a channel invert as indicated by a published USGS rating curve. Note the offset (3-ft in this example) present between the LiDAR terrain DEM and the estimated bathymetric stream profile.**

Figure 3.3. Example Channel Modification to Estimate Bathymetry Along a Stream

More sophisticated approaches are possible for estimating bathymetry, and when data is readily available, these approaches may be merited. In some cases, it may be necessary or desirable to use more detailed bathymetric estimation approaches, such as variable cross-sectional shapes, or nested channel modifications to produce a terraced floodplain. When reliable surveyed bathymetry is available, it should be incorporated into modeling and used rather than the approximate terrain modification estimation approach discussed here.

Note on Terrain Modifications in RAS Mapper

Terrain modifications in RAS Mapper take advantage of vector editing tools to create points, polylines, and polygons to modify an existing RAS terrain. These tools are useful for incorporating bathymetry into a terrain surface, as well as many other uses. Additional information regarding the use of terrain modifications is disused in this document, but also in the HEC-RAS Mapper User Manual: https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/rasdocs/rmum/latest/terrain-modification

docs.title: Selection of Channel Roughness Values
docs.text:
Once channel bathymetry is estimated and its parameters (e.g. cross-section shape, width, and depth) are constrained by reasonable values (determined from the LiDAR DEM/aerial imagery), channel roughness can be estimated. This Manning’s n roughness value is a calibration parameter and should be selected to match modeled water surface and flow results to a published rating curve. Specifically the roughness value used for the stream channel should be used to match the lower portion of the rating curve where flows are contained in-channel. Manning’s n calibration regions can be used in HEC-RAS to define specific n values in specific areas. It is often desirable to buffer a stream centerline to the width of the stream channel such that areas wetted during low flows have their own uniquely defined roughness coefficients.

Through modification of bathymetry estimates and channel roughness, the in-channel portion of the rating curve should be made to match well to published rating curve data. If calibration to the in-channel portion of published rating curves cannot be achieved using commonly accepted roughness values, this may indicate a larger structural problem with the model. Reexamine 2d cell meshing and stream bathymetry for potential issues. Also consider factors such as gage data reliability, backwater influence, hydraulic structure influence, boundary conditions, and/or changed hydraulic conditions which may not be captured by modeling.

Note on Spatially Varied Manning’s Roughness

For FFRD models where 2D cell sizes may be large, enabling the Spatially Varied Manning’s n on Faces option for 2D areas is recommended and can improve the fidelity of hydraulic roughness on long cell faces. When enabled this option varies the Manning’s roughness coefficient as a function of elevation depending on the relationship between a 2D cell face and spatially varied Manning’s n layer. This option is briefly described in the HEC-RAS user manual here: https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/rasdocs/r2dum/latest/development-of-a-2d-or-combined-1d-2d-model/creating-hydraulic-property-tables-for-2d-flow-areas

docs.title: Defining Floodplain Geometry with Breaklines
docs.text: Once the in-channel portion of the rating curve matches well to observed data, focus can be shifted towards representing the floodplain. Capturing terrain features in the floodplain, such as embankments, berms, ridges, and other impediments to flow is of critical importance to accurately representing flood behavior. This is especially true for FFRD RAS models, which may use large cell sizes that can easily misrepresent terrain features if proper breaklining is not performed. There are known limitations of the current RAS 2D meshing system which result in 2D cell faces not snapping properly to defined breaklines. Modelers must take care to ensure cell faces are snapped and that breaklines are properly enforced. Conflicts often arise where multiple breaklines intersect one another, these areas require special attention and manual manipulation of 2D cell points from modelers to ensure that high ground is captured by cell faces.

docs.title: Selection of Floodplain Roughness Values
docs.text:
Once floodplain terrain features are represented by the 2D computational mesh, final calibration to the rating curve can be performed through modification of floodplain roughness coefficients. This effort is focused on the upper portion of the elevation-discharge rating curve where elevations exceed bank-full, as the in-channel portion of the rating curve should be established by previous steps discussed above. Additional information on selection of these roughness values can be found in the main SOP document, section 8.9.6 – Roughness Values. Like the in-channel regions of the model, RAS calibration regions can be used to define specific areas where Manning’s n values are modified. Also see the note above regarding the use of spatially varied Manning’s roughness, which can be useful in calibrating floodplain model results.

If calibration to the floodplain portion of published rating curves cannot be achieved using commonly accepted roughness values, this may indicate a larger structural problem with the model. Refer to section 3.2.2 for discussion of reasonable and commonly accepted model parameters.

docs.title: Flow Hydrograph Timing and Magnitude
docs.text: Once the hydraulic model is sufficiently reproducing the published elevation-discharge rating curve, flow hydrographs can be examined for proper timing and magnitude. These variables can be influenced by many factors, including: upstream roughness value selection (in the stream channel, floodplain, and upland areas), upstream mesh resolution, refinement, breaklining, 2D equation set selection, and to a lesser degree, hydraulic property table tolerance and other variables. Hydrograph timing and magnitude are also heavily influenced by HEC-HMS hydrologic model outputs, namely gridded excess precipitation and baseflow hydrographs. The interaction of all these variables is complex and engineering experience and judgement are required to achieve satisfactory calibration. To achieve successful hydraulic calibration, it may be necessary in some instances to modify hydrologic model parameters which serve as the primary inputs to the hydraulic model. This should be discussed with the technical team lead and hydrologic modeler.

docs.title: Conformance Phase
docs.text:
Models are calibrated to historic events in isolation during the calibration phase of the project. During the conformance phase of the project, the full system of models are linked in such a way that an adjustment to the parameters of one model can affect the outputs from a separate model. For this reason, coordination across modeling teams in necessary to optimize the performance and accuracy of the full model system rather than any single model.

The sections below discuss best practices for coordination between the modeling team as well as some of the specific feedback loops that are anticipated in the FFRD program.

  • HMS and RAS Coordination: Can RAS be calibrated successfully using HMS outputs? RAS modelers must provide Feedback to HMS lead and attempt to resolve

  • Differences in Dam Representation in HMS & RAS.

  • HMS runoff response vs. RAS runoff response: Comparison of subbasin hydrographs to ensure volume and timing match reasonably well. SOP calls for RAS to include a reference line at each subbasin outflow location, so that a direct comparison can be made

  • RAS Pool elevations vs. ResSim Pool elevations should match reasonably well (there are different situations where this comparison can be made for historic events, but this will always be a valid comparison for SST events.

  • Etc

After key target calibration metrics are achieved, the models are computed across the frequency range and results are compared to observed annual maximum series at primary, secondary, and tertiary gages. This compute consists of one realization of at least 500 synthetic years as outlined in the Model Linking Job Aid. If the frequency output is biased high or low at this stage (exceeding confidence limits on the observed data), the rainfall runoff model likely needs to be modified to account for calibration across the entire frequency range. Figure 12.1 presents an example of an annual maximum series compared to a gage location that represents a significant bias. This bias was corrected by updating the HEC-HMS model (the wrong parameterization of the basin model was utilized in the first set of simulations in this case). The correct modifications to models are handled on a case-by-case basis as they must be considered within the context of the broader watershed to evaluate the appropriate fix for any observed conformance to frequency curves.

docs.title: Model Integration Actions
docs.text: [the model integration process in terms of data management and workflow for FFRD is described in on other sections. Refer to this without replicating here]

docs.title: Historic Events Replication
docs.text: After all previously calibrated models have been integrated into the watershed modeling system, the historic events are run to the system to ensure the simulated results adequately match observations. These simulations may result in identification of model compatibility concerns. For instance, a relatively small error in at the outflow of an upstream RAS model could propagate downstream and demonstrate increasing errors. This is also an opportunity to validate that all model linkages are properly defined.

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Parameter Sets and Initial States
docs.text: Details pertaining to the preparation of hydrologic models and pertinent data for use within the Conformance Phase are contained within Job Aid 11 – Model Delivery for Cloud Compute.

docs.title: Hydraulic Model Initial Conditions
docs.text: [content here]

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Routing vs. Hydraulic Model Routing
docs.text: [models should align in un-regulated basins]

docs.title: Frequency Calibration
docs.text:
After a long-term simulation is executed through the watershed modeling system, the frequency relationships for various parameters can be compared to observed relationships. The parameters for comparison are described in Chapter 12 of the SOP and metrics for achieving acceptable conformance to those parameters are describe in a separate job aid. The sections below discuss considerations for model adjustments within the watershed modeling system to improve the conformance metrics.

To address model conformance to observed data and expected results, the modeling team should start with the upstream models (i.e. Precipitation to Hydrology to Consequences) to identify the source of any non-conforming results. The downstream models must not be over-fit to observations if the simulated inputs to those models are not adequate.

docs.title: Precipitation
docs.text:
Compare simulated precipitation (from SST precipitation-only runs?) to the observed basin-averaged precipitation based on the AORC dataset. Extract the annual maximum series of precipitation using HEC-DSSVue. Perform the analysis and comparison for the 1 hour, 24 hour and 72 hour precipitation annual maximum series. Reference the Generating Subbasin Averaged Precipitation AMS With HEC-HMS Guide for guidance on this process.

[start with simulated precipitation comparison to observed basin-average values directly from the AORC dataset. Consider 1 hour, 24 hour and 72 hour max precip]

[describe adjustments available]

docs.title: Liquid Water at Soil Surface Frequency
docs.text: • Extract the subbasin averaged time series of liquid water at the soil surface (LWASS) from the period of record simulation results using HEC-Vortex utility. Reference the Grid to Point Converter Tutorial for guidance on this process.Extract the liquid water at soil surface (LWASS) annual maximum series from the PoR simulation using HEC-DSSVue or HEC-SSP. Compare the PoR simulation results to the SST simulated results.

docs.title: Hydrologic Model Flow Frequency
docs.text: Adjustments can be made to calibrate the HEC-HMS simulated flow frequency to the observed flow frequency. Using monthly average values for both computed and observed flows and SWE can help capture general trends over a long-term simulation. Adjustments to PX and Base Temperature parameters can improve agreement of monthly SWE averages. Adjustments to loss rates and baseflow parameters can be made to improve agreement in monthly flow averages. Next, visually inspect the two datasets. Plot the observed vs. simulated annual maximum series on an x-y plot, a perfect fit would have a regression line slope of 1. Plot the two flow frequency curves. Adjustments to constant loss rate, storage coefficient, and groundwater 1 fraction will likely have the largest impact on peak flows. There should also be consideration on where to prioritize the calibration of the flow modeled flow frequency curve. In general flood risk is concerned with the upper-end (less frequent) portion of the curve. Some guidance for the process of calibrating to a flow frequency curve can be found here: Calibrating a Continuous Simulation Model

docs.title: Job Aid 13—Model Delivery for Cloud Compute
Worflow Context
docs.text:
This job aid describes the specific requirements and steps for delivery of models for cloud compute of synthetic events during the Conformance Phase. The model integration lead is responsible for delivery and verification of quality control (QC) checks of all models to the cloud as well as the associated final Model Linking Register that includes all inputs required for successful development of cloud compute manifests and execution in the cloud.

The model integration lead is also responsible for several steps that must occur in the desktop environment to validate accurate linking of all models and to confirm model performance (which are not described in this job aid). The pre-conformance review checklist must be completed with all items closed to ensure the linked system of models has been validated and all cloud-preparation steps have been completed.

Figure 1. Models are delivered for cloud-compute during the Conformance Phase of the project, the third phase of the FFRD Workflow.

Figure 2. Focus of this Job Aid in relation to steps within the Conformance Phase.

docs.title: Final Model Linking Register Delivery
docs.text:
A fully developed Name Table, Model Linking Register, Dam Scoping Table, and Levee Breach Locations are developed during the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase. These products provide the guide for model linking strategy throughout the FFRD project. They may be updated during the Calibration Phase or Conformance Phase of the project when unique issues are identified.

The Model Linking Register is finalized early in the conformance phase when the full system of hydraulic models are linked in a desktop environment and tested. This Model Linking Register is an easily readable table that will contain all information required to build cloud-compute manifests described in detail in a separate job aid. These cloud-compute manifests control the actual linking of output data to input data that is summarized in the Model Linking Register with cloud-compute. Several cloud-compute manifests are required and include those for actions included in various plug-ins (i.e., hms-mutator-plugin, hms-runner, ressim-runner, fragility-curve-plugin, and ras-runner)

Scripting to automate certain steps in the production of the compute-manifests has the potential to improve efficiency and ability to QC linking data.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
The desktop version of the model, prior to upload to the cloud should include internal referencing to input files as shown in the schematic below. This structure mimics the directory structure of how the output data from individual events are stored in Model Library by cloud-compute, which allows for efficient viewing and replication of cloud results in later steps.

Note that the terrain is stored at a higher-level directory where all models can share it.

The location for upload of the models for cloud-compute is not shown in this figure. The actual model upload locations are described separate from this document (unique for each model).

Figure 3. Model Library Output Data Directory Structure to be Mimicked on Desktop Prior to Model Upload. Not the Location for Model Upload for Cloud-Compute

docs.title: Naming Convention Control
docs.text:
The FFRD SOP includes robust specific criteria for naming conventions that apply to model file names and internal model element names. These naming conventions are critical for the ability to control model linkages, perform various QC steps, and reliably access output data among other uses.

The most critical moment in the FFRD process to ensure naming conventions follow the standards is at model upload for cloud compute.

Although the SOP includes various review steps and checklists to address naming convention issues, there is opportunity for human error. Automated processes to check for adherence to naming conventions using scripts has the potential to reliably catch issues and ensure consistency across the FFRD program.

docs.title: Time Window for Stochastic Storm Transposition Events
docs.text:
The model integration lead will guide the event time window duration selection as described in the main SOP. It will be long enough to ensure rainfall in the upper basin has sufficient travel time to so that the downstream boundary of the model reaches a peak.

A specific starting date and time will also be selected for use for the SST events and model preparation for cloud compute, but it will be varied for each event within cloud-compute.

docs.title: Hydrologic Modeling System Model Delivery
docs.text:
The following sections serve as a checklist for transforming a calibrated HEC-HMS project and preparing it for Conformance and Production simulations using cloud-compute.

The synthetic event sampling of initial states and basin parameter sets is based on the HEC-HMS modeler or model integration lead delivering a large dataset .basin and .control files that represent possible combinations of conditions and parameter sets on each day throughout the period of record used for stochastic storm transposition (SST).

docs.title: Software Version
docs.text: The HEC-HMS software version used for model development must be verified and consistent with the version called for in Section 6 of SOP Volume II.

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text:
First, use the File>Save As... option within the HEC-HMS user interface to create a copy of the project. Use the Copy DSS data (time-series, paired, grid data) option to internalize any externally referenced HEC-DSS data as shown in the Figure 4. Note, this process does not copy external data like .asc or .tif files.

Figure 4. HEC-HMS Save Current Project As Dialog

docs.title: Model Settings
docs.text: The following subsections describe modifications that must be made to HEC-HMS model files and project configurations to support stochastic simulations during the Conformance and Production phases.

docs.title: Basin Models
docs.text: Several modifications must be made to the basin models, which were created during the historic event calibration phase. In general, initial conditions for all basin elements (e.g., subbasins, reaches, and reservoirs) must be specified in absolute, not relative terms. This enables efficient stochastic sampling and specification of their values.

docs.title: Subbasins
docs.text:
The Structured Discretization method must be used. The standard hydrologic grid (SHG) projection must be used along with an appropriate cell size (e.g., 2,000 meters), as shown in Figure 5.

Figure 5. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Discretization Component Editor

The Simple Canopy method must be used. The Depth Initial Storage Method must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 6 shows an example of these options.

Figure 6. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Canopy Component Editor

Note that the initial storage depth will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

If snow accumulation and melt processes are relevant to the watershed of interest, the Gridded Temperature Index Snow method must be used (otherwise, this process should be set to None). For each initial state variable, the Parameter Grid option must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 7 shows an example of these options.

Figure 7. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Snow Component Editor

Note that the parameter grids for each state variable will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

If surface storage processes are relevant to the watershed of interest, the Simple Surface method must be used (otherwise, this process should be set to None). The Depth Initial Storage Method must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 8 shows an example of these options.

Note that the initial storage depth will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

The Deficit and Constant Loss method must be used. All parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 9 shows an example of these options.

Note that the initial deficit will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

The ModClark Transform method must be used. All parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 10 shows an example of these options.

Figure 8. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Surface Component Editor

Figure 9. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Loss Component Editor

Figure 10. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Transform Component Editor

The Linear Reservoir Baseflow method must be used. The Discharge Initial Type must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 11 shows an example of these options.

Figure 11. HEC-HMS Subbasin, Baseflow Component Editor

Note that the GW1 and GW2 Initial discharge values will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

docs.title: Reaches
docs.text:
Depending upon the physical processes within the watershed of interest, multiple reach routing methods may be utilized within HEC-HMS FFRD applications. Regardless of the chosen routing method, the Specified Discharge Initial Type must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 12 shows an example of these options.

Figure 12. HEC-HMS Routing Component Editor

Note that the initial discharge values will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

docs.title: Reservoirs
docs.text:
Depending upon the physical processes within the watershed of interest, multiple reservoir routing methods may be utilized within HEC-HMS FFRD applications. Regardless of the chosen routing method, the Elevation Initial Condition must be used. All other parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged. Figure 12 shows an example of these options.

Figure 13. HEC-HMS Reservoir Component Editor

Note that the initial elevation values will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations.

docs.title: Meteorologic Model
docs.text:
Unlike the basin models, a brand-new meteorologic model named, SST, should be created for use during the Conformance and Production phases. The Metric Unit System must be specified (this is used when transposing the gridded boundary conditions). For all FFRD applications, the Gridded Precipitation method must be selected. If snow accumulation/melt or evapotranspiration processes are relevant to the watershed of interest, the Gridded Temperature method must be selected (otherwise, this process should be set to None). Similarly, if evapotranspiration processes are relevant to the watershed of interest, the Gridded Hamon Evapotranspiration method must be selected (otherwise, this process should be set to None). The Replace Missing option should be specified as Set to Default. Figure 14 shows an example of these options.

Figure 14. HEC-HMS Meteorology Model Component Editor

Within the Gridded Precipitation node, any valid precipitation grid name can be chosen. These datasets will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations. The Normalize Start Time Shift Method must be selected. The Transpose option should be set to Yes. Default values near the middle of the watershed in the basin model's coordinate reference system should be specified within the X and Y Coordinate entry fields. Figure 15 shows an example of these options.

Figure 15. HEC-HMS Meteorology Model, Gridded Precipitation Component Editor

If the Gridded Temperature method was selected, within the Gridded Temperature node, any valid temperature grid name can be chosen. These datasets will be stochastically sampled and set during conformance and production simulations. The Normalize Start Time Shift Method must be selected. Figure 16 shows an example of these options.

Figure 16. HEC-HMS Meteorology Model, Gridded Temperature Component Editor

If the Gridded Hamon method was selected, all parameter selections/values achieved during model calibration should be left unchanged within the Gridded Hamon node. Figure 17 shows an example of these options.

Figure 17. HEC-HMS Meteorology Model, Gridded Hamon Component Editor

docs.title: Control Specifications
docs.text:
Similar to the meteorologic model, a brand-new Control Specification model, named SST, should be created for use during the Conformance and Production phases. 01Jan2100 should be entered within the Start Date entry field. 00:00 should be entered within the Start Time entry field. 04Jan2100 should be entered within the End Date entry field. 00:00 should be entered within the End Time entry field. Depending upon the physical processes within the watershed of interest, multiple computational time steps may be utilized within HEC-HMS FFRD applications. Figure 18 shows an example of these options.

Figure 18. HEC-HMS Control Specifications Component Editor

docs.title: Simulation Runs
docs.text:
Similar to the control specifications, a brand-new simulation run model, named SST, should be created for use during the Conformance and Production phases. Any basin model can be selected within this simulation as they will be updated during the conformance and production simulations. The SST meteorologic model and SST control specifications (which were previously created) should be selected. The Spatial Results option should be set to Yes and the Spatial Interval should be set to 1 Hour. Figure 19 shows an example of these options.

Figure 19. HEC-HMS Simulation Run Component Editor

The No Ratio method should be selected on the Ratio tab. Additionally, the No Save States option should be selected on the States tab.

docs.title: Importing Precipitation Data (Storm Catalog)
docs.text:
The tool used for generating storm catalogs produces a .grid file that follows the specification of a normal HEC-HMS .grid file that links gridded datasets to a project. This lets you quickly copy and paste a list of entries for hundreds of storms into your project and effectively import them without needing to manually create hundreds of gridsets in HEC-HMS.

For the steps below, the project.grid file is the .grid file that exists in the HEC-HMS project and matches its name. It contains all the grid data used in calibration and POR simulations The catalog.grid file is the one that was created by StormCloud/StormHub and only contains the precipitation data for storms in the storm catalog.

The instructions below assume you are using Notepad++ to edit the files.

Make sure your HEC-HMS project is closed before editing any internal files.

  1. Copy (or move) the storm data .dss files to the project data folder in a subdirectory called catalog.

  2. Make a backup copy of the catalog.grid file and give it the file extension .gridbak with the same filename as the original catalog.grid file.

  3. Open the original (not backup) catalog.grid file in a plaintext editor.

  4. Find and replace file paths to point to the project's catalog folder.

  1. Scroll down to the first grid data entry in the catalog.grid file and find the DSS File Name entry.

  2. Highlight the portion of the file path up to the last backslash (just before the file name), as shown in Figure 20.

Figure 20. Section of Text to Replace within .grid File

  1. Press Ctrl+H to open the Replace dialog. Note that it automatically places the highlighted text in the Find what field.

  2. In the Replace with field, enter data\catalog.

  3. Click Replace All on the right side.

  1. Save the catalog.grid file but leave it open.

  2. Make a backup copy of the project.grid file and give it the file extension .gridbak with the same filename as the original project.grid file.

  3. Open the original (not backup) project.grid file in a plaintext editor in a new window.

  4. Scroll to the bottom of the project.grid file and find the last grid entry. Add a blank line after the "End:" tag for the last grid, and then press Enter again to go to the following line.

  1. Each entry must be separated by a blank line.
  1. From the catalog.grid file, copy all the storm grid entries and paste them into the project.grid file below the blank line you added.
  1. Make sure not to copy the catalog.grid file's header (the first four lines beginning with "Grid Manager: ...”)
  1. Save the project.grid file. Close both .grid files.

When you re-open your HEC-HMS project, you will see that hundreds of new gridsets are listed in the Watershed Explorer.

docs.title: Model Cleanup
docs.text: The HEC-HMS project used during the Conformance and Production phases should be as lean as possible because it will be copied many times and reproducing unnecessary data is a waste. Below are several things that help reduce the size of the SST project. Most of the cleanup can happen within the HEC-HMS user interface (e.g., deleting basin models) but there are hints below where deleting a large number of entries using the HEC-HMS input files is faster (i.e., within Notepad++). Always be careful when editing HEC-HMS input files and make a backup before doing so.

docs.title: Basin Models
docs.text: First, remove any basin models except for the finalized event calibration basin models. Also, remove any linkages to observed data (e.g., flow, stage, SWE) on the Options tab (set to none) of all basin elements (e.g., subbasins, junctions, reaches, etc.) as this information will not be utilized during stochastic simulations.

docs.title: Meteorologic Models
docs.text: Next, remove any met models except for the SST met model you created in the steps above.

docs.title: Control Specifications
docs.text: Also, remove control specifications except for SST from the project.

docs.title: Time-Series Data
docs.text:
It is unlikely that you will need any time-series data if you have unlinked all your observed data, are using gridded processes, and do not have any specified discharge elements (reservoirs, sources, etc.) in the model. If you are confident that you need certain time-series data, do not delete them.

The following process removes the links from the HEC-HMS project, but you will need to clean up the DSS files manually. The DSS files that contain the time-series data were copied over during the initial Save As step. You can use the File>References tool and filter to Time Series Data to see where these data are on disk and delete them manually. They should have been internalized to the copied project's data folder and are safe to delete because they are copies of the original.

  1. Make a backup copy of the project.gage file that exists in your HEC-HMS project and matches its name. Give it the file extension .gagebak with the same filename as the original project.gage file.

  1. Open the original (not backup) project.gage in a plaintext editor.

  2. Delete everything below the file header (first 5 lines).

docs.title: Paired Data
docs.text:
Unlike time-series data, it is very likely that several paired data objects are utilized within the basin models that will be used during the stochastic simulations. Thus, care needs to be taken to organize these paired data objects in such a way that any errors or compute issues can be efficiently investigated during the conformance and production phases.

Begin by culling any unused paired data objects (i.e., storage-discharge, elevation-storage, etc.) within the HEC-HMS project. Then, for the remaining paired data references that utilize external DSS files, combine all records into a single DSS file. This paired data DSS file should be placed within the project's data folder. For any paired data references that utilize the Manual Data Entry method, these records can remain unaltered.

docs.title: Simulation Runs
docs.text:
Remove any simulation runs or any other computes (e.g., Uncertainty Analysis, Optimization Trial, etc.) from the project except for the SST run you created. Other compute types can be removed by deleting directory within the base project directory (with the HEC-HMS project closed):

  • Delete the montecarlo directory to delete all Uncertainty Analyses.

  • Delete the optimizer directory to delete all Optimization Trials.

  • Delete the ensemble directory to delete all Ensemble Analyses.

  • Delete the forecast directory to delete all Forecast Alternatives.

  • Delete the frequency directory to delete all Frequency Analyses.

docs.title: DSS Files in the Project Directory
docs.text:
There will be some straggler files associated with now-deleted simulations to clean up. Delete any DSS files with a name that matches a meteorologic model which was removed. These files contain a met data cache and can be removed. You can verify this is the case by opening the DSS file in DSSVue and verifying:

  • It only contains meteorologic data.

  • The F-part for all paths contains "MET:xxxxx" where "xxxxx" is the name of the met model.

Delete any DSS files with a name that matches a simulation run which was removed. These files contain a They can be deleted. You can verify this is the case by opening the DSS file in DSSVue and verifying:

  • It only contains simulation results.

  • The F-part for all paths contains "RUN:xxxxx" where "xxxxx" is the name of the simulation run.

Delete any DSS files with a name that matches other simulation types (e.g., Uncertainty Analysis, Optimization Trial, etc.) can be deleted as well.

docs.title: Other Files in the Project Directory
docs.text: All files with the .out and .log extensions can be safely deleted. Additionally, all .sqlite files that do not match one of the basin models that remain within the HEC-HMS project can be deleted. Finally, the results directory can be safely deleted.

docs.title: Cleaning Up GIS Data
docs.text: There will not be modification of the geospatial information for any basin element, so the source or processed terrain data will no longer be necessary. Thus, the gis, maps, and terrain directories should be deleted.

docs.title: Developing Continuous Initial Snow States
docs.text:
This process is only required for projects that utilize the Gridded Temperature Index snow method:

  1. First, re-run the period of record with full spatial results turned on to get full set of gridded state variables for snow.
  1. Second, sanitize the output gridded data sets using the Vortex | Sanitizer tool.

docs.title: HEC-HMS Model Delivery
docs.text: The prepared and cleaned up model files must be delivered by posting them to the appropriate location on the project directory. There are two distinct deliveries associated with the HEC-HMS model delivery: the runnable model and template files for use by cloud-compute tools to generate input files that account for variability in antecedent conditions for each stochastic event.

docs.title: Base Model
docs.text:
Post the prepared and runnable HEC-HMS models to the following location within the project directory:

Delivery Location: conformance > hydrology > [basin-name]

Figure 21. Example Delivered HMS Model Dataset

docs.title: Antecedent Condition Template Files
docs.text:
The basin-record-gen-plugin cloud-compute actions take HMS template files to create an array of .basin and .control files to represent the variation in antecedent conditions for stochastic events. The input template files to be delivered are:

  • Calibrated Period of Record HECHMS DSS output

  • Individual .basin files from each calibration event

  • Individual .control files from each calibration event

The template files that will be used by cloud compute will be posted to the location below.

Delivery Location: conformance > basin-generation

docs.title: HEC-ResSim Delivery
docs.text: The following sections serve to describe how to prepare and deliver the HEC-ResSim FFRD model for conformance phase and production phase runs using Cloud Compute.

docs.title: Software Version
docs.text: The HEC-ResSim version used for model development must be verified and consistent with the version called for in Section 7 of SOP Volume II.

docs.title: Create the Conformance Run Template
docs.text:
A single alternative and a single simulation shall be the starting point for the conformance model:

  • Conformance alternative name=“fema_ffrd”

  • Conformance simulation name=“fema_ffrd”

docs.title: Conformance Alternative
docs.text:
The simplest way to create the “fema_ffrd” alternative is to make a copy of one of the existing calibration alternatives.

  1. From the Reservoir Network Module, select the Alternative Editor, click on one of the calibration runs, and use the Save As… option.
  1. Save as “fema_ffrd” and provide an informative Description such as “FEMA FFRD Cloud Compute Alternative – Originated as a copy of April 1990 calibration alternative for use with ffrd cloud computes”

Figure 22. Step 2 Window

  1. No other changes should be necessary but ensure that the appropriate timestep and flow computation method are selected. The inflow mapping must be consistent with the simulation date range and timestep.

docs.title: Conformance Simulation
docs.text:
Next move to the Simulation Module and create the new “fema_ffrd” simulation.

  1. As previously state, it is easiest to start with the calibration simulation that was paired with the alternative used to create the “fema_ffrd” alternative, because the simulation dates must match the available data. First open the matching calibration simulation, and use the right-click secret menu to copy the Simulation Time Window.

Figure 23. Step 1 Window

  1. Use the Simulation>Create a New Simulation option to create the fema_ffrd simulation, and give it an informative description such as, “FEMA Future of Flood Risk Data (FFRD) Cloud Compute Simulation.”

  2. Use the secret right-click Paste TimeWindow option to make the simulation time window match the currently available data. The cloud compute modeler will automate the updates to these dates to match what is sampled during the production runs.

  3. Run the simulation to ensure that nothing has broken, and note the total compute time for the report.

Figure 24. Step 4 Window

docs.title: Within Model Cleanup
docs.text:
As with HEC-HMS, the HEC-ResSim watershed used during the Conformance and Production phases should be as lean as possible because it will be copied many times and reproducing unnecessary data is a waste. While some model-cleanup was done during the calibration modeling, the following steps shall be taken to ensure the most compact, streamlined model for conformance and production.

Note

Always be careful when directly editing HEC-ResSim model files and make a backup before doing so.

These instructions assume you have already done some cleanup and thorough documentation during earlier phases, e.g., removed all unnecessary networks and scripts, and any particularly messy, disorganized pieces of the model.

docs.title: Create a Zipped Backup Model
docs.text:
Before zipping up your model, make sure the current state has been fully documented. Use the File>Watershed Info… feature to log a comment about the current state of the model.

Figure 25. Zipped Backup Model Window

Then completely close the model and all associated files before creating a zipfile back up. Now that there is a backup of the model, delete as much superfluous files as possible.

docs.title: Remove All Simulations except “fema_ffrd”
docs.text:
Reopen the model and go to the Simulation Module. Delete all simulations except fema_ffrd, using the Simulation>Delete Simulation option.

Figure 26. Delete Simulation Window

docs.title: Delete all Alternatives except fema_ffrd
docs.text: From the Network Module, Alternative Editor, deleted all but the fema_ffrd alternative. There is no bulk delete option, but Ctrl+D can be used to make this process slightly faster.

docs.title: Remove all Unnecessary Outputs in fema_ffrd alternative
docs.text:
Generally, only simulated flow and elevation outputs are required to be saved. The outputs that are required for FFRD simulations either feed directly to a HEC-RAS model, aid in identification interesting events (event filtering), or provide the required data to develop a relevant frequency curve for conformance metrics.

The DSS Output tab in the alternative writes all computed and interpolated time series by default. Prior to handoff, the user should uncheck both of these in the Alternative Editor. The user should also go a step further and uncheck additional items on the subtabs. On the Reservoir tab, the user should only need the Reservoir Pool and flows so all other outputs can be unchecked. The user can also uncheck all outputs on the Reaches and Diversion tabs. For Junctions and State Variables, the user may want to uncheck some outputs; however, it is recommended that any record that may be referenced during validation (such as downstream control points or data handoff points between models) should remain selected.

Figure 27. Alternative Editor Window

docs.title: Excess Rules and Operation Sets
docs.text:
Rules and operation sets create very little extra bulk to model file size, but can create clutter and confusion. To alleviate this, during the Calibration Phase, ensure that the intention behind rules was included in their descriptions, and that the operation sets used for FFRD runs were clearly marked. Cleaning up unneeded operation sets from a model with 16 reservoirs, each with 3 operation sets required 208 clicks but did not result in any noticeable file size reduction, so the modeler should focus on tidiness in this case, and may leave extra operation sets in the model without causing harm.

Figure 28. Operation Set Deletion Window

docs.title: Delete Unnecessary Map Layers
docs.text: From the View>Layers menu in any module, remove any map layers that are unneeded. Do not remove the Stream Alignment or Study layers or associated subparts.

docs.title: Manual Model Cleanup
docs.text: Next you will remove remaining excess files via the Windows File Explorer or similar. Document changes in the WatershedInfo file and close the watershed and associated files. The final cleanup step is the most dangerous. As a precaution, zip a copy of the model again.

docs.title: Map Files
docs.text:
Remove unneeded map shapefiles from the maps directory via the File Explorer. For example:

  • highways

  • project points.

docs.title: Shared Directory Files
docs.text: The watershed shared directory holds inputs and other shared files. Remove the files not needed.

docs.title: Networks, Alternatives, Simulations
docs.text:
Remove remnant files associated with the networks, alternatives, and simulations already deleted from the ResSim GUI. Table 1 lists files to consider removing from the rss subdirectory.

Table 1. RSS Subdirectory Removable Files

File Extension Keep Remove bak Any associated with fema_ffrd, ffrd_{basin-name}.*.bak, stream.align.bak, WatershedInfo.md.bak Everything else conf rss.conf dbf

resop.dbf

ressysop.dbf

{network-name}_ExtendedOp.dbf

{network-name}_ExtendedSysOp.dbf

Files associated with old networks dbt

resop.dbt

ressysop.dbt

{network-name}_ExtendedOp.dbt

{network-name}_ExtendedSysOp.dbt

Files associated with old networks dss

{network-name}_Extended.dss

{network-name}_Extended-GlobalVariablesTables.dss

Files associated with old networks fits Two files associated with each alternative All other fits files igv one file associated with each alternative All other igv files malt one file associated with each alternative No other files existed mcscript one file associated with each alternative All other mcscript files ralt one file associated with each alternative No other files existed rssrun none No rssrun files needed for ResSim standalone simperiod One file associated with each simulation No other files existed simrun One file associated with each simulation xml none no xml reports were needed Xsd, xlt None no AlternativeInput report files needed

docs.title: ResSim Script Considerations
docs.text: ResSim models with scripts may be flagged as a security risk within the Civil Works Business Intelligence (CWBI) cloud, and no jars or python modules will be accepted on CWBI. To address this concern, modelers will clean existing models of scripts by reviewing each script function and replacing it wherever possible with simpler operations. If scripted operations (rules or state variables) are necessary, the modeler will conduct a careful review and write justification for the CWBI security team to support the need for the script’s inclusion on CWBI. Modelers should keep in mind that scripted rules and state variables will not be calculated during the HEC-ResSim lookback period, and this can impact overall model results. Scripts must be structured to only require the data available within the event.

docs.title: ResSim Model Delivery
docs.text:
Open the watershed and ensure everything remains functional in each module. Make one last note in the WatershedInfo.md file of everything that was deleted. Post the model to the Model Library.

Delivery Location: conformance > reservoir-operations > [basin-name]

If the model is in zipped format prior to uploading to the model library, the unzip in Model Library should be used to ensure the model is posted in an un-zipped format. Note that for HEC-ResSim, uploading in zip format may be required to avoid firewall filtering of specific files associated with the model.

Figure 29. Zip File Upload on Model Library

docs.title: HEC-RAS Delivery
docs.text:
A final calibration phase HEC-RAS model for specific historic storm events is the starting point for the Conformance Phase.

Various changes are made to prepare the model for generalized synthetic storm events driven by SST before they are posted to the Model Library in preparation for cloud-compute, which are described in the sections below.

docs.title: Software Version
docs.text: The HEC-RAS software version used for model development must be verified and consistent with the version stated in Section 8 of SOP Volume II.

docs.title: Plans and Naming Conventions
docs.text:
HEC-RAS model is delivered with a single plan file and single flow file with generalized naming conventions to accept SST events that will not be confused for actual observed events. The following changes are made to the original calibrated model names:

  • Project and Filenames: [model-unit]

  • Geometry Name: [model-unit]

  • Plan Name: SST

  • Plan Short Name: SST

docs.title: Plan File
docs.text: The following adjustments should be made on the desktop version of the model plan file.

docs.title: Time Window
docs.text:
The simulation time window selected for SST events, described section 1.4 above, will be used for the generalized SST event HEC-RAS time window. It will match the time window used in HEC-HMS and HEC-ResSim.

Note that the time window is somewhat imperfect in that initial conditions in the HEC-RAS model start during the look-back period prior to the 72-hour precipitation event. The processes for FFRD SST event time windows, start date, and initial conditions will be considered for improvement in future versions of the SOP.

docs.title: Warm-up Time Period
docs.text:
The plan file warm-up time period settings should be set to balance accuracy and efficiency in terms of cost and computation time in the cloud-compute environment.

The use of a warm-up time period allows for the channels in the model to be primed with water before the start of the simulation. Note that in the context of the FFRD HEC-RAS models, excess precipitation gids will not contribute to initial flows in channels so the flow boundary conditions will be the primary contributor during the warm-up, if used.

In some cases, the modeler may determine that warm up time periods do not improve the ability of the HEC-RAS model to predict peak water surface elevations, and it may not be needed.

Figure 30. HEC-RAS Unsteady Computation Options and Tolerances Window

docs.title: Initial Conditions Points
docs.text: Ensure that the Keep initial elevations constant during warmup option is set to yes, which allows initial conditions (IC) points representing pool elevations that will be varied in SST events to remain constant when a warmup time period is also sued.

docs.title: Restart Files
docs.text: If restart files were used for the calibration runs, they must be removed for the cloud-based SST events.

docs.title: Breaches
docs.text: All breach locations with various breach parameters will be included in the final calibration phase model prior to the start of the Conformance Phase. For the model upload for cloud compute, all breaches should be turned on (Breach This Structure checkbox). The trigger breach elevation may be set to the toe of the structure to be breached, but cloud-compute will handle random sampling of this value during computations.

docs.title: Equation Set
docs.text: The 2D equation set selected in the plan file should be consistent with the selected equation set used for the calibration of the model. This should be verified prior to model upload for cloud compute.

docs.title: Matrix Solver
docs.text: The plan file 2D matrix solver selected in HEC-RAS may be evaluated to balance computational cost and numerical stability prior to delivery to cloud compute.

docs.title: Solver Cores Setting
docs.text:
The plan file, Solver Cores setting on the run should be set to optimize efficiency in terms of cost and computation time in the cloud-compute environment. Coordination with the cloud environment support team is needed to determine the optimal Solver Core setting, which accounts for type of compute resource to be used and computation time. Note that the ras-runner compute manifest should also call the appropriate compute resources that match this HEC-RAS solver core setting.

Figure 31. View of Window that Defines Solver Core Settings

docs.title: Output Control Options
docs.text:
Ensure that no unnecessary output is written due to settings in the Output Control Options. It is common to turn on certain variables to be saved to the HDF5 output file during the model development and calibration process, but those should not be carried forward for the cloud-compute runs during the Conformance or Production Phases of the project.

Figure 32. Example Setting for Output Control Options

docs.title: Mapping Output Interval
docs.text: The mapping output interval can have a significant impact on HDF output file size. The hydraulic modeler should coordinate with the model integration lead and other team members to investigate whether the critical event-level outputs can be achieved with a longer mapping output interval. Note that the maximum water surface elevation and maximum velocity for each cell is recorded at the computation interval, which lessens the need for a short mapping output interval.

docs.title: Geometry
docs.text:
Although no special changes to the geometry file will be necessary to deliver the desktop model for cloud compute, it is possible that minor geometry adjustments will be needed in the conformance phase models due to unique issues that were not discovered during the calibration of individual models. Note if any updates to the models that affect linking take place, corresponding updates to Model Linking Register are needed. The model integration lead will own the Model Linking Register throughout the process.

The geometry file (.g01) should be uploaded with the model to the compute directory. Note that the file is not required for cloud-compute, but it is retained to allow for a desktop modeler to work with the model locally in future steps if needed.

docs.title: Flow File
docs.text:
Several updates to the flow file are needed prior to upload for cloud compute to fully link to the input data sources for SST events. Relative path name references should be based on the directory structure.

Note that cloud-compute ingests only the HDF files for most of the key inputs for the computation and does not use the flow file. However, the flow file itself (.f01) should be uploaded with the model to the compute directory to allow for a desktop modeler to work with the model locally in future steps if needed.

docs.title: Observed Data
docs.text: Deleted references to observed data hydrographs from the calibration events. Rating Curve tables may be maintained.

docs.title: Upstream Inflows
docs.text: Any upstream inflow hydrographs must be updated to reference appropriate simulated outputs based on the Model Linking Register.

docs.title: Meteorological Data
docs.text:
Excess Precipitation grids must be updated to reference HMS results from SST events.

Reference the directory structure for relative pathnames for reference to the excess precipitation grids.

Filename: exported-precip.dss

docs.title: HMS Baseflows
docs.text:
Baseflow hydrographs must be updated to reference HMS results from SST events.

Reference the directory structure for relative pathnames for reference to the baseflows.

Filename: SST.dss

docs.title: Downstream Water Surface
