docs.text: Appropriate adjustments to the downstream water surface boundary condition must be made. This may be an adjustment from an observed time series to a rating curve, or from an observed tidal stage time series to a representative fixed water surface during synthetic rainfall events. The main body of the SOP provides general guidance on selection.

docs.title: Dam Releases from ResSim or HMS
docs.text:
Dam outflow hydrographs must be updated to reference ResSim or HMS results from SST events according to the Model Linking Register and Dam Model Scoping Table.

Reference the directory structure for relative pathnames for reference to the simulated dam outflows.

ResSim output filename: simulation.dss

HMS output filename: SST.dss

docs.title: RAS Mapper File
docs.text:
The RAS Mapper file must associate with the terrain-modeling file according to the directory structure. Reference to the default location for land cover should remain in place to allow for modelers to download the model and recreate results. Links to plans and results from calibration efforts or any data other than the single delivered plan must be deleted.

The RAS Mapper file is also used in cloud-compute during depth and velocity grid generation. This process will refer to the separate path to a lower resolution grid. The requirements for this version of the RAS Mapper are not final as of June 2025.

docs.title: Additional HEC-RAS Delivery Steps
docs.text: The following sections show addition actions that are required to prepare the HEC-RAS models for cloud compute.

docs.title: Run Plan on Desktop
docs.text:
The HEC-RAS model plan must be executed on desktop to build the intermediate files that are used directly by cloud compute. After all final changes to the model are made, all out-of-date intermediate files should be deleted and the model should be run to create a clean and current set of files.

Note that the delivered .p01.HDF file will include results data that will be removed from the file during the cloud-compute.

docs.title: Verify Dimensions Inflows in HDF File
docs.text:
The HEC-RAS HDF file must have dimensions that match the number of time steps for boundary condition inputs. For example, if the duration of the simulation for SST events is 10 days with hourly outputs the HEC-HMS or HEC-ResSim outputs will have 240 ordinates.

Likewise, the HEC-RAS *.p01.HDF file input tables should have 240 ordinates.

Path: Event Conditions>Unsteady>Boundary Conditions>Flow Hydrographs

Figure 33. HDF Input Dimensions

docs.title: Verify Dimensions of Excess Precipitation
docs.text:
The dimensions of the excess precipitation inputs must match the values expected by the HEC-RAS HDF file. The SST-generated excess precipitation grid dimensions can be checked via the Grid Info dialog through the DSS-Vue software, as shown in Figure 34. The HEC-RAS HDF file dimensions can be checked via the table size from the example path shown in Figure 35. Note that the HEC-RAS HDF table first dimension includes a time step component, while the second dimension value is a total number of values in a single grid (number of cells wide by number of cells tall).

Figure 34. Location to Check Excess Precipitation Dimensions

Figure 35. Precipitation Grid Dimensions in HEC-RAS HDF File

docs.title: List Cells Affected by Initial Conditions Points
docs.text:
The cloud compute will vary initial reservoir elevations for each event for all reservoirs modeled as reservoirs in HEC-HMS or included in the HEC-ResSim model, as defined in the Dam Scoping Table. Each of these reservoirs will include a HEC-RAS Initial Conditions point.

The control for the varied IC point elevation is not within the flow file that desktop HEC-RAS users are familiar with. Rather, the reservoir pool starting condition are fed to the Linux/cloud compute for HEC-RAS through the .p01.HDF file, from the Event Conditions/Unsteady/Initial Conditions/2D Flow Areas/Perimeter 1 path.

Figure 36. HDF File Path that Controls IC Point Starting Elevations

This path includes an array of IC elevations for each cell in the model, and must be over-written prior to cloud compute. The HEC-RAS modeler integrator must deliver a list of all cells that could potentially be wet for the highest possible sampled IC to the cloud compute team. To do this, a temporary model run using a very-high-IC-point, high water level for the reservoir should be computed. After the computation completes, the resulting HDF file must be opened, the previously mentioned path tabulated and exported to a table using the HDFViewer software. This list of cells will be used by cloud compute for over-writing of pool levels in SST events.

Note

HEC-RAS will ignore an IC in cells with bottom elevations that are lower than the pre-set IC elevation. This is why it is appropriate to set IC elevations for all cells that are possibly wet for IC.

To generate this list of points, the following steps can be followed.

  1. Open the .p01.hdf file and navigate to the array mentioned above.

  1. Table > Select All > Copy. This data is the initial elevation applied to each cell in order of cell number.

  2. To reformat this data into a list of cell numbers. Paste these values into Excel. Insert a column to the left named Cell Num, number this column starting at 0.

Figure 37. Table Resulting from Step 3

  1. Filter these values, remove all ‘NaN’ rows. Also remove all rows referencing cells whose initial elevations may be specified but will not be varied in cloud compute.

Figure 38. Table resulting from Step 4.

  1. Sort remaining cells by smallest to largest cell number.

  2. Copy these cell numbers (only the cell numbers) into a new sheet using Paste Special>Transpose. This will convert the column of cell numbers into a single row. Save the sheet as a comma delimited file (.csv).

  3. Cell numbers will be stored in a single row, separated by commas.

Figure 39. Text list resulting from Step 7

docs.title: Delivery of Cell List for Initial Conditions Points
docs.text:
This list of cells will be delivered as a field in the Model Linking Resister, which will be utilized in the development of the HEC-RAS compute manifests described elsewhere.

When multiple IC points that will be used for reservoir pool initial elevations are present in a model unit, multiple cell lists will be prepared in a similar manner to that described above.

Delivery Location: basin-data > scoping-data > model-linking-register

docs.title: HEC-RAS Model Delivery
docs.text:
Fully run the final model on desktop and perform all QC of results described in other sections.

Remove output files, unneeded subfolders, and backup files from directory.

Post the full set of remaining files from desktop to the Model Library. Although not all remaining model files are required for cloud-compute, the full directory should be posted to the runnable directory to retain a reference and to allow the model to be downloaded for a desktop replication of a cloud event run.

Delivery Location: conformance > hydraulics > [model-unit]

docs.title: Land Cover
docs.text: No special adjustments to the landcover layer are needed to deliver the model for cloud compute. The land cover layer should remain in its HEC-RAS default location and should be uploaded with the model to the compute directory. Note that the data is not required for cloud-compute, but it is retained to allow for a desktop modeler to work with the model locally in future steps if needed.

docs.title: Mapping Terrain Delivery
docs.text:
As described in Section 3.5 of SOP Volume II, multiple versions of the HEC-RAS terrain will be managed by the hydraulic modeler.

The terrain-production is the full resolution terrain used for modeling, and the final version will be maintained in the following path.

Delivery Location: terrain-modeling > [model-unit]

The terrain-mapping is the lower resolution exported version of the terrain that will be used for results mapping and processing in cloud-compute. This terrain merges all vector-based terrain modifications and any layers or tiles associated with the terrain used for model development and will be posted to the following path.

Delivery Location: terrain-mapping > [model-unit]

docs.title: Create Exported Terrain for Mapping
docs.text:
Merging and resampling of terrain to produce the terrain-mapping version is performed within HEC-RAS Mapper. This software provides adequate tools to perform this process seamlessly and minimizes opportunities for user error by using a consistent system. The merged/resampled output DEM should be generated directly from the HEC-RAS terrain which is associated with the HEC-RAS geometry file used for FFRD modeling. This terrain should include any vector terrain modifications used to alter the terrain, such as: hydro-connectors, culverts, bathymetry estimates, levees, etc.

To merge and resample a terrain in RAS Mapper, right-click the terrain associated with the FFRD model geometry. Select Export Layer, and Export Raster as shown in Figure 40. The exported raster name should be descriptive and follow naming convention guidance as shown in Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions. It should include the exported cell size as specified in Section 4 of SOP Volume II. The checkbox to Export To Single Raster should be enabled. This is required if the input HEC-RAS terrain .hdf file references several individual raster DEMs which may be of variable cell resolutions. The output cell size should be defined in feet.

Note that terrain modifications should be incorporated into the exported merged/resampled terrain. By default, if vector terrain modifications are included in the HEC-RAS terrain .hdf file, they will be burned into the exported terrain. If the width of a terrain modification is small (i.e., substantially less than the resolution of the exported raster file), the terrain modifications may be misrepresented in the output terrain surface as smoothing can occur when rasters are resampled. This is generally acceptable for cloud result mapping but is a reason to beware when using resampled terrains for hydraulic modeling.

Figure 40. HEC-RAS Mapper Export Terrain Dialog

Figure 41. RAS Mapper Export Terrain Secondary Dialog

docs.title: Breach Data Delivery
docs.text:
The system response probability tables for each breach location are produced by the dam and levee technical leads as described in separate job aids.

The model integration lead is also responsible for ensuring that the dam and levee breach data is delivered in the appropriate format and directory location for use in the stochastic simulations via cloud compute. The System Response Template is provided for use in Appendix C of SOP Volume II. A single file is created for all breaches for the entire project. The template includes instructions for populating the data in an Excel table that includes data validation checks to avoid common errors and exporting to a .csv format for direct use in cloud-compute.

The system response table is delivered to the following location.

Delivery Location: conformance > system-response

Figure 42. View of System Response Template Format

docs.title: Status of This Document
docs.text:
This document is provided as a raw draft for early delivery, but has not undergone complete content review, editing or finalization.

This document will complement the online cloud compute documentation with specific FFRD requirements and procedures.

docs.title: Workflow Context
docs.text:
Compute manifests are the critical files that contain instructions for a cloud compute plugin to perform a compute job. They are *.json formatted files that control many aspects of the cloud compute such as connections between inputs/outputs, paths for output storage, docker-file source (and version), and certain model parameters.

The model integration lead is responsible for delivering models and the final Model Linking Register for cloud compute as described in Job Aid 11 – Delivery of Models for Cloud Compute. This Model Linking Register is an easily readable table that will contain all information required to build many of the required cloud-compute manifests

The cloud environment lead is responsible for creation of compute manifests and execution of the cloud compute jobs. The purpose for each model manifest for FFRD cloud compute is provided in the table below, and more detailed descriptions are available through the cloud compute documentation (https://github.com/USACE-Cloud-Compute/cc-home). The manifests utilized for cloud compute throughout a project will be stored in the ‘conformance/manifests’ and ‘production/manifests’ directories.

Adhering to proper naming conventions and guidance for where and how to store models significantly improves the success of model integration and sets the stage for significant automation. This SOP provides strict guidance on naming conventions of directory structure, models, and model elements that facilitates the integration of the models.

Scripting to automate certain steps in the production of the compute-manifests has the potential to improve efficiency and ability to QC linking data.

Table 1.1. List of all cloud-compute manifests for an FFRD project

FFRD Manifest purpose plugin ~~block-generation~~ ~~Initialization of randomized storm events~~ ~~seed-generator~~ ~~seed-generator~~ ~~Initialization of randomized storm events~~ ~~seed-generator~~ ~~fishnets~~ ~~Generate valid set of of storm placements~~ ~~hms-mutator~~ ~~hms-mutator~~ ~~Apply random SST storm locations as boundary conditions in preparation for hms runs~~ ~~hms-mutator~~ ~~hms-runner~~ ~~Execute HMS model for all events~~ ~~hms-runner~~ ~~ressim-runner~~ ~~Execute ResSim model for all events~~ ~~ressim-runner~~ ~~system-response~~ ~~Sample random breach trigger elevations based on system response curves~~ ~~system-response~~ ~~ras-prep~~ ~~Prepare all input files for ras runs~~ ~~ras-runner~~ ~~ras-runner~~ ~~Execute RAS model(s) for selected events~~ ~~cc-ras-runner~~ ~~cc-ras-postprocessor~~ ~~Postprocess RAS results including depth and velocity for each event~~ ~~cc-ras-postprocessor~~ ~~aggregator~~ ~~Generate summary flood risk maps included AEP grids.~~ ~~Aggregator~~ ~~consequence-runner~~ ~~Compute summary consequences at each structure.~~ ~~consequence-runner~~

Figure 1.2. Focus of this Job Aid in relation to steps within the Conformance Phase

docs.title: Conformance Phase Model Manifests and Computes
docs.text:
Utilizing the Model Linking Register and the models delivered for cloud compute, the cloud compute lead creates a set of model manifests constructed to support cloud computation. In addition to the delivered models, some other models supporting cloud compute are created.

Cloud-Compute Setup

  1. Create an event generator manifest on the local desktop. Coordinate with the meteorologists to confirm the target number of events per year.

    • Create a seed generator manifest and a seeds.json file that is uploaded to the checkpoint-validation/seeds folder within the watershed repository in the model library. The seeds.json file contains seeds for SST, HEC-HMS, fragility curves, and consequences are the standard seeds and likely the only seeds to consider. Verify with cadre lead that no other sampling is necessary.

  2. Upload the storm catalog, including listed files, to the conformance/storm-catalog folder within the watershed repository in the model library.

    • Catalog.grid

    • Transposition domain.gpkg

    • Watershed boundary.gpkg

    • DSS folder containing data for all 440 storms.

  3. Develop antecedent data sets utilizing the POR HEC-HMS run, the calibration event HEC-HMS runs, and the storm catalog. Post the antecedent data sets to conformance/hydrology/basinmodels.

  4. Develop a compute manifest for SST. Ensure the correct seed set is connected to the storm selection process.

    Initial Cloud-Computation

  5. System Response: Develop a compute manifest for system response curves. Coordinate with Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    1. Run event #1 through system response and verify the output is appropriate.

    2. Run a full realization through system response curves. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the levee data team to adjust the system response curves as needed to conform to the expected trigger elevation distribution at the breach locations.

  6. HMS: Develop a compute manifest for HEC-HMS. Coordinate with Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    1. Run event #1 through HEC-HMS.

    2. Run event one through the integrated model on the desktop.

    3. Model Integration Lead is responsible for verification that event #1 cloud results are compatible with a replicated run in the desktop environment. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the HEC-HMS modeling team to adjust, the HEC-HMS model as needed.

    4. Run a full realization through HEC-HMS. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the HEC-HMS modeling team to adjust the HEC-HMS model as needed ensure initial states and basin parameters are being properly sampled.

    5. ~~Run all calibration events using the manifests for the HEC-HMS model used for frequency calibration to validate the changes to the model to meet frequency calibration do not cause a model’s historic event calibration to fall out of acceptable ranges.~~

  7. ResSim: Develop compute manifest for ResSim utilizing the delivered model and the Model Linking Register. Coordinate with Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    1. Run event #1 through HEC-ResSim.

    2. Run event #1 through the integrated model on the desktop.

    3. Model Integration Lead is responsible for verification that event #1 cloud results are compatible with a replicated run in the desktop environment. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the HEC-ResSim modeling team to identify and correct any issues.

    4. Run a full realization through HEC-ResSim. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the HEC-ResSim modeling team to adjust the HEC-ResSim model as needed to ensure model is producing expected results.

    5. ~~Run all calibration events using the manifests for the HEC-ResSim model used for frequency calibration to validate the changes to the model to meet frequency calibration do not cause a model’s historic event calibration to fall out of acceptable ranges.~~

  8. RAS: Develop compute manifests for all RAS models utilizing the delivered models and the Model Linking Register. Coordinate with Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    1. Run event #1 through HEC-RAS.

    2. Run event #1 through the integrated model on the desktop.

    3. Model Integration Lead is responsible for verification that event #1 cloud results are compatible with a replicated run in the desktop environment. Identify any issues and adjust or work with the HEC-RAS modeling team to identify and correct any issues.

      Fidelity Check

  9. Run event 3 events through HMS, ResSim, System Response and RAS.

  10. Run replicas the 3 cloud-computed events through the full integrated watershed model on the desktop (HMS, ResSim and RAS).

  11. Modeling Integration Lead is responsible for verification that results are compatible. Model Integration Lead and Cloud Compute Lead will coordinate with any modeling team members necessary to identify and correct any issues.

    Single Realization Computation

  12. Develop a list of events that contribute to the annual maximum series based on HMS, ResSim and System Response results.

  13. Run a full realization through HEC-RAS for frequency calibration, excluding events that do not contribute to the annual maximum series.

    Conformance Metric Evaluation

  14. The model integration lead, model team members, levee data lead, and cloud compute lead will evaluate H&H models against observed data at the primary, secondary, and tertiary gages. The conformance metrics will be used to guide acceptability of the results.

  15. Adjust watershed model parameters as needed to achieve conformance to observed data

  16. ~~Run all calibration events using the manifests for the HEC-RAS model used for frequency calibration to validate the changes to the model to meet frequency calibration do not cause a model’s historic event calibration to fall out of acceptable ranges.~~

    Hazard Maps and Consequences

  17. Hazard Grids (events): Develop compute manifest for hazard grid generation utilizing the delivered models and the Model Linking Register. Coordinate with Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    1. Run hazard map production for event one.

    2. Verify resulting hazard grid matches expected results for the same event with comparison in RASMapper. Coordinate with the Model Integration Lead to resolve any issues.

    3. Compute hazard grids for all events with RAS results

  18. Hazard Grids (aggregated): Develop compute manifest for aggregated hazard grids.

    1. Run map aggregation for all results

  19. Consequences: Develop compute manifests for consequences computation for each model unit.

    1. Run a full realization through the consequences model. Identify any issues and work with the consequences modeling team to adjust as necessary.

      Conformance Metric Evaluation (re-visit)

  20. The consequence lead, model integration lead, levee data lead, model team members and cloud compute lead will evaluate H&H models results & consequence results and make any adjustments needed. The conformance metrics will be used to guide acceptability of the results.

    Conformance Phase Delivery

  21. Cloud compute team member computes all final summary outputs.

  22. Conformance Review Checklist is finalized with all items closed out to document the review process throughout the phase.

  23. Cloud compute team member computes average cost per event for cloud compute runs based on compute costs during conformance phase. Coordinate to ensure sufficient budget for production.

docs.title: Production Phase Manifests and Computes
docs.text:
1. Copy the contents of the conformance/storm-catalog folder to the production/storm-catalog folder.

  2. Copy the contents of the conformance/hydrology folder to the production/hydrology folder. Only copy the final model; do not copy the archived models.

  3. Copy the contents of the conformance/reservoir-operations folder to the production/reservoir-operations folder. Only copy the final model; do not copy the archived models.

  4. Copy the contents of the conformance/hydraulics folder to the production/hydraulics folder. Only copy the final models; do not copy the archived models.

  5. Copy the contents of the conformance/consequences folder to the production/consequences folder. Only copy the final models; do not copy the archived models.

  6. Copy the contents of the conformance/seeds folder to the production/seeds folder.

  7. Using the event generator manifest from conformance, update the blocks per realization to target 2,000 blocks per realization and update the target total events to be 1 million.

  8. Run event one starting with storm selection and computing through consequences to verify all linkages continue to function properly in production.

  9. Run realization one through HEC-HMS. Verify none of the hydrologic model simulations in the realization failed. Review computed frequency curve for realization one to ensure validity.

  10. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to the hydrologic model, create a hot fix folder in the production/hydrology folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update HEC-HMS manifest to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  11. Run realization one through system response. Verify none of the system response simulations in the realization failed. Review computed frequency curve for realization one to ensure validity.

  12. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to the system response curves, create a hot fix folder in the production/system-response folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update system response manifest to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  13. Run realization one through HEC-ResSim. Verify none of the reservoir operations model simulations in the realization failed. Review computed frequency curve for realization one to ensure validity.

  14. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to the reservoir operations model, create a hot fix folder in the production/reservoir-operations folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update HEC-ResSim manifest to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  15. Using the event level maximum files from the hydrology, system response, and reservoir operations, produce a list of events describing which hydraulics models to simulate for realization one.

  16. Run realization one through HEC-RAS using the specified list of events. Verify none of the hydraulic model simulations in the realization failed. Review computed frequency curve for realization one to ensure validity.

  17. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to a hydraulic model, create a hot fix folder in the production/hydraulics folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update HEC-RAS manifest to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  18. Run realization one through Consequences. Verify none of the consequences model simulations in the realization failed. Review computed frequency curve for realization one to ensure validity.

  19. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to the consequences model, create a hot fix folder in the production/consequences folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update Consequences manifest to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  20. Run realizations 2-50 through HEC-ResSim. Verify none of the hydrology, system response, and reservoir operations events failed.

  21. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to a model, create an appropriate hot fix folder in the appropriate model directory within the production folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update the appropriate manifests to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  22. Using the event level maximum files from the hydrology, system response, and reservoir operations, produce a list of events describing which hydraulics models to simulate for realization 2-50.

  23. Run realizations 2-50 through Consequences. Verify none of the hydraulics simulations, resulting mapped outputs fail, or consequences models failed.

  24. For any failed events, if the failed events require changes to a model, create an appropriate hot fix folder in the appropriate model directory within the production folder with the hot fix model with the date of upload included in the name (e.g., duwamish20250115). Update the appropriate manifests to use hot fix models. Recompute failed events using hot fix models.

  25. Create summary outputs including mean and standard deviation AEP and recurrence interval grids for depth, velocity, and duration.

  1. Produce a production of stochastic simulation report. Report is filed in documentation/reports/production-phase.
  1. Validation and Production Team lead coordinates with the Basin Team Lead to prepare SOG PPT presentation.

docs.title: Verification of Cloud Compute Event Results
Status of This Document
docs.text:
This document is provided as a raw draft for early delivery, but has not undergone complete content review, editing or finalization.

The ability to interact with model results that are produced via cloud compute is central to various phases of the FFRD workflow. Quality control checks to ensure that the cloud-compute generated model results match results that would be obtained via a desktop computation process is an important activity. Workflows for performing these activities within the Hydraulic HEC-RAS model are provided below.

docs.title: Workflow for Producing a Synthetic Event on Desktop (HEC-RAS)
docs.text:
This workflow allows for the simulation of a given synthetic event using HEC-RAS on a desktop environment. It utilized cloud simulated hydrology (HEC-HMS) and reservoir operations (HEC-ResSim) outputs and therefore these datasets must be available in the Model Library.

Note that by default, gridded excess precipitation from the HMS model is written as a HDF file. This format can be ingested by HEC-RAS as a precipitation boundary condition in a cloud environment, but not in a desktop environment. Therefore, an additional post-processing step is required in the cloud environment to generate gridded excess precipitation in HEC DSS file format, which can be ingested into RAS desktop.

docs.title: Downloading Data and File Management
docs.text:
1. Copy full original desktop model that was used for cloud delivery. This includes the Hydraulics, Hydrology and Reservoir Operations folders. Download and verify references to external data sources (DEM, Land Cover, ResSim, HMS Baseflows, HMS Excess Precip). By downloading the hydraulic model which was executed for cloud simulation, it ensures all computational settings (such as timestep, equation set, etc.) are consistent between cloud and desktop simulations.

  2. Make a Copy of the RAS model-desktop into a new directory name, "event 2" or similar. In general, it is beneficial to mirror the directory structure of the Model Library on the desktop environment to maintain relative file paths and aid in inter-model connections.

  3. Download the Hydrology, Reservoir Simulation, and System Response directories from the SST event of interest from the computable repository, and save to the new directory (i.e. "\event 2").

  4. If the model-unit of interest requires input from an upstream model-unit, download the Hydraulics data output from the upstream model unit. By default, this output will be stored within the us-model-unit.p01.hdf file.

docs.title: Verifying Connections and Simulation Settings in the Hydraulic Model
docs.text:
1. It can be beneficial to create a new RAS unsteady flow file and plan for the cloud event to be simulated, such as “event 7703”.

  2. Within HEC-RAS software, update the simulation window dates and times for the plan to align with SST event. This time window can be extracted from the ResSim simulation.dss timeseries data, among other locations. (Note the HMS time window for pilots has been extended 1 day prior to the rainfall to support a ResSim requirement for a “lookback period”, therefore the HMS simulation time may not be appropriate for use in RAS.)

  3. Ensure the hydraulic model is referencing the correct locations of HMS and ResSim Output. This includes: Baseflow hydrograph timeseries (from HMS), Gridded Excess Precipitation (from HMS), and Reservoir Releases (from ResSim). For timeseries boundary conditions, it can be beneficial to use the “Plot all the flow boundary condition hydrographs” button to quickly identify linkage issues with DSS connections. Also note that the simulation window must be updated prior to connecting DSS files for the plotting to succeed.

  4. Initial reservoir elevations which are sampled stochastically are adopted in the hydraulic model from either the ResSim or HMS model. In general, if a reservoir exists in the ResSim model, the initial elevation should be adopted from reservoir operations model. If it does not exist in ResSim, but does exist in HMS, it should be adopted from the hydrology model. Not all initial reservoir elevations are sampled stochastically, and therefore some may remain unchanged in the RAS unsteady flow file if they do not exist in either the ResSim or HMS models. These initial elevations can be manually extracted from either the HMS or ResSim DSS output files and should be the reservoir elevation from the initial timestep of the simulation. These initial elevations are specified for reservoir Initial Conditions (IC) Points.

    1. A secondary method to extract initial reservoir elevations is to open the model-unit.p01.hdf file for the cloud simulated RAS model for the event of interest.

      This hdf file is located in the Model Library here:

      ffrd-basin > conformance (or production) > simulations > event-data > event# > hydraulics > model-unit > model-unit.p01.hdf

      Within the model-unit.p01.hdf file, the initial reservoir elevations are specified in:

      Event Conditions > Unsteady > Initial Conditions > “IC Point Elevations” table

      (IC point names are stored in “IC Point Names” table)

  5. If the model-unit of interest requires input from one or more upstream hydraulic models, the timeseries of flows from those upstream units will need to be extracted from the upstream model units’ us-model-unit.p01.hdf file. This process can be done through a manual copy/paste exercise. The outflows from the upstream model unit will be entered into the RAS unsteady flow file as a timeseries flow hydrograph.

docs.title: Setting Up Breach Initiation Triggers
docs.text:
1. Breach initiation is triggered at a specified water surface elevation which is sampled from the System Response Probability (SRP) curves stochastically. These breach trigger elevations must be set for each breachable structure in the RAS unsteady flow analysis window. Breach trigger elevations for individual events are stored in the failure_elevations.json file located:

    ffrd-basin > conformance (or production) > simulations > event-data > event# > system-response > failure_elevations.json

docs.title: Simulating Event and Compare Results with Cloud Simulation
docs.text:
1. Compute hydraulic model on desktop.

  2. Compare results to ensure alignment. Note that a cloud simulated model-unit.p01.hdf result file from a given event can be downloaded and opened in HEC-RAS Mapper by dragging/dropping the file into the Mapper window. One opened, this file can be directly compared against a desktop simulated event in Mapper. However note the cloud simulated event may not display the correct simulation time window.

    Timeseries plots from a cloud simulated event can be viewed by opening the model-unit.p01.hdf file in RAS Mapper, expanding the Results > sst > Geometry component for the cloud simulated plan, then enabling a model element (such as a reference line or SA/2D connection), right clicking the element, and selecting Results Time Series > Hydrographs.

docs.title: Viewing Mapped summary Results
docs.text:
1. Go to computable watershed in model library (e.g. FFRD Kanawha Computable).

  1. Go to the aep-grids directory.

  2. Download aep_1000.zip file.

  3. “extract to here”.

  4. Open project in QGIS.

  5. View results, if something looks fishy, you can add depth grids.

  6. Go to the runs directory, select the event number of interest.

  7. Go to the depth-grids directory.

  8. Go to the ras model of interest.

  9. Download the ras model.

  10. “extract to here” browse to the vrt file.

  11. Load the vrt file.

  12. Right click on properties, symbology load from file. Load the depth grid qml file for symbology.

docs.title: Finding a Big Event in a Location
docs.text:
1. Go to the computable watershed in model library.

  1. Go to the statistics directory.

  2. Download the peaks.csv file.

  3. Open in excel.

  4. Find a location.

  5. Find a large value in that location.

  6. Identify the event number for that large value.

  7. Download that event number data for that location.

    Alternatively, graphical results plots can be very useful in identifying specific large events. These plots are located:

    ffrd-basin > conformance (or production) > simulations > summary-data > plots

docs.title: Verify Event-By-Event RAS Cloud Results
docs.text:
This section provides the information necessary to interact with the RAS output data generated by the cloud-compute of synthetic events. It describes the relevant files available after cloud compute, procedures for accessing the results and a general checklist for verification and investigation into the accuracy of the results on an event-by-event basis.

These procedures apply to various stages of the FFRD workflow, and a formal review using a checklist is required during certain phases as described in the SOP.

docs.title: List of Files available for Investigations (with descriptions)
docs.text:
The files in the directory \production>hydraulics>model-unit> directory (see Figure 1) include the model input files that were directly used for the cloud-compute. These are considered “the model”. Note that the files shown below are from the ‘production’ folder, but a very similar structure is also shown in the ‘validation’ area. Note that the cloud-compute uses the linux version of the RAS executable and several files that desktop RAS modelers commonly interact with are not direct inputs to cloud-compute but are still posted to this directory for reference and all to support a workflow to re-create a desktop version of the event when needed.

Figure 1.3. Location of Input Model Files

The event root directory (see Figure 1.4) is shown below and includes results from each of the H&H models used for the project.

Figure 1.4. Example Simulation Event Root Directory

The hydraulics model output files are stored in the \production>simulations>event#>hydraulics>model-unit directory as shown in Figure 1.5 below. They include the HDF file, b-file and runtime output log. The HDF file is the primary output file the contains all RAS output and can be viewed with either RASMapper or HDFView. The b-file is small text file that contains trigger elevations for each breach in the RAS model, and can be considered an input file for the RAS event.

Figure 1.5. Location of Hydraulics Model Output Files

The hydrologic model ouptuts are stored in the \production>simulations>event#>hydrology>basin-name directory as shown in Figure 1.6 below. The key files

The Exported Precip file is the “excess precipitation” runoff grid from HMS after losses are calculated and is a key output from the HMS model and is a direct input into the RAS model. As a standard in cloud-compute, the Exported Precip file is only saved in HDF format for ‘production’ simulations and the same data in DSS format is not saved in order to reduce files size storage. However, for ‘validation’ runs, the DSS format precip should be available. Note that accumulated precipitation grids are also viewable in the HDF output file from RAS.

Although not used directly in RAS, the ‘Storms.dss’ file (location shown in Figure 1.7) is direct precipitation that was used as input into HMS and is available for reference.

Figure 1.6. Location of Hydrology Model Output Files

Figure1.7. Location of Rainfall Grid. Note that this is not a direct input to RAS.

The reservoir operations model output is available in the ‘simulation.dss’ file as shown in Figure 1.8 below. This file includes reservoir outflow records that are used as a boundary condition for RAS and includes modeled elevation hydrographs.

Figure 1.8. Reservoir Operations Model Output File

The sampled trigger elevations for a breach are viewable in the failure_elevations.json file as shown in Figure 1.9 below. Note that these elevations are also viewable in the hydraulics>basin-name>B-file, but they are sourced from this file during cloud-compute.

Figure 1.9. System Response (breach) Model Results

docs.title: Verification of Cloud Simulated and Desktop Recreated Events:
docs.text:
The following list contains many of the items needed to validate cloud-simulated events on a desktop computer. These items also apply to cloud events which have been replicated on desktop, as discussed in section 1.1.

Checklist Items:

  • Check that it completed successfully (in RAS Mapper, or in rasoutput.log (Progress= 1.0)

  • Check volume accounting error as a percentage in rasoutput.log (should be less than around 3%-5%)

  • Check maximum WSE error in rasoutput.log (may be large due to large cells used in FFRD and steep terrain)

  • Check that rainfall was applied (create new result map layer > additional 2d variable > cumulative precipitation depth)

  • Check for breaches. Check that breaches occurred as they should have.

  • Check hydrographs (enable geometry form under results heading > enable sa/2d conns > right click structure > plot structure variables >hydrographs). Look for instability or other obvious problems.

  • Compare select rating curves against calibration/published rating curves

  • B file matches sampled breach elevations from failure-elevations.json file?

  • Check that BC line flows respected for ressim & HMS outputs, as shown in RAS HDF output file?

  • Check that excess precip applied properly

  • Check starting pool in RAS matches with starting pool from ResSim?

  • Check starting IC points respected & held constant during warmup?

  • Check reservoir initial condition. Does it contain a flat pool at IC? Any issues around edges of pol?

  • Check boundary condition flows are respected for ResSim & HMS outputs

  • Check that excess precipitation displays on top of mesh in RasMapper and typical pattern of depths from fluvial flooding are present 

  • Compare runtimes between cloud and desktop simulations

docs.title: Job Aid 16—Event Selection for Hydraulic Modeling
docs.text:
The process below aims to develop a list of specific events that may drive annual maximum hazards based on hydrologic, reservoir operations, and system response modeling results. This selected subset of events is then computed through the hydraulic models which provide the modeled hazard information necessary to produce annual hazard and risk probability outputs.

Summary files that tabulate the maximums of many variables in many locations for each event are generated. Using the event maximum summary data, a list of annual maximum contributing events is generated.

docs.title: Events Based on Hydrolgy and Reservoir Operations Model Results
docs.text:
Using summarized hydrology and reservoir operations event maximum outputs, apply the following steps to generate the list of annual maximum contributing events.

  1. Read hydrology and reservoir operations summarized output event maximums following guidelines in Table 1.

  1. Read the blocks.json file containing the relationship of each event number to a synthetic year.

  2. Iterate over each synthetic year, over each parameter and location.

  1. Find the maximum value within the synthetic year.

  2. Store the event number and maximum value with the parameter and location.

  1. Iterate over the stored maximums and event numbers for each parameter and location.
  1. Sort each parameter and location based on maximum value.

  1. Select the events within the target recurrence interval range. (For FFRD, this is 1/10 and 1/2,000.)

  2. Store the event numbers for those selected events.

docs.title: Events based on possible breach event
docs.text:
The following steps identify events that may result in breaching, which could lead to annual maximum contributing events behind levees or downstream of dams.

  1. Develop a rating curve relationship to estimate water surface loading at each specific breach location based on the most appropriate correlated output parameter from either they hydrology model or the reservoir operations model.

    1. Hydraulic model results from events identified in Section 1.1 are needed to support the development of these rating curves during the conformance phase of the project. For the production phase of the project, the previously established rating curves can be used directly.

  1. Generate estimated water surface loading at each breach location for each event using the relationship from Step 1 and output summaries from other models.

  2. Read the event level maximum files breach trigger elevations containing event maximums following guidelines in Table 1.

  3. Utilize the relationship developed in Step 1 and compare the breach trigger elevation at each breach location to determine if a breach may occur. If a breach may occur at any location, record the event number.

  4. Store all identified event numbers that may include a breach of any structure

Table 1. Key Parameters and Locations Identifying Annual Maximum Contributing Events

Parameter Location Model Instantaneous Excess Precipitation All subbasins Hydrology Flow (Instantaneous) Subbasin outlets, reaches, and/or junction elements at primary gages Hydrology Flow (Regulated - Instantaneous) At primary, secondary, and tertiary gages and reservoir outflows Reservoir operations Pool Elevation At all dams with system response curves Reservoir operations Breach Trigger Elevations At all dams and levees with system response curves System response

docs.title: Final Event List
docs.text: After events are identified for hydrology, reservoir operations, and breaching, deconflict lists ensuring event numbers are only identified once.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: This document describes processes for acquisition of the structure inventory, adjustments to the structure inventory, and computation of consequences for a Future of Flood Risk Data (FFRD) project.

docs.title: Preparing Structure Inventories (Data Preparation Phase)
docs.text: The delivery process for the initial structure inventory for an FFRD project during the data preparation phase is described below.

docs.title: Structure Inventory Scales
docs.text: The structure inventory points are represented by geopackage files using the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) National Structure Inventory (NSI) data schema documentation. There are two scales of structure inventory used in FFRD. The first is the Hydrologic Unit Code (HUC)-4 watershed scale, where all the structures in the basin are in one file. The second scale is at the modeling unit boundaries (the River Analysis System [HEC-RAS] model boundaries), where the HUC-4 level file is split into one geopackage for each modeling unit. These smaller scale files cannot be created until the modeling unit boundaries have been finalized. This job aid describes how to create the structure inventories using the NSI data as the source.

docs.title: Structure Inventory File Type and Naming Conventions
docs.text: Each structure inventory file is a geopackage containing a point data layer named nsi as the only table. The name of the geopackage itself is defined in Job Aid 06, Standard Naming Conventions.

docs.title: Full Watershed Study Structure Inventory
docs.text:
During the Scoping and Data Preparation Phase, prior to the creation of the mesh refinement regions, the full study watershed structure inventory is developed. There are two primary methods to download the NSI data. The first is directly from the NSI website. This allows for downloading of NSI geopackages at the state level. Clip the state geopackage data to the study watershed boundary, and if multiple states are required, clip each state file to the watershed, and merge the output files into a single geopackage for the entire watershed.

The second method is to use the county-level NSI api to download county-level data in JSON format, convert the files to geopackage, clip the data to the watershed boundary, and merge the outputs together to a single output geopackage. This can be accomplished using a python script tool in a geographic information system (GIS) environment.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > structure-inventory

docs.title: Modeling Unit Structure Inventories
docs.text:
Once the modeling unit boundaries are finalized, the full watershed structure inventory geopackage is split into separate geopackages for each hydraulic model unit. In ESRI software, the split tool is used, and the split results are used to create the unique geopackage files for each modeling unit. This can be accomplished more efficiently through a script that will pre-form the split and create the individual geopackage outputs with the appropriate naming conventions.

This dataset will remain without further refinements and serve as a reference to the original NSI dataset throughout the project.

Delivery Location: basin-name > basin-data > structure-inventory

docs.title: Improvements to Structure Inventory (Calibration Phase)
docs.text: The purpose of this section is to identify potential issues in the hydraulic modeling and the placement of the structure inventory points. This subsection applies to calibration phase and occurs after the structure inventory data is prepared.

docs.title: Identifying Hydraulic Modeling and Structure Placement Issues
docs.text: Improvements to the structure inventory can include resolving structure placement issues.

docs.title: Workflow and Timeline
docs.text:
The following ordered steps occur during the calibration phase of the FFRD project.

  1. Once the HEC-RAS model units have been finalized and the HEC-RAS meshes have been developed, the consequences team member will generate structure inventories for each modeling unit.

  2. The hydraulic modeler utilizes the inventory by computing depths for the calibration events at the structures and reviewing the depths for outliers.

  3. The hydraulic modeler will resolve any hydraulic modeling issues identified and compile any observed issues with the structure inventory that the consequences team member should review.

  4. Once a hydraulic model is submitted for review with computed depths at the structures for each calibration event, the consequences team member will pull the files locally for review.

  5. The consequences team member will review the depth estimates for the events and the issues identified by the hydraulic modeler to review and identify issues that may be hydraulic in nature or structures that need to be relocated or removed. The consequences team member will provide a list of potential hydraulic modeling issues back to the hydraulic modeler and update the structure inventory as required to address structure inventory location issues.

  6. The consequences team member will deliver the calibrated structure inventory project directory.

Delivery Location: basin-name > calibration > consequences

  1. As described in Job Aid 07, Model Library Use, the current dataset to be applied for computations must always maintain a consistent pathname and filename. When iterations of the structure inventory improvements occur, outdated versions may be archived by moving them to an archive subdirectory with an appended date stamp included in the filename.

docs.title: Improvements to Hydraulic Model or Structure Placements
docs.text: Reviewing hydraulic model outputs with the structure inventory helps to focus the review to areas that are currently high risk and will receive greater scrutiny as well as to provide an additional perspective in the review aside from gage-based metrics alone. The hydraulic team members and the consequences team members complete this job aid. The following sections provide some context for common issues in hydraulic modeling and structure inventory placements. The hydraulic modeling team member will focus on identification of hydraulic modeling issues during calibration of the hydraulic models; however, it is likely that in reviewing the results some structure placement issues may be noticed and documented. The consequences team member is responsible for structure placement issues; however, it is likely that the consequences team member might notice hydraulic issues missed by the hydraulic modelers during their review.

docs.title: Hydraulic Model Refinements
docs.text: Hydraulic model refinements are necessary when the hydraulic model results in depths at structures that are unreasonable for the modeled event. This can happen due to lacking terrain modifications, poorly aligned cells, datum issues with the levee crest profiles, or other geometry or plan settings. Some common issues are illustrated below.

docs.title: Terrain Modifications
docs.text:
When defining river channels in hydraulic models, sometimes islands containing infrastructure can be improperly defined in the channel. Simply reviewing the hydraulic modeling without noticing the structures can yield hydraulic model results that look good, but are actually inconsistent with reality. Figure 1 illustrates an example where an industrial complex on an island floods with every calibration event even though the complex is rarely flooded. The terrain did not reflect the island adequately.

Figure 1. An Incorrectly Flooded Industrial Complex due to Terrain

docs.title: Stream Alignment Issues
docs.text:
If automated tools are used to define stream centerlines, or old orthophotography is utilized to review a model, it can happen that a river does not align with the real river. Using the structure inventory can quickly identify these issues.

Figures 2 and 3 illustrate an example where a simulated result does not align with the real channel and causes unrealistic damage on structures.

Figure . Misaligned Simulated Result Causing Unrealistic Structure Damage

Figure 3. The Channel from Before but without the Inundation Overlay

docs.title: Poorly Aligned Cells
docs.text:
Utilizing large cell sizes and highly detailed terrains can yield results where terrain modifications should be used. Figure 4 illustrates the edge of a cell that coincided with a missing terrain mod that results in significant ponding flooding some structures that should not get flooded.

Figure 4. Incorrect Structure Flooding due to Missing Terrain Modification Coinciding with a Cell Edge

Sorting based on depth can support identification of problem areas in the hydraulic modeling quickly. See Figures 5 and 6 for an example where 6 of the top 15 deepest depths for an event occurred in one area of the watershed. Figure 5 shows the flooding at the structures in excess of 45 feet. Figure 6 shows the structures on rooftops in Open Street Maps. This should indicate an issue with the hydraulic modeling and further investigation would be warranted.

Figure 5. Flooding In Excess of 45 feet

Figure 6. Structures on Rooftops

In Figure 7, 4 more of the top 15 structures are flooded to depths over 30 feet in a single problem area.

Figure . Structures Flooded to Depths more than 30 feet

This likewise is a low area in the terrain and a location where the mesh accumulates flooding inappropriately. Fixing the problems in Figure 5 and Figure 7 can likely address the most significant flooding in the watershed very quickly.

docs.title: Structure Inventory Refinements
docs.text: The base level NSI utilizes parcel data and building footprints to locate structures. No single parcel data source in the United States is fully complete and no footprint data source has yet achieved a perfect representation of all buildings in the United States. The generation of the NSI leverages other sources of information to fill in data gaps. An example is reliance on the Census data census block level housing units with other Census datasets to generate structures when no parcel or footprint data exists. Reconciliation of these diverse datasets can yield incorrect results occasionally. It is advisable that manual calibration of the inventory is performed to prevent overestimation of average annual damages. The consequence team member is responsible for calibration of the inventory used for production of results. The following sections provide some low effort solutions to improve significant issues quickly. The NSI technical documentation Frequently Asked Questions can also provide background on common issues and how to address them. The primary objective of this job aid is structure placement improvements or significant issues impacting consequence estimation.

docs.title: Initial Depth Outputs Sort By Depth
docs.text:
Calibration using the computed results from the hydraulic modeling calibration can quickly identify structures that are in areas that have previously flooded. In some cases, the structures are inappropriately placed and should be relocated. Common issues occur near rivers when no building footprints exist, but a parcel record does. By default, the NSI drops a point in the center of the parcel, which is good for a neighborhood, but some states extend parcels to the centers of rivers, which can lead to structures being placed in the centers of rivers. By sorting on depth and reviewing the deepest depths at structures the inappropriately placed structures can be quickly reviewed and relocated. Figure 8 illustrates an example of deep depths on a nonexistent structure identified in a parcel for a potential harbor.

Figure 8. Nonexistent Structure in a Harbor

docs.title: Location Based on Imagery
docs.text:
Various imagery sources are used in GIS software to adjust and verify structure locations. This effort focuses on areas near water sources such as rivers, creeks, streams, and lakes. Existing data, i.e., flood maps and dam and levee breach events, are used to target areas for location calibration. A primary goal of this calibration effort is to ensure structure points are not placed in areas subject to continuous inundation, such as within a river channel. Figure 9 shows a structure in a creek. Figure 10 shows imagery that would indicate the structure does not exist in that location. This structure should likely be removed from the NSI.

Figure 9. Structure in Inundation

Figure . No Structure Visible

docs.title: Improvements to Structure Inventory (Conformance phase)
docs.text: Evaluation of the quality of the hydraulic modeling and the placement of the structure inventory continues during the Conformance Phase of the project. The Conformance Phase starts with a calibrated structure inventory coming from Calibration Phase and hydraulic results that span the frequency range from the 5-year to the 2,000-year event become available to aid in the review of hydraulic model and structure placements.

docs.title: Conformance Phase Structure Inventory Data Management
docs.text:
At the start of the Conformance Phase, a copy of the Calibration Phase structure inventory is promoted to the conformance phase directory location. Any updates that to the inventory during the phase will be maintained with the same path and filename, and outdated versions may be archived.

Structure Inventory Location for Conformance phase:

basin-name > conformance > consequences

docs.title: Evaluation of Damages in Frequent Events
docs.text: The results from hydraulic and consequence modeling in the Conformance Phase are obtained as geopackages from the model library and reviewed to identify potential issues. Any structures flooded during the most frequent event (typically the 2-year event) are checked for accuracy using aerial imagery and other sources as described in Improvements to Structure Inventory (Calibration Phase). Any potentially significant issues with hydraulic modeling will be discussed by the team. Structure point locations are edited when necessary to reflect realistic results. Examples of this include moving a point from the center of a structure to an edge to keep it from flooding at the most frequent events.

docs.title: Evaluation of Damages and Flood Frequencies in Leveed Areas
docs.text:
Results within leveed areas are inspected to determine if flood frequencies and damages on the interior of the levee are higher than expected. Some water will likely pool in a levee interior during frequent events, but there shouldn’t be large amounts of structure flooding at frequent events (10-year and more frequent). The Annual Exceedance Probability grid can be used in conjunction with the other results files to inspect levee system results for reasonableness.

Any significant issues with interior levee results will be documented and reported to discussed with the team for further investigation. Any issues not addressed will be documented in the reporting, including any identified causes and solutions. Examples of remaining issues could be levees that do not tie into high ground or lack of pumping and interior drainage systems in the hydraulic model.

docs.title: Computation of Consequences
docs.text: Technical procedures for computation of consequences based on hydraulic modeling results are included in cloud-compute plugin documentation. In general, the inputs to a consequence computation are a set of hydraulic model results describing the hazard, a structure inventory describing the exposure, and a set of damage functions describing the vulnerability. The FFRD directory structure described throughout this SOP support the standardized delivery of input data for the consequence compute.

docs.title: Production Phase Structure Inventory
docs.text:
At the start of the production phase, a copy of the conformance phase structure inventory is promoted to the production phase directory location.

Structure Inventory Location for Conformance phase:

basin-name > production > consequences

docs.text:

When complete, this document will provide specific evaluation techniques and criteria for model performance for both event-based and quasi-continuous simulations. This document will not provide guidance on steps to adjust performance.

docs.title: Observed Data
docs.text: An annual maximum series of the subbasin average precipitation for each duration are calculated for the project. This data is computed during the scoping and data preparation phase of the FFRD process and used for quality assurance of the observed data. [as described in section X. data is stored in X]

docs.title: Modeled Data
docs.text: Average precipitation for each event over multiple durations for each subbasin for each event will be calculated. [reference tool available for mining this data. Bartles java script?]

docs.title: Plots
docs.text:
Plots displaying a comparison of the modeled and observed precipitation frequency with be produced.

Figure .. Example AMS precipitation plot for an individual subbasin

docs.title: Methodology decision rationale
docs.text:
Future of Flood Risk Data

Standard Operating Procedure

Documentation of Decision Rationale

DRAFT\ Month YYYY

This page is intentionally blank.

Contents

Section 0 – Document Status 1

1.2 Modeling Approach 2

1.3 Recurrence Interval 3

2.2 Sequencing of Production with Approvals Prior to Data Collection and Preparation 1

docs.title: – Document Status
docs.text: December 2025 – Status of this document. The content document was primarily developed in August of 2024 and was paired with an earlier draft version of the FFRD SOP. In certain areas it became out-of-sync with the December 2025 draft version 1 of the SOP but largely documents much of the rationale behind the core procedures. The need for a robust methodology decision rationale document was emphasized during reviews of the SOP conducted in September-October 2025 and plans are in place to fully update the “Volume III – Methodology Rationale” as a core delivery that serves as a foundation for the procedures, workflows and software recommended in “Volume II - Technical Standards and Procedures”.

docs.title: —Future of Flood Risk Data Context and Background
docs.text:
The Federal Emergency Management Agency’s (FEMA’s) Future of Flood Risk Data (FFRD) initiative seeks to shift from a binary approach to a graduated approach for flood risk and flood hazard for the nation. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) is developing a proposed methodology for FEMA to produce graduated hazard and risk. Many of the requirements for the basic methodology are defined in the standard operating procedure (SOP) developed for this initiative and in the methodology concept recommendation paper, Determining the Path: A look into Modeling Methodology (2020), produced by USACE.

This section covers the background decisions present in the methodology recommendation and the SOP.

docs.title: Implementation Strategy/Basin Order
docs.text: The FFRD methodology describes the implementation of a single hydrologic unit code (HUC) 4 watershed. During national implementation, basins must be prioritized in some way.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Exposure value

  • Stream miles

  • Hydrologic order.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: The FFRD project delivery team (PDT) recommends hydrologic order allowing harvesting of results for lower hydrologic order basins. This may not be critical, but it is the current recommendation.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: This topic was evaluated as part of projecting future production costs and timelines. Currently the SOP does not require the basin order and this decision has not been finalized.

docs.title: Modeling Approach
docs.text:
For FFRD, the overall modeling approach must meet the requirements specified in the Biggert-Waters Flood Insurance Reform Act of 2012 (BW-12) and described by FEMA.

  • Describe graduated flood hazard and risk from the 5- to the 2,000-year event

  • Describe graduated flood risk everywhere including areas of potential future development

  • Describe flood risk, including potential, for levee and dam breaches.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Metamodeling

  • Simulating discrete events and assigning annual exceedance probabilities (AEPs) to the output

  • Stochastic simulations using stochastic storm transposition (SST) and Model Suite with full-basin coverage

  • Procure third party data

  • Fathom

  • KatRisk.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: Participants in the inaugural steering committee meeting (Washington DC, May 2022) decided to investigate the costs and limitations of a methodology that includes stochastic simulations over a suite of full-basin models through pilot projects. The team discussed the large computational burden that may be associated with this approach. Trade-offs between modeling effort in un-populated areas versus populated areas were discussed. FEMA’s statuary requirement to provide flood information for all areas and desire to develop detailed hazard and consequence information that recognizes parameters such as velocity and duration were key considerations and discussion points. The decision included the team writing a draft SOP using this approach—adapting it based on lessons learned from each pilot study—and attempting to balance these considerations.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text:
Options not selected did not supply the necessary outputs to meet the requirements of BW-12.

The discussion into using metamodeling determined sufficient training data does not yet exist to properly train basin-wide metamodels for graduated hazard and risk products.

The FFRD team decided to remove the assumptions of AEP neutrality. Computing discrete AEP events and assigning AEPs to the output reinforces assumptions of AEP neutrality.

Procuring third party data was not accepted because the FFRD data should be the authoritative federal standard by which third party data is compared.

docs.title: Recurrence Interval
docs.text: The FEMA FFRD requirements document (USACE 2022) directed the FFRD PDT to use 1/5 AEP to 1/2,000 AEP.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• The 2,000-year event and everything more frequent

  • The range of interest=1/2 AEP to 1/2,000 AEP

  • The range of interest=1/5 AEP to 1/2,000 AEP.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text:
Requirements direct the use of 1/5 AEP to 1/2,000 AEP.

The Kanawha River Basin Pilot ran all events lower than the 2,000-year event to have data to compare to the computation of only statistics above the 2- or 5-year events. There is concern regarding the manipulation of the statistics if all events are not run.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text:
According to FEMA actuaries, recurrence intervals over 1/2,000 do not have statistical effect so their use was not considered viable.

Running for all events more frequent than the 2,000-year event increases the computational burden. The alternative of running everything between the 2-year and the 2,000-year events reduces computational burden (roughly by half).

docs.title: Collaboration with multiple stakeholders
docs.text: Coordination with local and regional stakeholders is a key aspect of a large-scale flood modeling effort. Federal, state, and local partners, as well as individuals have a stake in the modeling efforts. In general, the modeling goal is to provide the highest quality flood risk data possible to all stakeholders such that management decisions may be informed directly using reliable flood risk data.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Build FFRD models using technically-focused PDT

  • Engage stakeholders in data collection efforts and with in-progress briefings by the PDT

  • Require approval for models by local stakeholders before finalizing products.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: The SOP provides guidance for FFRD PDTs on how to engage stakeholders throughout the modeling process ensuring the PDT is responsive to the needs of the local interests and has an open line of communication. This coordination assists in obtaining the best available input data and existing models and in recognizing other local needs. This coordination also aims to achieve some level of local buy-in of model results.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text:
To produce high quality flood risk data, models must be produced based on the best available science and input data and should not be overly influenced by local political contexts.

PDT members must collaborate with stakeholders ensuring key local data is utilized and the results achieve local “buy-in”.

docs.title: Accessibility of Future of Flood Risk Data
docs.text: Two of the stated key objectives of the FFRD initiative are to modernize data management and delivery and to empower private and State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial (SLTT) stakeholders. Allowing some level of access to FFRD results, reports, and underlying models is key to meeting these objectives.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• No centralized storage of FFRD models

  • Centralized storage of FFRD final models using existing systems, such as network storage within a federal agency

  • Centralized storage of FFRD models on S3 cloud buckets with a Model Library interface using role-based access.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: The FFRD SOP leverages the Model Library and Gov-cloud S3 buckets for centralized storage. This system allows for data sharing throughout the model development process, provides for storage and archiving of final models and model results. This system can allow access to models and results to all stakeholders while meeting data security requirements.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text:
A lack of centralized storage would result in future difficulties in finding and utilizing FFRD models. It also would not leverage efficiencies associated with modern technology.

Using existing central storage systems within a single agency presents difficulties in managing access to the data across agencies, as well as outside of the agency.

docs.title: Current Condition Risk is Based on Most Recent Data
docs.text: FFRD studies will develop current condition risk based on data available at the start of the project. For a nationally consistent program, which may take many years to complete, dealing with inconsistent periods of analysis must be addressed. As of August 2024, nothing is being changed for future conditions.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Use data for current conditions based on data from a fixed date (e.g., 2025); do not gather data past that if a basin is started in 2027

  • Always grab data available to the start of the year, e.g., gather 2026 data when starting a 2027 watershed

  • If a storm happens during the development of the watershed, and it is considered critical by the PDT, it may be included in the analysis.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: Always grab best and most complete data (e.g., gather 2026 data if it is available when kicking off a 2027 study). If data comes online during the project development; it does not need to be included in the project.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: If data is available, it should be included in the project if it does not delay the project.

docs.title: Riverine and Coastal Independence
docs.text: Coastal and inland are currently being kept separate to minimize the possibility of double counting flood risk.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Develop riverine and coastal FFRD models and products independently and maintain separate results datasets that may overlap in some areas

  • Synchronize all input parameters and approaches such that an identical model system is used for both riverine and coastal FFRD studies.

  • Coordinate between riverine and coastal FFRD teams. Ensure collaboration and find methods for data sharing and develop guidance for sharing FFRD data in overlapping areas.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: The riverine and coastal teams, while working autonomously, must coordinate and seek commonality in approaches and consistency in output data.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: Consistent approaches and outputs are needed to effectively communicate flood risk to the stakeholders.

docs.title: Accounting for Urban Drainage
docs.text: The FFRD initiative is tasked with directly assessing pluvial flooding, and the most critical pluvial flooding locations within the United States are overlayed with communities relying on urban drainage infrastructure to reduce flood occurrence.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Allow drainage to occur based only on flow-paths appearing on bare-earth digital elevation models (DEMs)

  • Require explicit modeling of surface water drainage systems and ditches, which may require DEM modifications and two-dimensional (2D) mesh refinements to allow drainage

  • Seek a solution to account for urban drainage with a simplistic method such as translating a threshold rainfall intensity directly to outfall or adjusting outputs with a method outside of the hydraulic model

  • Seek a solution to explicitly model urban storm drain networks including underground pipes and storm-sewer inlets, which would include a data-collection of the drainage network.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text:
The first three pilots are using the draft SOP, which calls for adding an increased level of River Analysis System (HEC-RAS) modeling detail in developed areas having a structure density above a set threshold. The level of detail of modeling in those areas is left to professional judgement of the modeler, but modeling of the urban drainage network is generally limited to surface drainage features.

As of September 2024, the FFRD SOP calls for accounting for all major surface water drainage (ditches, canals, etc.) for developed areas directly in the HEC-RAS model. The software being applied has limited tools available to model subsurface drainage and input data required for urban drainage networks is sparse. It is a recognized weakness in the modeling approach that surface flooding in developed areas may be over-estimated due to lack of urban drainage modeling, and solutions will be sought in future.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: This selected option is the most expedient solution in the short term.

docs.title: Ice Jams
docs.text: Ice jams can cause elevated stages and result in consequences creating risk in some areas of the United States. The FFRD SOP must address ice jambs.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Do nothing

  • Account for ice jams in FFRD modeling.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: As of August 2024, there is no plan for dealing with ice jams.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: Although ice jams are an important aspect of flooding for some areas of the country, the issue has not risen to a critical level for the initial pilot projects, and, therefore, has yet to be addressed.

docs.title: Sequencing of Production with Approvals Prior to Model start
docs.text: There is a need to ensure data collection is complete prior to kicking off a basin study.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Role of person to review data collection (review lead)

  • National Review Board

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: The review lead reviews pre-model data collection and the data collection reporting to ensure projects are fully ready to scope. The review lead is a highly technical team member who understands all portions of the FFRD Basin Modeling. This review occurs as finalization of a review of the data collection and preparation efforts.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: A National Review Board was not selected as this senior oversight group (SOG) will be reviewing each of the basins at a minimum of three checkpoints. This team, comprised of national-level subject matter experts (SMEs) provides technical advice, oversight, and project review at a programmatic level. A detailed review was considered excessive workload given other time constraints expected with a National Level Subject Matter Expert.

docs.title: Sequencing of Production with Approvals Prior to Data Collection and Preparation
docs.text: There is a need to ensure data is available prior to kicking off a basin data collection and preparation process.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• FEMA representative

  • Role of person to review data collection (review lead)

  • National Review Board (SOG)

  • Other?

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: Rationale to support options.

docs.title: Rationale for why the other options were not selected
docs.text: Rationale not to support options

docs.title: Program Personnel
docs.text: Certain Key Members are needed within a project delivery team, and at a programmatic level.

docs.title: Options available
docs.text:
• Within a Team:

  • Technical Team Lead

  • Project Manager

  • Data Preparation Team Lead

  • Geospatial Lead

  • Data Collector and Preparers

  • Levee Data Team Member

  • Dam Data Team Member

  • Hydrologic, Hydraulic, and Reservoir Operations Modelers

  • Meteorologist/SST Specialist

  • Validation and Production Lead

  • Validation and Projection Team Members

  • Consequence Specialist

  • Documentation Lead

  • Cloud Compute Team Member.

  • Programmatic Level:

  • Program Manager

  • Review Lead

  • Reviewers

  • FEMA HQ/Regional Representative (Contracting Representative)

  • Clout Environment Support

  • Expert Model/Software Support Lead

  • Software Maintenance Leads

  • Documentation and SOP Lead

  • Key Stakeholders

  • Senior Oversight Group

  • Steering/Advisory Committee.

docs.title: Selection and rationale
docs.text: All Team Members listed above are considered needed, unless specific item to be modeled is not located within a basin (i.e. no levees, major dams).
